proposals.title: Augmenting for Robustness: Repurposing Adversarial Misspellings for LLM Unlearning and Medical QA
proposals.motivation: Adversarial attacks, such as character-level misspellings, are known to degrade the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs), particularly in high-stakes domains like medical question answering. Concurrently, the field of machine unlearning seeks to efficiently remove specific data or knowledge from models. Both robustness and unlearning are critical for safe, reliable deployment, but current data augmentation methods for these tasks often lack the adversarial realism needed for stress-testing. This work addresses the dual challenge of creating robust models and effectively evaluating unlearning.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically augmenting training and evaluation datasets with adversarially generated misspellings will improve the robustness of LLMs to such perturbations and provide a more sensitive, causal metric for assessing the efficacy of unlearning algorithms in removing specific factual knowledge.
proposals.method: We will adapt adversarial text attack generators (e.g., TextBugger, DeepWordBug) to produce realistic misspellings on targeted subsets of training data (e.g., medical facts from PubMed). For unlearning evaluation, we will create a benchmark where models are trained on a corpus including specific facts, then unlearn them. We will measure unlearning success not just by a drop in base accuracy, but by the causal effect of the misspelling augmentation: a successfully unlearned model should show no performance gap between clean and misspelled queries about the forgotten fact, whereas a model that retains the knowledge will be vulnerable. We will test this on both language and vision-language models adapted for medical tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) A novel data augmentation strategy that repurposes attack methods for robustness training, 2) A new causal evaluation metric for unlearning based on adversarial vulnerability, and 3) Empirical results on the interplay between robustness and unlearning in medical LLMs, extending benchmarks like OpenUnlearning and MedQA.
proposals.novelty: While misspellings are used for attack and simple augmentation, their systematic application as a tool for *evaluating* unlearning via a causal robustness gap is novel. Furthermore, inverting the adversarial objective to serve core model improvement tasks (robustness and unlearning) in medical domains represents a significant shift in perspective.

proposals.title: Adversarial Unlearning: Evaluating and Fortifying LLM Unlearning Against White-Box Attacks
proposals.motivation: Current evaluations of LLM unlearning methods, such as those in OpenUnlearning, often assume a naive threat model where the adversary queries a black-box model. In real-world scenarios, such as removing copyrighted or private medical data, an adversary may have detailed knowledge of the unlearning defense itself, gleaned from model extraction or published methodologies. This stronger threat model is critical for assessing the true robustness of unlearning in security-sensitive applications like medical teaming with LLMs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that under a white-box threat model where the attacker knows the unlearning algorithm and parameters, current state-of-the-art unlearning methods will be significantly more vulnerable to adversarial extraction of 'forgotten' knowledge than previously reported, and that a causal, perturbation-based evaluation framework can both quantify this vulnerability and guide the development of more robust defenses.
proposals.method: We will adapt the OpenUnlearning benchmark to a white-box setting. For each major unlearning method (e.g., gradient ascent, model editing), we will give the adversary full access to the defense model's architecture, unlearning algorithm, and hyperparameters. We will then develop and test adaptive adversarial attacks, such as optimized prompt perturbations and gradient-based input crafting, designed to bypass the specific unlearning mechanism. The core evaluation will use causal metrics from Lipton's prior work to isolate the causal effect of the attack on knowledge recovery, controlling for confounders.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic benchmark for white-box robustness of LLM unlearning, delivering a more realistic security audit for deployment. It will contribute a novel evaluation framework, GenAudit-Adv, extending causal audit tools to adversarial settings, and propose initial defensive strategies, such as randomized unlearning, to increase robustness.
proposals.novelty: While prior work evaluates unlearning efficacy, it largely ignores adaptive adversaries with defense knowledge. This proposal explicitly bridges the gap between the unlearning and adversarial robustness literatures, applying a causal, white-box threat model—common in security—to the nascent problem of machine unlearning for LLMs.

proposals.title: Unlearning Sensitivity: A Causal Framework for Measuring and Improving Robustness in Clinical Text Classifiers
proposals.motivation: Recent work on LLM unlearning and medical adaptation reveals a critical gap: we lack a principled understanding of how a model's sensitivity to specific clinical terms (e.g., 'cancer', 'benign') relates to its robustness against spurious correlations and adversarial perturbations. In high-stakes medical NLP, a model overly sensitive to certain keywords may perform well on i.i.d. data but fail catastrophically when terminology shifts or confounders are present, posing a direct patient safety risk. This problem is underexplored, as existing robustness evaluations often lack a causal, interpretable link to fundamental lexical sensitivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a language model's sensitivity to specific clinical terms, as measured by causal unlearning interventions, is inversely correlated with its robustness to domain shifts and adversarial examples in downstream classification tasks. Furthermore, we posit that strategically 'unlearning' excessive sensitivity to non-causal terms can improve robustness without harming in-distribution accuracy.
proposals.method: We will adapt the evaluation framework from 'OpenUnlearning' and the causal audit methodology from 'GenAudit' to the medical domain. First, we will develop a benchmark using clinical text datasets (e.g., MIMIC-III) to measure sensitivity via the causal effect of ablating specific terms on model predictions. Next, we will correlate these sensitivity metrics with robustness scores against known challenges (e.g., synonym substitution, demographic shift). Finally, we will test if applying targeted unlearning methods (e.g., gradient ascent, model editing) to reduce sensitivity for identified 'brittle' terms leads to quantifiable gains in robustness, using a causal mediation analysis to validate the intervention.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, causal framework for diagnosing robustness vulnerabilities in medical NLP models via sensitivity analysis, 2) a new benchmark linking unlearning metrics to robustness outcomes in a clinical context, and 3) empirical evidence on whether controlled unlearning can serve as a robustness-enhancing regularization technique, advancing the goal of reliable AI for medical teaming applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats unlearning and robustness as separate threads, this proposal novelly posits a causal, measurable relationship between them. It uniquely applies the causal intervention toolkit from model editing and unlearning to the specific problem of medical classifier robustness, moving beyond correlation to test a direct interventional hypothesis.

proposals.title: Robust Unlearning: Defending Language Models Against Multi-Character Adversarial Attacks on Unlearned Concepts
proposals.motivation: Recent work on LLM unlearning, such as Zachary Chase Lipton's OpenUnlearning, focuses on removing specific knowledge or behaviors. However, the robustness of unlearned models against adversarial attacks remains underexplored. In pre-2026 security contexts, single-character adversarial attacks have been shown to bypass safety filters and elicit forbidden knowledge; multi-character attacks present a more potent and realistic threat, potentially reversing unlearning efforts and compromising model safety.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current unlearning methods produce models whose decision boundaries remain vulnerable in high-dimensional text spaces, and that a defense integrating causal perturbation analysis with a learned, multi-scale character encoder can significantly improve robustness against multi-character adversarial attacks on unlearned concepts.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'word recognition defense' framework to the unlearning domain. First, we will generate a benchmark of multi-character adversarial attacks against models unlearned using state-of-the-art methods (e.g., gradient ascent, KL minimization). Our defense will employ a Siamese network with a character-level CNN-BiLSTM encoder to project perturbed and clean inputs into a shared space. We will train this model using a contrastive loss, guided by causal metrics to identify and harden the most vulnerable semantic features associated with the unlearned concept, effectively learning an invariant representation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel benchmark for evaluating the adversarial robustness of unlearning algorithms, 2) a new defense method that extends security from single- to multi-character perturbations, and 3) causal insights into which model features are most destabilized by unlearning, informing more robust algorithm design.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial defenses exist for standard models, applying and adapting them to the unique vulnerability profile of *unlearned* LLMs is novel. The integration of causal analysis with a character-level adversarial detector specifically for the unlearning context moves beyond simple input filtering to a more fundamental hardening of the model's internal representations.

proposals.title: RobustText: Adversarial Spelling Correction via Error-Correcting Code-Inspired Decoding for Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains like medicine, where factual accuracy is critical. However, they remain vulnerable to adversarial character-level perturbations (e.g., 'diabetes' -> 'd1abetes') that can bypass safety filters or cause harmful factual errors, as highlighted by research on model auditing and unlearning. This fragility undermines trust in human-AI teaming scenarios, necessitating robust input sanitization methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that principles from classical error-correcting codes (ECCs), such as Hamming distance decoding and Reed-Solomon parity checks, can be adapted to create a lightweight, model-agnostic pre-processor that detects and corrects adversarial character manipulations in text inputs to LLMs more effectively than standard spell-checkers.
proposals.method: We will develop RobustText, a two-stage framework. First, we design a novel token embedding that maps characters and common typos to a codebook space, imposing a minimum distance between valid words inspired by ECC codewords. Second, we train a small causal transformer as a decoder to map perturbed token sequences to the nearest valid 'codeword' (correct word) in this space, using a loss function that incorporates edit distance and semantic similarity constraints. We will evaluate on adversarial benchmarks created from medical texts (e.g., MIMIC-III notes) and general domain corpora, using metrics for correction accuracy, downstream task performance impact, and inference latency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, interpretable method for adversarial spelling correction grounded in information theory, 2) a unified evaluation benchmark for text robustness focusing on character-level attacks, and 3) a practical tool for hardening LLMs in sensitive applications like medical QA, complementing unlearning and factual error correction research.
proposals.novelty: While ECCs are well-established in communications, their principled application to adversarial text correction for LLMs is novel, moving beyond heuristic spell-checking or robust training. It uniquely bridges classical coding theory with modern NLP security, a direction largely unexplored in pre-2026 LLM robustness literature.

proposals.title: RobustText: Adversarial Training for Discrete Text via Gradient-Guided Token Perturbations
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) deployed in high-stakes domains like medicine are brittle to simple input perturbations, such as typos or misspellings, which can degrade performance and erode trust. Existing adversarial training methods from computer vision (e.g., PGD) are not directly applicable to discrete text tokens, leaving a critical gap in robust model development. This work addresses the need for a principled, gradient-based method to improve LLM robustness against realistic, character-level adversarial attacks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting the iterative, gradient-based adversarial attack framework of Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) to operate on continuous token embeddings and mapping perturbations back to the discrete vocabulary via a nearest-neighbor projection, we can generate effective adversarial text examples for training, thereby significantly improving model robustness against misspellings.
proposals.method: We will adapt the PGD algorithm to the text domain by treating the input as a sequence of token embeddings. At each iteration, we compute the gradient of the loss with respect to these embeddings, apply a small perturbation in the embedding space, and then 'project' the perturbed embedding back to the nearest valid token in the vocabulary using a k-nearest-neighbors search over the model's embedding matrix. This generates adversarial text examples (e.g., 'hosptial' for 'hospital') which will be used in an adversarial training loop, mixing them with clean data to fine-tune a base LLM. We will evaluate robustness using established metrics on benchmarks like AdvGLUE and a new medical QA typo benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This project will deliver a novel adversarial training algorithm specifically designed for discrete text, a benchmark for evaluating robustness to misspellings in medical QA, and empirical evidence on the efficacy of this method for improving model reliability. It directly contributes to the themes of robust LLM deployment and medical adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training is standard in vision, its direct application to discrete text via gradient-based token-space perturbations is non-trivial and under-explored. Our core novelty is the formalization of a PGD-like 'projection' step for text, moving beyond simple synonym substitution or random character noise to use model gradients to find the most damaging yet plausible typos.

proposals.title: Phonetic Unlearning: Mitigating Homophonic Hallucinations in Large Language Models via Soundex-Enhanced Recognition
proposals.motivation: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains like medicine, where factual accuracy is critical. A persistent failure mode is the generation of plausible-sounding but incorrect information, often exacerbated by homophonic misspellings or phonetic confusions in user queries or source data. Pre-2026 benchmarks for LLM unlearning and factual correction, such as those in OpenUnlearning and GenAudit, primarily evaluate semantic or token-level errors, neglecting this phonetic dimension of model vulnerability.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating phonetic matching algorithms, such as Soundex and Metaphone, into the recognition layer of LLM unlearning or correction frameworks will significantly improve their robustness to homophonic misspellings, leading to more accurate identification and mitigation of factually erroneous outputs.
proposals.method: We will extend the evaluation framework from OpenUnlearning to create a new benchmark dataset of homophonic misspellings for medical and factual entities. We will then modify the error detection mechanism in a base unlearning method (e.g., gradient ascent) or a correction method like GenAudit by incorporating a phonetic similarity score (using Soundex and Double Metaphone) alongside traditional token embeddings. This hybrid distance metric will be used to identify when a model's output or an internal representation is phonetically similar to, but semantically divergent from, a target fact to be unlearned or corrected.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A novel benchmark for evaluating LLM robustness to phonetic confusion, filling a gap in current unlearning and auditing metrics. 2) A lightweight, interpretable method to enhance existing unlearning and correction algorithms, particularly for applications in noisy-text domains like clinical notes or user-generated content. 3) New insights into the interaction between acoustic-form representations and factual knowledge in LLMs.
proposals.novelty: While phonetic algorithms are classic in NLP, their integration into modern LLM unlearning and factual correction pipelines is unexplored. This approach moves beyond purely semantic or token-based evaluation, addressing a concrete, underexamined pathway for model error that aligns with Lipton's focus on rigorous, causal-style evaluation of model failures.

proposals.title: Aligning for Robustness: Character-Level Adversarial Correction in LLMs via Sequence Alignment
proposals.motivation: Character-level adversarial edits (e.g., typos, homoglyphs) are a persistent, low-cost vulnerability for LLMs, especially in high-stakes domains like medical QA where factual integrity is critical. Current defenses, often based on token-level detection or retraining, are brittle and computationally expensive. This work addresses the need for a lightweight, interpretable method to model and correct these perturbations directly, enhancing model robustness without extensive retraining.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that character-level adversarial edits can be effectively modeled as a sequence alignment problem, and that applying dynamic programming algorithms like Needleman-Wunsch or Smith-Waterman will enable precise, efficient correction of these edits, thereby restoring the factual accuracy of LLM outputs.
proposals.method: We will treat the original, clean text and the adversarially perturbed input as two sequences for alignment. Using a custom scoring matrix that penalizes suspicious character substitutions (e.g., 'l' to '1') and rewards matches, we will apply the Needleman-Wunsch algorithm for global alignment to reconstruct the intended prompt. This corrected sequence will be fed to the LLM. We will evaluate the method on benchmarks like AdvGLUE and medical QA datasets, comparing output fidelity before and after correction against baselines like spell-checkers and adversarial training.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, computationally efficient framework for character-level adversarial defense, grounded in bioinformatics. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating LLM robustness to subtle perturbations and deliver a practical tool for 'fixing' inputs in real-time, complementing existing unlearning and auditing pipelines for safer model deployment.
proposals.novelty: While sequence alignment is foundational in bioinformatics, its application to model and correct character-level adversarial attacks on LLMs is novel. This approach moves beyond token-based or gradient-based defenses, offering a discrete, interpretable, and alignment-based perspective on robustness.

proposals.title: EdgeMedUnlearn: Efficient Unlearning for Medical LLMs on Resource-Constrained Devices
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) for medical applications on edge devices (e.g., point-of-care diagnostics, wearable health monitors) is limited by computational constraints and stringent data privacy regulations like HIPAA. Current unlearning methods, which aim to remove specific data or knowledge from models, are computationally intensive and designed for cloud-scale models, making them impractical for on-device use where patient data must be processed locally and securely.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a causal, task-specific intervention on a heavily compressed medical LLM can achieve effective, verifiable unlearning of sensitive patient concepts with minimal performance degradation on core medical tasks, enabling privacy-preserving deployment on edge hardware.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained, medically-tuned small language model (e.g., a distilled version of BioBERT or PubMedBERT) using quantization and pruning for edge deployment. We will then develop a lightweight unlearning algorithm based on gradient ascent and causal mediation analysis, targeting specific factual associations (e.g., patient identifiers) within the model's layers. Efficacy will be evaluated using a unified benchmark adapted from OpenUnlearning, with metrics for forget quality, model utility on medical QA, and inference latency on target edge devices.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, efficient unlearning framework tailored for compressed models on edge devices, 2) a benchmark for evaluating privacy-utility trade-offs in resource-constrained medical AI, and 3) open-source code for a deployable 'edge-unlearning' module, advancing the field of trustworthy AI in clinical settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored unlearning for large models and medical adaptation separately, this proposal uniquely integrates causal unlearning with aggressive model compression specifically for the edge computing domain, addressing the critical intersection of efficiency, medical task performance, and regulatory-compliant data removal.

proposals.title: Adversarial Minimalism: A Causal Framework for Character-Level Attack Efficiency and Defense Failure in Robust Word Recognition
proposals.motivation: Robust word recognizers, designed to filter harmful content, are critical for safe LLM deployment in sensitive domains like medical teaming. However, their vulnerability to adversarial character-level edits (e.g., 'bad' -> 'b@d') remains a significant security flaw. Quantifying the precise conditions for defense failure is essential for moving beyond heuristic robustness and building truly reliable safety systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a minimal set of character edits, defined by a causal graph of visual and semantic similarity, can reliably induce failure in robust word recognizers, and that this minimal perturbation set is significantly smaller than those used in prior adversarial text attacks.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal framework to model the effect of character edits (substitutions, insertions, deletions) on recognizer failure, treating the recognizer as a black-box system. Using a search algorithm based on Bayesian Optimization, we will efficiently explore the discrete edit space to find minimal adversarial examples. We will evaluate attack success and edit distance on a benchmark of safety-critical terms, drawing from medical and toxic language datasets, against state-of-the-art recognizers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, causal understanding of the failure modes of text-based safety filters, yielding a novel benchmark for evaluating adversarial robustness. It will contribute a methodology for 'stress-testing' safety systems that aligns with Lipton's focus on unlearning evaluation and factual error correction in model outputs.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on adversarial attacks, which often seeks any successful perturbation, this study focuses on causal, *minimal* character edits as a precise measure of defense fragility, framing the problem through the lens of efficient unlearning and audit (GenAudit) of a model's robustness claims.

proposals.title: Unlearning by Distillation: Architecture-Agnostic Knowledge Transfer for Efficient Model Adaptation
proposals.motivation: The need to adapt or 'unlearn' specific knowledge from large language models (LLMs) is critical for safety, privacy, and domain-specific deployment, as highlighted by recent unlearning benchmarks. However, effective unlearning often requires retraining or distilling into a new, efficient model, a process hampered by architectural mismatch between large teacher and compact student networks. Current distillation methods struggle when representations are structurally misaligned, limiting reliable knowledge transfer for creating specialized, efficient models in domains like medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a lightweight, trainable projection module that adaptively maps a teacher model's internal representations into a form compatible with a student's architectural constraints will enable more robust and semantically faithful knowledge distillation, even between radically different network architectures.
proposals.method: We will design a projection module, likely based on a small multi-layer perceptron or attention-based adapter, inserted between the teacher's intermediate layers (e.g., final transformer block outputs) and the student's input space. This module will be trained jointly with a multi-objective loss combining standard distillation loss (KL divergence on outputs) and a novel representational alignment loss (e.g., centered kernel alignment) to ensure functional translation of knowledge. We will evaluate the method using established unlearning benchmarks (e.g., OpenUnlearning framework) and medical adaptation tasks, comparing against baseline distillation and unlearning techniques on metrics of efficacy, efficiency, and retained general capability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical method for transferring refined or 'unlearned' knowledge from large foundation models to efficient, heterogeneous student architectures, directly aiding model specialization and deployment. It will contribute a new evaluation perspective to the unlearning literature by framing efficient adaptation as a robust distillation problem, bridging the fields of model compression, unlearning, and domain adaptation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior distillation work that assumes architectural similarity or uses fixed projection strategies, our method learns an optimal, adaptive mapping to overcome representational mismatch, explicitly targeting the challenge of transferring 'corrected' or selectively filtered knowledge from unlearning procedures.

proposals.title: Refactoring Knowledge: Architecture-Agnostic Distillation via Causal Constraint Transfer
proposals.motivation: Current knowledge distillation and unlearning methods are constrained by architectural similarity between teacher and student models, limiting deployment of large models (e.g., LLMs) on diverse, efficient hardware like neuromorphic chips. This creates a bottleneck for specialized applications in medical AI and human-AI teaming, where model efficiency and adaptability are critical. We need a paradigm that decouples functional knowledge from its architectural implementation.
proposals.hypothesis: The core knowledge of a neural network can be represented as a set of architecture-agnostic, causal constraints on its input-output behavior. A fundamentally different student architecture can be trained to satisfy these constraints, achieving functional equivalence without structural mimicry.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will extract a causal graph or a set of invariant prediction rules from a pre-trained transformer teacher (e.g., a medical LLM) using causal abstraction or influence function analysis, creating an intermediate 'constraint specification'. Second, we will train a structurally dissimilar student (e.g., a spiking neural network or a small MLP) using a novel loss that penalizes violations of these constraints, rather than mimicking logits or features. We will evaluate on benchmarks from OpenUnlearning and medical QA, measuring functional fidelity, efficiency gains, and robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodology for cross-architectural knowledge transfer, enabling the deployment of complex model capabilities on hardware-optimized substrates. It will contribute a novel evaluation framework for functional equivalence beyond standard metrics, advancing the fields of efficient AI and model unlearning where architectural change is required.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard distillation which aligns internal representations, our method translates knowledge into an intermediate constraint language inspired by biological pathway refactoring. This breaks the direct architectural coupling assumed by prior work, enabling transfer between radically different computational substrates.

proposals.title: The Minimalist's Paradox: Does Architectural Mismatch and Predictor Weakness Improve Black-Box Shift Estimation?
proposals.motivation: Black-Box Shift Estimation (BBSE) is crucial for detecting dataset drift in deployed ML systems, especially in high-stakes domains like medical AI where models can fail silently. Current BBSE methods often assume the predictor is a good proxy for the true label distribution, but the impact of the predictor's own architecture and capacity on estimation robustness is poorly understood. This gap is critical as practitioners increasingly apply BBSE to complex, black-box foundation models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using a deliberately weak and architecturally mismatched student model for BBSE can, paradoxically, lead to more robust or computationally efficient shift estimation by reducing overfitting to the teacher's specific output structure, but that this benefit has clear failure boundaries defined by student capacity and architectural distance.
proposals.method: We will fix a complex teacher model (e.g., a large vision-language model) and systematically vary student architectures (e.g., linear models, shallow random forests, tiny MLPs) trained solely on the teacher's soft labels. Using controlled covariate shift datasets (e.g., perturbed medical imaging data), we will measure estimation error of the true label distribution shift. We will employ causal graph analysis to disentangle the effects of architectural family from model capacity and use evaluation metrics from recent unlearning benchmarks to assess robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of the 'student' in BBSE, yielding practical guidelines for choosing shift detectors for black-box models. It will contribute a new diagnostic framework for BBSE failure modes, advancing the field beyond treating the predictor as a black box within a black box.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on BBSE algorithm derivation, our study uniquely isolates and probes the role of the predictor model itself, challenging the implicit assumption that a better proxy predictor always yields better shift estimation.

proposals.title: Architectural Mismatch in Machine Unlearning: Efficient Student-Teacher Knowledge Transfer for LLM Forgetting
proposals.motivation: Current machine unlearning methods for LLMs, such as gradient ascent or fine-tuning on curated data, often assume the unlearned model (student) must retain the same architecture as the original model (teacher). This is computationally inefficient and limits deployment in resource-constrained settings like medical applications or on-device AI. A method enabling effective unlearning into a smaller, more efficient student model would dramatically improve the practicality of unlearning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a smaller, more efficient student model (e.g., a distilled or sparse architecture) can be trained to mimic the post-unlearning behavior of a large teacher model, achieving comparable unlearning efficacy while improving inference efficiency and retaining general capability on non-forgotten data.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend knowledge distillation and unlearning techniques. First, we will apply a state-of-the-art unlearning method (e.g., gradient ascent on forget data) to a large teacher LLM. We will then use this unlearned teacher to generate responses on a retained dataset, creating a new training target. A smaller student model (e.g., a pruned version of the teacher or a different efficient architecture like a mixture-of-experts) will be trained via standard distillation loss to match these targets, while also using causal metrics to ensure the forget set information is not transferred. We will evaluate using the OpenUnlearning benchmark for unlearning efficacy and standard NLP benchmarks for retained performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for parameter-efficient and architecturally flexible machine unlearning. It will deliver concrete metrics on the trade-offs between unlearning quality, model efficiency, and task performance. The resulting methods will be particularly valuable for domains like medical AI, where model updates for compliance (e.g., removing faulty knowledge) must be deployed efficiently.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge distillation is well-studied, its application to the specific objective of machine unlearning, where the goal is to *not* learn specific information, is unexplored. This challenges the implicit assumption that the unlearned model must be architecturally identical to the original, opening a new direction for efficient model lifecycle management.

proposals.title: Distillation from Deliberately Degraded Oracles: A Controlled Study of Knowledge Transfer in the Presence of Teacher Impairment
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation is a cornerstone of model compression and transfer learning, yet its mechanisms are often conflated with dataset effects. In high-stakes domains like medicine, where models may need to be unlearned or adapted from imperfect sources, it is critical to isolate the pure distillation signal. This work aims to rigorously quantify when and how knowledge transfer occurs even from a teacher that is objectively worse than an oracle, providing foundational insights for robust and safe model deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a student model trained via distillation from a deliberately impaired teacher can outperform an identically-capacity model trained directly on hard labels, but only for specific types and severities of teacher impairment that preserve relative class relationships or useful inductive biases.
proposals.method: We will use a strong pre-trained LLM (e.g., Llama-2 or GPT-3.5) as the oracle. We will create impaired 'weak teachers' by applying controlled perturbations: causal interventions (e.g., masking specific factual knowledge via unlearning methods), architectural degradation (e.g., pruning attention heads), and noise injection into logits. We will then distill from these teachers into smaller student models (e.g., distilled BERT or small Llama variants) on benchmark datasets (e.g., MedQA for medical reasoning, OpenUnlearning benchmarks for factual recall). The core comparison is between students trained on the teacher's soft labels and baseline students trained solely on the original dataset's hard labels, controlling for all other factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic map of the distillation benefit landscape under controlled teacher impairment, separating the signal of knowledge transfer from simple data learning. It will yield practical guidelines for when distillation is beneficial in scenarios like model adaptation, unlearning, and learning from noisy experts. The resulting metrics and causal framework will directly inform safer and more reliable LLM teaming and medical adaptation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that compares teachers of different inherent capacities, we deliberately degrade a single oracle to create a controlled ablation study of distillation. This isolates the distillation mechanism itself, moving beyond correlation to causal analysis of what aspects of a teacher's output constitute 'transferable knowledge'.

proposals.title: Catalytic Learning: Improving Large Model Generalization via Weak Teacher Scaffolding
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are prone to overfitting to spurious correlations and memorizing training data, which can lead to poor generalization and factual errors. While knowledge distillation from powerful teachers is common, it can propagate teacher biases and limit student discovery. This work investigates a novel, resource-efficient paradigm inspired by heterogeneous catalysis to guide large model optimization towards more robust solutions.
proposals.hypothesis: A weak, highly regularized teacher model can act as a selective 'promoter' or scaffold, constraining the initial optimization trajectory of a much larger student model to avoid poor local minima and discover more generalizable features, ultimately leading to superior final performance compared to training the strong student from scratch or with a powerful teacher.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'unlearning' evaluation framework from OpenUnlearning to create controlled datasets with spurious features. We will design a weak teacher (e.g., a heavily quantized or dropout-intensive small network) and a strong student (a standard LLM). The training protocol will interleave phases: the student's loss is a weighted sum of its own task loss and a divergence loss from the weak teacher's early-layer representations, with this scaffolding weight annealed to zero. We will rigorously evaluate using causal mediation analysis (from GenAudit) to trace the teacher's influence and benchmark against baselines on medical QA tasks (inspired by Medical Adaptation papers) to measure robustness and factual accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, theoretically grounded method for improving LLM training efficiency and generalization. It will contribute a formal framework for 'catalytic learning' and deliver empirical evidence on when weak guidance outperforms strong distillation. The findings could inform more effective and computationally cheaper strategies for adapting large models to sensitive domains like medicine.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard teacher-student performance hierarchy, proposing that a deliberately weak model can be a superior guide. It moves beyond knowledge transfer to conceptualize the teacher as a transient optimization constraint, a novel analogy drawn from catalysis not previously explored in ML.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Test-Time Adaptation via Inverted Distillation: Joint Estimation of Source Noise and Label Shift
proposals.motivation: Real-world deployment of machine learning models, especially in high-stakes domains like medicine, is plagued by distribution shifts. A critical but understudied scenario is when both the source training data has unknown label noise/corruption and the target test distribution exhibits an unknown label shift. Pre-2026 domain adaptation and test-time adaptation methods often assume a clean source or known shift, limiting their robustness in practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the distillation loss and learning dynamics of a strong student model, trained via knowledge distillation from a weak teacher trained on the uncertain source data, encode a reliable signal to jointly disentangle and estimate the true source label noise and the test-time label shift in an unsupervised manner.
proposals.method: We will formalize a novel 'inverted distillation' framework. A weak model (e.g., a small transformer) is first trained on the source data with its unknown p(y). This teacher then guides a strong student (e.g., a larger LLM) on unlabeled target data via standard KD loss. We will develop a bi-level optimization procedure that treats the source label corruption (as a confusion matrix) and the target label shift (as a re-weighting vector) as latent variables. The student's adaptation trajectory and the distillation loss landscape will be used to derive a causal-inspired estimation algorithm, building on pre-2026 methods like Black Box Shift Estimation but extended to the dual-unknown setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel unsupervised adaptation algorithm for the challenging dual-unknown label distribution scenario, 2) a new perspective on knowledge distillation as a tool for domain discovery rather than just model compression, and 3) a rigorous evaluation benchmark, extending ideas from OpenUnlearning, to measure performance under compounded source and target distribution shifts.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is inverting the traditional distillation setup—using a weak, uncertain teacher to guide a strong student—and repurposing this process not for model compression, but as a joint estimation mechanism for two fundamental types of label distribution uncertainty that are typically studied in isolation.

proposals.title: Inverted Distillation: Investigating Knowledge Transfer from Weak Teachers to Strong Students
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation is a cornerstone of model compression and transfer learning, but its standard formulation assumes a stronger teacher guides a weaker student. This paradigm overlooks realistic scenarios like continual learning, where a new, powerful model must integrate knowledge from legacy systems, or collaborative settings where diverse, specialized but weaker models exist. Understanding if and how weak teachers can improve strong students is crucial for efficient, incremental learning in teams of models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a strong student model can extract useful, complementary knowledge from a weak teacher via distillation, leading to performance gains on specific tasks, particularly those requiring robustness or calibration, even when the teacher's overall accuracy is lower.
proposals.method: We will design controlled experiments using pre-trained LLMs (e.g., LLaMA-2-7B as the 'weak' teacher, LLaMA-2-70B as the 'strong' student). We will apply standard distillation loss (KL divergence on logits) and feature-based distillation on intermediate layers. To isolate the effect, we will benchmark on medical QA datasets (e.g., MedQA) and causal reasoning tasks, using the evaluation frameworks from OpenUnlearning and GenAudit to measure not just accuracy but also factual consistency, calibration, and robustness to spurious correlations. We will compare against the strong student's baseline and a reversed, standard distillation setup.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence and mechanistic analysis of inverted distillation, challenging the conventional wisdom of the teacher-student hierarchy. It will offer practical guidelines for model 'teaming' and legacy knowledge integration. The findings could also inform new unlearning strategies, where a weak 'anti-teacher' guides a strong model to forget specific concepts.
proposals.novelty: While prior work assumes teacher superiority, we explicitly invert this relationship to test the limits and mechanisms of knowledge transfer. This directly connects distillation theory to practical 'teaming' and incremental learning problems that lack a single strong oracle.

proposals.title: Consistency-Regularized Unlearning: Robust Knowledge Distillation for LLM Forgetting and Correction
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often contain outdated, incorrect, or sensitive information. Current unlearning and correction methods, such as fine-tuning on negative examples or gradient ascent, can be unstable and degrade general performance. Label-free knowledge distillation, where a student model learns from a teacher's outputs, offers a promising alternative but is vulnerable to inheriting and amplifying the teacher's errors and miscalibrated confidences.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting standard label-free distillation with a strong consistency regularization objective will produce a more robust student model that resists overfitting to teacher errors, leading to more effective and stable unlearning and factual correction in LLMs.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Consistency-Regularized Label-Free Knowledge Distillation framework for LLM unlearning. The 'teacher' will be the original model containing the information to be removed. The student is trained with a combined loss: (1) a standard KL divergence loss on the teacher's softened output distributions for a given prompt, and (2) a consistency loss (e.g., Jensen-Shannon divergence) between the student's outputs for multiple semantically equivalent or adversarially perturbed versions of the same prompt. We will integrate this with causal tracing methods to identify and prioritize unlearning of specific factual associations, and evaluate using unified benchmarks from OpenUnlearning and factual error metrics from GenAudit.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more stable and robust method for LLM unlearning and correction that mitigates catastrophic forgetting and performance collapse. It will advance the field by introducing a self-supervised, regularization-based perspective to model editing, and deliver a practical toolkit evaluated on established medical and general-domain benchmarks for model adaptation and audit.
proposals.novelty: While consistency regularization is known in semi-supervised learning, its application as a core stabilizer for label-free distillation in the context of LLM unlearning and correction is novel. This approach directly addresses the core vulnerability of distillation—overfitting to a flawed teacher—which is critical for safety-critical applications like medical AI.

proposals.title: Self-Consistent Knowledge Distillation: A Bootstrapping Framework for Label-Free Model Compression
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation (KD) is a cornerstone for deploying large language models (LLMs) in resource-constrained environments, such as clinical settings where data privacy is paramount. However, standard KD relies on labeled data or the teacher's soft labels as a static target, which can propagate and even amplify the teacher's biases or factual errors. A distillation method that can self-correct and validate its consistency using only the teacher's outputs would be valuable for creating more robust, efficient models, especially in sensitive domains like medicine where external validation data is scarce.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a bootstrapping distillation loss, inspired by ecological mark-recapture estimation, can train a student model to match a teacher's predictive distribution with high fidelity using only the teacher's own soft predictions, without access to ground-truth labels, and that this process can improve the student's consistency and reduce its adoption of the teacher's systematic errors.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Lincoln-Petersen mark-recapture estimator to KD. First, the teacher model 'marks' a large unlabeled dataset by generating softmax distributions (soft labels). This dataset is randomly split into overlapping 'marking' and 'recapture' subsets. The student is initially trained on the marking set with a standard KD loss. Then, a novel consistency loss is computed by having the student make predictions on the recapture set and measuring the divergence (e.g., KL-divergence) between these predictions and the teacher's original marks on the same set. The final objective combines the initial distillation loss and the recapture consistency loss, creating a self-supervised, closed-loop training regime.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, label-free knowledge distillation framework that reduces dependency on potentially noisy or unavailable ground truth, 2) a rigorous evaluation protocol, inspired by causal inference and benchmarking methods from unlearning research, to measure the fidelity and error propagation between teacher and student, and 3) empirical validation in domains like medical QA, demonstrating practical utility for model compression where data is sensitive.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the reformulation of knowledge distillation as a self-consistent estimation problem, directly translating a non-machine learning statistical method (ecological mark-recapture) to create a bootstrapping training loop that uses the teacher as its own validation mechanism, moving beyond static soft-label matching.

proposals.title: Architecture-Agnostic Knowledge Distillation: A Pure Teacher Supervision Framework for Analyzing Invariant Knowledge
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation (KD) is a cornerstone of model compression and transfer, but its standard formulation conflates the teacher's learned knowledge with the inductive biases of its architecture. This entanglement makes it difficult to understand what constitutes architecture-invariant knowledge versus architecture-specific representation. As the field increasingly deploys diverse model families (e.g., Transformers for LLMs, ResNets for vision) and studies their unlearning and adaptation, a clean framework to isolate and analyze pure knowledge transfer is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by removing the standard cross-entropy loss with ground-truth labels and forcing distillation to rely solely on the teacher's soft labels, we can create a testbed that reveals which learned features and decision boundaries are fundamentally architecture-agnostic, and which are artifacts of a specific model's structural priors.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled distillation suite where a high-performing teacher model (e.g., a Vision Transformer) distills knowledge into architecturally distinct students (e.g., ResNet, DenseNet, MLP-Mixer) using only a Kullback-Leibler divergence loss between their output distributions. We will systematically vary teacher-student architecture pairs across vision and language tasks (using established pre-2026 benchmarks like ImageNet and GLUE). Performance will be evaluated against standard KD baselines, and we will employ causal mediation analysis and representation similarity metrics to trace which teacher activations and layers are most critical for successful cross-architectural transfer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A novel, controlled framework for studying knowledge disentanglement from architectural bias, 2) Empirical insights into the architectural compatibility of learned representations, informing future model compression and unlearning strategies, and 3) A new evaluation paradigm for 'pure' knowledge transfer that complements existing benchmarks like OpenUnlearning.
proposals.novelty: While KD is well-studied, prior work consistently uses ground-truth labels as an auxiliary signal. Our strict removal of this signal creates a uniquely constrained setting to isolate the teacher's inductive bias, offering a novel lens on knowledge that is invariant to radical architectural shifts.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Knowledge Distillation: Training Student Models Solely on Teacher Output Distributions
proposals.motivation: Standard knowledge distillation relies on a cross-entropy loss with ground-truth labels, which can be unavailable, unreliable, or privacy-sensitive in domains like medicine. This dependence limits distillation's applicability for unlearning specific data, adapting models to new domains without labeled data, or leveraging outputs from black-box teacher APIs. A method to distill knowledge using only the teacher's output distribution would significantly broaden the utility of distillation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a student model can be effectively trained to match a teacher's capabilities by minimizing a divergence metric between their output distributions over a large, unlabeled corpus, without any access to ground-truth labels, and that this process can be regularized to prevent degenerate solutions.
proposals.method: We will formulate distillation as minimizing the forward KL divergence between the teacher's predicted label distribution and the student's distribution over the same inputs. To prevent mode collapse and encourage confident predictions, we will incorporate a regularization term based on the entropy of the student's outputs. We will evaluate this approach by distilling large pre-trained LLMs (e.g., Llama-2) into smaller models on benchmark tasks (e.g., medical QA from MMLU) and compare performance against standard supervised distillation and baseline self-training methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and empirical validation for label-free knowledge distillation, expanding its use in data-scarce and privacy-conscious scenarios. It will contribute a new evaluation benchmark for unlearning and adaptation methods where true labels are intentionally withheld, directly extending themes from OpenUnlearning and Medical Adaptation studies.
proposals.novelty: While self-training uses pseudo-labels, our method entirely forgoes a categorical 'label,' instead matching the full teacher distribution, a non-trivial shift from standard distillation. This approach is distinct from prior work on unlearning or correction (e.g., GenAudit) which often assumes access to a verifiable ground truth.

proposals.title: Stabilizing Knowledge Distillation via Historical Ensemble Consistency for Robust LLM Unlearning
proposals.motivation: Current methods for unlearning in Large Language Models (LLMs) often rely on knowledge distillation from a static teacher model, which can limit adaptation and lead to suboptimal student performance. This is particularly critical in sensitive domains like medicine, where models must forget outdated or incorrect information without degrading their overall factual integrity. A dynamic teacher that evolves with the student could improve guidance, but risks instability and catastrophic forgetting of retained knowledge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamically updated teacher model, constrained by consistency regularization against an ensemble of its own historical snapshots, will provide a more effective and stable training signal for LLM unlearning, leading to superior trade-offs between forgetting target data and preserving general model utility compared to static teacher approaches.
proposals.method: We adapt the student-teacher framework for unlearning, where the student is trained to diverge from the teacher on 'forget' data. The teacher is periodically fine-tuned on a weighted mixture of the original 'retain' dataset and the student's high-confidence predictions. Each teacher update is constrained by a novel Historical Ensemble Consistency (HEC) loss, computed as the KL-divergence between the new teacher's outputs and a fixed, exponentially-weighted ensemble of all past teacher checkpoints. We will evaluate on established unlearning benchmarks (e.g., TOFU) using causal metrics for forgetting efficacy and base model performance preservation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, stability-constrained dynamic teacher algorithm for LLM unlearning, providing a new method to improve the fidelity of the unlearning process. We will deliver a comprehensive empirical evaluation in both general and medical adaptation contexts, analyzing the trade-off between teacher adaptability and consistency. The proposed HEC loss offers a generalizable technique for stabilizing iterative model updates beyond unlearning.
proposals.novelty: While ensemble methods and consistency regularization exist, applying a *historical* ensemble of the *teacher model itself* as a stability constraint during iterative co-evolution with a student is novel for unlearning. This contrasts with prior work using static teachers or ensembles of students, directly addressing the tension between teacher adaptation and training signal stability.

proposals.title: Red Queen Distillation: Co-Evolving Teacher-Student Dynamics for Efficient LLM Unlearning and Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Current knowledge distillation and unlearning methods for LLMs treat the teacher model as a static source of knowledge, which can lead to inefficient training and failure to target a student's persistent weaknesses. Inspired by Zachary Chase Lipton's work on unlearning benchmarks and medical adaptation, we posit that a dynamic, responsive teacher could significantly improve sample efficiency and final performance, especially in high-stakes domains like medicine where models must rapidly adapt and unlearn outdated or incorrect information.
proposals.hypothesis: A distillation framework where the teacher's behavior (e.g., its attention to specific data points or its output-temperature schedule) is dynamically adapted based on real-time student error metrics will lead to faster convergence and superior final performance compared to static distillation, particularly for tasks requiring unlearning and domain adaptation.
proposals.method: We will develop a 'Curriculum Co-evolution' loop where a student LLM (e.g., a compact clinical model) is trained via distillation from a larger teacher LLM. The teacher's distillation strategy will be governed by a lightweight policy network. This policy will take as input a vector of student performance metrics (e.g., per-class loss, gradient norms, confusion matrix entries) and output a control signal—such as a data sampling distribution, a temperature parameter for label softening, or a weighting for adversarial examples. We will train this policy using reinforcement learning (e.g., a policy gradient method) with the student's validation performance as the reward, creating a closed-loop system. Evaluation will use benchmarks from OpenUnlearning and medical adaptation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide 1) a novel, biologically-inspired framework for adaptive knowledge transfer, 2) a set of concrete algorithms for dynamic teacher-student co-adaptation, and 3) empirical evidence on the benefits of such feedback for LLM unlearning and medical domain adaptation, contributing to more efficient and robust model training pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning and self-paced learning adjust the data presented to the student, this work uniquely proposes to dynamically adapt the *teacher's behavior* itself based on the student's state, framing the interaction as a co-evolutionary game rather than a one-way knowledge transfer.

proposals.title: Bidirectional Co-Evolution for Architecture-Agnostic Knowledge Transfer
proposals.motivation: Standard knowledge distillation relies on a static, monolithic teacher, which is inefficient for transferring knowledge to structurally different or heavily pruned student models. This is particularly limiting in domains like medicine, where deploying specialized, efficient models is crucial but architectural constraints vary. A dynamic, co-adaptive teaching paradigm could unlock new levels of efficiency and specialization.
proposals.hypothesis: A teacher model that adapts its own representations based on the evolving architectural and learning characteristics of a student will enable more effective distillation, leading to student models that are both more performant and more parameter-efficient than those trained via static distillation.
proposals.method: We will implement a framework with two interacting loops. First, a causal inference module will analyze the student's error patterns to identify architectural bottlenecks. Second, based on this analysis, we will adapt the teacher's output distributions (e.g., via attention head masking or gradient-based unlearning of teacher features irrelevant to the student) using a metric-driven objective. The student's architecture will be iteratively pruned using movement pruning, guided by the teacher's adapted knowledge stream.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for adaptive model compression and specialization, moving beyond static distillation. We will deliver a unified benchmark for evaluating adaptive teaching methods and demonstrate its utility by creating highly efficient, specialized models for a challenging domain like medical QA.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior distillation or unlearning work, the teacher is not a fixed source of truth but an adaptive agent that learns to teach a specific, evolving student architecture, creating a true bidirectional feedback loop.

proposals.title: Dynamic Knowledge Transfer: A Causal Framework for Adaptive Teacher-Student Learning in LLM Unlearning
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation and unlearning for LLMs typically rely on a static teacher model, which can propagate the teacher's biases and errors into the student. This is particularly problematic in safety-critical domains like medicine, where factual accuracy is paramount. A fixed teacher also cannot adapt to the student's evolving knowledge state during unlearning, potentially hindering efficiency and final model quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically updating the teacher model during the student's unlearning process, guided by causal metrics of factual retention and error propagation, will yield a student model with superior performance on target tasks (e.g., factual accuracy) and improved unlearning metrics compared to static-teacher distillation.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal evaluation framework to measure the teacher's influence on student error trajectories. Using this, we will design an adaptive teacher update rule, where the teacher is periodically fine-tuned or ensembled from intermediate student checkpoints that demonstrate high fidelity on a retained 'gold' dataset and successful unlearning on a 'forget' set. We will benchmark this method against static-teacher baselines on the OpenUnlearning benchmark, focusing on medical QA tasks to assess factual correctness and teaming performance (e.g., GenAudit-style error correction).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, adaptive algorithm for knowledge transfer in LLM unlearning, 2) a causal analysis framework for diagnosing teacher-student interaction during training, and 3) empirical results demonstrating improved medical adaptation and factual error reduction in unlearned models, advancing towards more reliable AI teaming.
proposals.novelty: While teacher-student distillation is well-established, the concept of a dynamically evolving teacher informed by causal metrics of the unlearning process itself is novel. This moves beyond treating the teacher as a fixed oracle and instead frames it as an adaptive component within a coupled learning system.

proposals.title: The Devil's Advocate in the Machine: Adversarial Ensemble Distillation for Robust and Calibrated Model Compression
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation from ensembles is a standard technique for model compression, but it risks distilling the ensemble's collective biases and overconfidence into a single, brittle model. This is particularly critical in high-stakes domains like medicine, where models must be robust to spurious correlations and provide well-calibrated uncertainty estimates. Pre-2026 work on LLM unlearning and auditing (e.g., GenAudit) highlights the difficulty of identifying and correcting systematic errors in large models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a student model to simultaneously match an ensemble's predictions and resist targeted adversarial challenges from a 'Devil's Advocate' network will yield a single model that is more robust, better calibrated, and less prone to inherited ensemble biases than standard distillation.
proposals.method: We will train a 'Devil's Advocate' network (a conditional generator or a small LLM) to analyze the teacher ensemble's predictions on a batch and generate challenging outputs: hard counterexamples via gradient-based perturbation or textual contradiction, and alternative label distributions that maximize student disagreement. The student model is then trained with a dual loss: a standard distillation loss (KL-divergence) to match the ensemble, plus an adversarial robustness loss (e.g., Jensen-Shannon divergence) to stay confident against the Advocate's critiques. We will adapt evaluation metrics from unlearning and causal mediation analysis to measure bias reduction and robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel distillation framework that explicitly mitigates ensemble bias transfer, advancing the goal of creating efficient, trustworthy models for sensitive applications. It will also provide new benchmarks and causal analysis tools for evaluating the robustness and calibration of distilled models, extending pre-2026 unlearning and medical adaptation research.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard adversarial training or distillation, which uses generic perturbations, our method employs a learned Advocate to generate critiques specifically designed to expose the ensemble's blind spots, framing bias mitigation as an adversarial game between the Advocate and the student.

proposals.title: CommitteeNet: Dynamic Teacher Weighting for Robust and Specialized Knowledge Distillation
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation (KD) is a cornerstone for deploying large models, but standard approaches—using a single teacher or a static ensemble average—fail to capture the nuanced expertise of multiple specialized models. This is critical in high-stakes domains like medicine, where different teachers may excel on different sub-tasks or data modalities. A more adaptive distillation method is needed to create compact, robust student models that leverage the collective, context-specific intelligence of a teacher committee.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distillation framework which dynamically weights the contributions of an ensemble of teacher models for each input, based on their estimated competence, will produce a student model that outperforms those trained with static averaging, particularly on heterogeneous data distributions and specialized tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a gating network that takes input features (e.g., embeddings, metadata) and outputs a probability distribution over teacher models. Teacher competence will be estimated per-sample using pre-computed confidence metrics (e.g., predictive entropy, attention patterns) and historical performance on a held-out validation set clustered by data similarity. The student model will be trained to minimize a loss (e.g., KL divergence) against the dynamically weighted soft targets from the committee, with the gating network's parameters updated jointly via meta-gradient descent to optimize final student validation performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, generalizable framework for context-aware knowledge distillation, 2) new evaluation protocols to measure student robustness and specialization gain over static KD baselines, and 3) empirical validation in domains like medical QA, where teacher specialization (e.g., radiology vs. cardiology models) is pronounced, advancing the 'teaming' and 'medical' adaptation themes in the lab.
proposals.novelty: While ensemble distillation exists, the key novelty is the sample-adaptive, learnable weighting mechanism inspired by committee decision-making, moving beyond uniform or pre-defined averaging. This directly challenges the standard KD paradigm by formalizing teachers as a deliberative body with variable influence.

proposals.title: EM-Distill: Adaptive Knowledge Distillation Under Latent Domain Shift for Robust Medical LLMs
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) adapted for medical applications often face performance degradation due to latent domain shifts in deployment data, such as variations in clinical note style or patient population. While multi-teacher knowledge distillation can leverage diverse expert models, and Expectation-Maximization (EM) can estimate latent variables, no existing method synergistically uses EM to guide distillation in the presence of an unknown shift. This gap limits the robust, data-efficient adaptation of LLMs in critical, heterogeneous medical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an EM algorithm, which iteratively infers latent domain assignments for unlabeled target data and uses these to re-weight teacher contributions in a distillation loss, will yield a student model more robust to domain shift than standard distillation or shift-agnostic ensemble methods.
proposals.method: We will develop an EM loop where the E-step estimates a soft domain assignment for each target instance using the student model's output divergence from each domain-specialized teacher (e.g., models fine-tuned on different hospital note corpora). The M-step updates the student model via a weighted distillation loss, where the KL-divergence penalty from each teacher is scaled by the corresponding E-step assignment probability. We will instantiate this with clinical LLMs (e.g., adapted Flan-T5 or LLaMA) and evaluate on benchmark medical QA datasets with synthetically induced and real-world domain shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, principled framework for domain-adaptive distillation, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating LLM robustness under latent medical domain shifts, extending prior unlearning and adaptation evaluation work, and 3) empirical evidence on the efficacy of EM-guided teacher weighting for improving model generalization in data-scarce, high-stakes scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop integration of latent variable estimation (EM) directly into the multi-teacher distillation objective, creating a co-adaptive process where the student's learning clarifies the domain shift, and the clarified shift refines the learning—a capability absent in prior static ensemble or post-hoc domain adaptation methods.

proposals.title: Ensemble Unlearning: A Multi-Teacher Knowledge Distillation Framework for Selective Forgetting in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current approaches to machine unlearning for LLMs, such as fine-tuning or gradient ascent, often rely on a single 'teacher' model (the original model) to guide a 'student' (the unlearned model), which can propagate or amplify the original model's biases and errors. This single-teacher paradigm is particularly problematic in high-stakes domains like medicine, where factual accuracy and the ability to remove harmful or outdated information are critical. A more robust framework is needed to improve the reliability and auditability of unlearned models.
proposals.hypothesis: Distilling knowledge from an ensemble of teachers—including the original model, a partially unlearned model, and a model trained on verified, curated evidence—will produce a student model that more effectively forgets targeted information while better preserving general utility and factual correctness compared to single-teacher distillation.
proposals.method: We will develop a benchmark for ensemble unlearning, extending frameworks like OpenUnlearning. The method involves: 1) Creating an ensemble from models with different knowledge states (e.g., base model, model after gradient-based unlearning, and a model like GenAudit's evidence-based corrector). 2) Designing a causal-inspired distillation loss that weights teachers' contributions based on metrics of factual correctness (from GenAudit) and forget quality. 3) Training a student model via this weighted distillation objective, evaluating on medical QA and fact-checking tasks to measure the trade-off between unlearning efficacy and retained performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, more robust algorithmic framework for LLM unlearning, moving beyond single-point guidance. It will yield a new benchmark for evaluating multi-teacher unlearning and offer empirical insights into how ensemble-based distillation mitigates the stability-plasticity dilemma in model editing, directly contributing to safer model deployment in domains like healthcare.
proposals.novelty: While ensemble methods and knowledge distillation are well-studied, their application to the *unlearning* problem is novel. This approach challenges the core assumption that unlearning requires guidance primarily from the model being unlearned, instead proposing a structured 'team' of teachers to balance forgetting with preservation of validated knowledge.

proposals.title: Free Energy Distillation: A Thermodynamic Framework for Balancing Fidelity and Entropy in Model Compression
proposals.motivation: Standard knowledge distillation (KD) treats the teacher as a static target, often leading to students that are merely compressed replicas, potentially inheriting the teacher's biases and failing to discover more efficient internal representations. This is especially critical in domains like medicine, where robust and efficient models are needed for clinical teaming. A principled framework to explicitly trade off teacher fidelity against student exploratory capacity is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: Formulating the distillation objective as the minimization of a thermodynamic free energy analog (F = U - TS) will produce student models that achieve better trade-offs between task performance (high teacher fidelity) and robust, diverse internal representations (high entropy), leading to improved generalization and efficiency.
proposals.method: We will design a novel loss function L_F = L_fidelity(Student, Teacher) - β * H(Student), where L_fidelity is a task-specific term (e.g., KL divergence on logits), H(Student) is an entropy measure (e.g., over intermediate layer representations or output distributions), and β is a temperature-like hyperparameter. We will implement and test this framework by distilling large language models (LLMs) like Llama-2 or clinical adaptations (e.g., Med-PaLM) onto smaller architectures. Evaluation will use standard accuracy metrics alongside novel causal-inspired metrics for representation diversity and robustness, benchmarked against traditional KD and recent unlearning baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, theoretically grounded loss function for KD that makes the fidelity-diversity trade-off explicit and tunable. It will contribute rigorous evaluation metrics for student model robustness and internal representation quality, advancing the fields of model compression and efficient LLM deployment, particularly for medical applications.
proposals.novelty: While entropy regularization exists in ML, directly mapping the full thermodynamic free energy minimization principle (U - TS) to KD, with the teacher explicitly as the 'internal energy' anchor, is a novel conceptual and algorithmic framing. This moves beyond viewing the teacher as just a target to viewing it as a guide within an exploration-exploitation trade-off.

proposals.title: Co-Adaptive Distillation: Dynamic Objective Selection for Efficient Knowledge Transfer in Evolving Models
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation is a cornerstone for deploying large models, but static distillation objectives fail to adapt to the teacher model's evolution during fine-tuning or continual learning, leading to suboptimal student performance. This is particularly critical in domains like medicine, where models are frequently updated with new data, and in LLM unlearning, where the teacher's knowledge distribution shifts rapidly. A dynamic framework is needed to maximize transfer efficiency at every stage of the teacher's lifecycle.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a co-adaptive distillation framework, which dynamically selects and weights distillation objectives based on real-time analysis of the evolving teacher's outputs, will yield faster student convergence and higher final fidelity than static distillation, especially in scenarios involving model editing or sequential adaptation.
proposals.method: We will develop a meta-controller that monitors the teacher's output properties (e.g., prediction entropy, confidence calibration, layer-wise representation drift). This controller will use a lightweight policy network, trained via reinforcement learning or online bandit algorithms, to select from a portfolio of distillation losses (KL divergence, attention transfer, gradient matching) and weight them. The student model is trained with this adaptive composite loss, creating a feedback loop where the teacher's updates influence the objective, and the student's progress informs subsequent selections. We will evaluate using causal mediation analysis to isolate the effect of dynamic weighting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, automated method for aligning distillation with teacher model dynamics, improving the efficiency of model compression and specialization. It will yield new metrics for characterizing teacher-student alignment during training and a benchmark for adaptive distillation, with direct applications in medical model adaptation and LLM unlearning pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses fixed or scheduled loss combinations, our approach introduces a closed-loop, data-driven controller that makes the distillation objective a function of the teacher's instantaneous state, a concept unexplored in pre-2026 literature. This moves beyond static benchmarking to in-training adaptation.

proposals.title: Beyond Logit Matching: A Causal Framework for Distilling Teacher Rationales in Knowledge Distillation
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation (KD) is a cornerstone for deploying large language models (LLMs), but standard objectives like Cross-Word Token Matching (CWTM) and Distillation with Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence on predictions (DKPP) treat the teacher as a static label source. This fails to capture the teacher's internal reasoning process, which is critical for distilling robust, generalizable, and interpretable models, especially in high-stakes domains like medicine. A deeper understanding of what to distill from teacher outputs is needed to move beyond simple output mimicry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distillation objectives designed to capture the teacher's causal reasoning pathways—specifically, the rationales behind its predictions—will yield student models that are more robust to distribution shifts and better at unlearning spurious correlations than those trained with standard logit-matching losses.
proposals.method: We will develop a suite of novel distillation loss functions grounded in causal inference and rationale extraction. First, we will adapt methods from causal mediation analysis to design a loss that encourages the student to align with the teacher's most salient input features (token attributions). Second, we will formulate a contrastive distillation objective that trains the student to match the teacher's output distribution on counterfactual inputs, promoting reasoning alignment beyond surface patterns.
