We will evaluate these losses against CWTM and DKPP baselines on the OpenUnlearning benchmark and medical adaptation tasks, using robustness and unlearning efficacy as key metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, principled framework for designing distillation objectives that target teacher reasoning, not just outputs. It will deliver concrete, scalable loss functions and an empirical analysis of their impact on model robustness and specialization, directly informing more effective LLM compression and adaptation pipelines for sensitive applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses teacher outputs as training signals, we propose to treat the teacher as a source of causal rationales, explicitly designing losses to distill its decision-making process—a shift from *what* the teacher predicts to *why* it predicts it.

proposals.title: Born-Again Networks for Unlearning and Adaptation: A Unified Evaluation in Vision, Language, and Medical Domains
proposals.motivation: Born-Again Networks (BANs), where a student model is trained to mimic a teacher of identical architecture, have shown promise for knowledge distillation and model compression. However, their potential for enabling controlled 'unlearning' of specific concepts or for efficient adaptation to new domains (e.g., medical AI) remains underexplored. This is critical as demands grow for models that can forget sensitive data or adapt to specialized tasks without catastrophic forgetting or full retraining.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that BAN training, when applied with targeted data curation and loss functions, can serve as an effective and efficient mechanism for both task-specific unlearning and domain adaptation, outperforming naive fine-tuning and matching specialized unlearning methods on a unified set of evaluation metrics.
proposals.method: We will extend BANs to three complex tasks: (1) Vision: unlearning specific object classes from ImageNet classifiers; (2) NLP: unlearning factual associations (evaluated with benchmarks like zsRE) and adapting clinical tone in LLMs; (3) Medical Vision-Language: adapting general VLMs to radiology report generation. We will employ a causal evaluation framework to measure efficacy, using metrics for unlearning (e.g., accuracy on forgotten concepts, membership inference attacks) and adaptation (domain-specific performance, retention of general knowledge). Methods will be benchmarked against baselines like gradient ascent and fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evaluation of BANs as a unified technique for unlearning and adaptation across modalities. It will deliver a rigorous benchmark, new metrics for causal assessment of knowledge removal, and practical insights for deploying adaptable, auditable models in sensitive domains like healthcare.
proposals.novelty: While BANs are known for distillation, applying them as a core primitive for *unlearning* and *cross-domain medical adaptation* is novel. The work uniquely integrates Zachary Chase Lipton's research themes—unlearning, LLMs, medical AI, and rigorous evaluation—into a single, methodologically coherent study.

proposals.title: Anchored Multi-Generation Learning: A Feedback-Controlled Framework to Prevent Catastrophic Forgetting in Sequential Model Distillation
proposals.motivation: Sequential knowledge distillation, where a student model becomes the next teacher, is efficient but prone to catastrophic forgetting and distributional collapse, degrading core competencies over generations. This is particularly critical in high-stakes domains like medicine, where model drift can introduce factual errors and compromise reliability. Existing methods lack robust, automated mechanisms to detect and correct this drift during the sequential training process itself.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that anchoring the sequential training process with a static ground-truth data arm and implementing a divergence-aware feedback controller will dynamically stabilize training, preventing catastrophic forgetting and enabling controlled, positive model evolution across generations.
proposals.method: We will implement a two-arm training pipeline: a sequential 'student-teacher' arm and a parallel 'anchor' arm trained solely on original ground-truth data. For each generation, a monitoring module will compute distributional divergence metrics (e.g., Fréchet Inception Distance on features, performance delta on a curated causal validation set) between the current student's synthetic outputs and the anchor model's outputs. A PID controller will use this divergence signal to adjust the next student's training curriculum, dynamically rebalancing the loss function or data mixture between synthetic teacher data and fresh ground-truth data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, automated framework for robust sequential distillation, directly addressing the unlearning of core facts in LLMs. It will yield a new benchmark for evaluating multi-generation learning stability and a set of actionable metrics for divergence-aware feedback control, applicable to both language and vision-language models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard sequential distillation or data augmentation, our method introduces a closed-loop feedback system that continuously measures and corrects for distributional drift, making the training process self-stabilizing. This integrates causal evaluation and unlearning perspectives into the core training dynamics of model iteration.

proposals.title: Evolving Adapters: A Directed Evolution Framework for Progressive Knowledge Refinement in Bottleneck Adapter Networks
proposals.motivation: Current methods for adapting large language models (LLMs) to specialized domains, such as medicine, often rely on single-stage fine-tuning or simple knowledge distillation, which can plateau in performance or fail to robustly integrate new knowledge. Inspired by the success of unlearning and factual correction techniques (e.g., GenAudit), there is a need for systematic, iterative adaptation strategies that can progressively refine model capabilities. This project addresses the challenge of efficiently guiding a model sequence toward superior, specialized performance without full retraining.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a directed evolution paradigm—with iterative cycles of stochastic training variation (mutation) and performance-based selection pressure—to a sequence of Bottleneck Adapter Networks (BANs) will yield a more performant and robust final model on a target task than standard multi-generation distillation or single-stage adaptation.
proposals.method: We will implement a directed evolution loop for BAN sequences. Starting from a base LLM (e.g., LLaMA-2 or a clinical BERT variant) with a randomly initialized adapter, each generation involves: 1) Training a 'student' BAN via a stochastic optimizer on data filtered or weighted by a 'selection pressure' function (e.g., a curriculum of increasingly difficult medical QA examples or a higher accuracy threshold on a factual consistency benchmark). 2) Evaluating candidate students on a held-out validation set that measures both target task accuracy and a robustness metric (e.g., consistency under counterfactuals). 3) Selecting the top-performing student's adapter parameters to serve as the initialized 'teacher' for the next generation. This cycle repeats, with the selection pressure intensifying, simulating evolutionary optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, biologically-inspired framework for iterative model adaptation, delivering a concrete algorithm for evolving BAN sequences. It will yield new empirical insights into how structured, multi-generational training can overcome performance plateaus in domain adaptation. The resulting evaluation suite and metrics will extend benchmarking practices in unlearning and model editing to progressive adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge distillation and progressive networks exist, formalizing the training of an adapter sequence as a directed evolution process—with explicit, tunable selection pressures replacing a static distillation loss—is a novel conceptual and methodological shift. This moves beyond simple sequential training to an optimization-guided search in adapter space.

proposals.title: BAN-Drift: Bootstrapped Adaptive Networks with Concept Trajectory Modeling for Evolving Label Semantics
proposals.motivation: In real-world domains like medicine, where diagnostic criteria evolve, or in social media, where sentiment labels drift, models face concept drift within label shift. Existing continual learning and test-time adaptation methods often treat drift as a nuisance to overcome, not as a semantic trajectory to model. This limits long-term deployment and interpretability, as models lose the ability to understand historical labeling conventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the trajectory of label concept evolution across a sequence of bootstrapped models will yield more robust adaptation to non-stationary environments and enable recovery of past labeling semantics, compared to methods that only adapt to the current distribution.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-generation Bootstrapped Adaptive Network (BAN) sequence. Each student model will be trained on pseudo-labels from its immediate teacher and a novel 'Concept Trajectory Distillation' loss. This loss will distill a compact representation of the evolving label semantics, captured via changes in the teacher models' prediction heads or attention patterns across generations, into the student. We will evaluate using controlled synthetic drift benchmarks and real-world medical time-series datasets where diagnostic codes have evolved.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for learning with explicit concept trajectory modeling, 2) new evaluation metrics for a model's ability to recover past label semantics, and 3) insights into the long-term adaptability of self-training paradigms in non-stationary environments, directly relevant to medical AI and LLM unlearning for outdated information.
proposals.novelty: While bootstrapping and self-training are established, the explicit distillation of the label concept's evolution trajectory across model generations is novel. This moves beyond simple adaptation to enable temporal interpretation of label semantics, connecting unlearning (forgetting outdated concepts) and adaptation (learning new ones) within a single, causal framework.

proposals.title: Iterative Distillation Degradation: A Causal Study of Multi-Generation Knowledge Transfer in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation is a cornerstone for deploying large language models (LLMs), but its long-term effects across sequential generations are poorly understood. In safety-critical domains like medicine, where models are iteratively adapted and deployed, understanding whether sequential distillation amplifies errors, biases, or forgets critical facts is crucial for reliable human-AI teaming. This work addresses a core gap in unlearning and adaptation literature: the multi-generational stability of distilled knowledge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sequential teacher-student distillation, without exposure to original data, leads to progressive performance degradation and error amplification, particularly for factual knowledge and reasoning, and that this degradation can be causally attributed to the compounding of approximation errors in each generation.
proposals.method: We will construct multi-generation sequences (e.g., 5-10 steps) using established distillation methods (e.g., sequence-level knowledge distillation) on a base model (e.g., Llama-2-7B). Each new 'student' model is trained solely on the outputs of the previous generation model, forming a Born-Again Network (BAN) chain. We will evaluate each generation using unified benchmarks from OpenUnlearning (for fact retention/unlearning dynamics) and medical QA datasets, employing causal mediation analysis to trace error propagation. Metrics will track factual accuracy (inspired by GenAudit), reasoning consistency, and calibration.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic, causal analysis of multi-generation LLM distillation, quantifying the 'distillation decay' rate for different capabilities. It will yield practical guidelines for sustainable model deployment cycles and contribute a novel benchmark for evaluating long-term knowledge preservation in iterative learning processes, directly informing safer medical adaptation pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While single-step distillation is well-studied, analyzing sequential BAN chains as a dynamical system for LLMs is novel. Combining this with a causal, benchmark-driven evaluation framework bridges the researcher's themes of unlearning, evaluation, and medical adaptation in a unique way.

proposals.title: Interlingua for Neural Networks: A Universal Intermediate Representation for Cross-Architectural Knowledge Distillation
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation is a powerful tool for model compression and transfer, but current methods are brittle when applied across fundamentally different neural architectures (e.g., DenseNet to Transformer). This limits the ability to repurpose knowledge from legacy or specialized models into newer, more efficient, or heterogeneous designs, a critical need in domains like medical AI where data is scarce and models are diverse. Drawing an analogy from comparative linguistics, where a pivot language enables translation between unrelated tongues, we hypothesize that a universal intermediate representation can solve this architectural mismatch.
proposals.hypothesis: Projecting knowledge from a source model into an architecture-agnostic 'interlingua'—such as a semantic feature graph or a small proxy network—will enable more effective and robust distillation into a heterogeneous target model than direct distillation or feature-matching methods.
proposals.method: We will formalize the interlingua as a lightweight, interpretable graph structure where nodes represent high-level semantic concepts and edges represent relational constraints, distilled from the source model's internal representations. For a more data-driven approach, we will also explore training a small, neutral 'proxy' network (e.g., a simple MLP) on the source's outputs and internal activations. This interlingua will then be used as a stable training signal via a novel distillation loss to guide the target model, with rigorous evaluation on established vision (CIFAR-100, ImageNet) and medical (CheXpert) benchmarks comparing DenseNet, ResNet, and Vision Transformer families.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generalizable framework for cross-architectural knowledge transfer, reducing the need for paired architectural designs. It will yield a publicly available benchmark for evaluating such heterogeneous distillation methods. The findings will advance the practical 'teaming' of diverse AI models and inform principles of 'unlearning' by showing how to extract and re-embed core knowledge independently of its original implementation.
proposals.novelty: While prior distillation work focuses on architectural similarity or task-specific adaptations, our approach is novel in its direct inspiration from linguistic interlinguas to create a purposefully architecture-neutral knowledge representation. This shifts the paradigm from direct imitation to mediated semantic transfer.

proposals.title: Unified Black-Box Shift Correction via Architecture-Agnostic Surrogate Distillation
proposals.motivation: In real-world deployments, such as medical image diagnosis with thousands of conditions, label shift between training and test data is common and catastrophic. Black-Box Shift Estimation (BBSE) offers a solution but requires a consistent confusion matrix, which is computationally prohibitive to compute for each large, proprietary model in extreme multi-class settings. This creates a barrier to reliable model auditing and adaptation in heterogeneous, black-box environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, lightweight surrogate model, distilled from the outputs of diverse, pre-trained source architectures, can produce a sufficiently accurate and generalizable confusion matrix to enable robust, scalable BBSE across multiple black-box models and domains.
proposals.method: We will train a compact surrogate (e.g., a small Transformer or efficient CNN) via knowledge distillation on the softmax outputs of heterogeneous source models (e.g., ResNets, DenseNets, Vision Transformers) on a held-out validation set. This surrogate's confusion matrix will be used in the BBSE inversion step to estimate the target label distribution. We will evaluate the framework on large-scale medical imaging datasets (e.g., CheXpert with 14+ labels) and long-tail classification benchmarks, comparing estimated shifts against ground truth and downstream model calibration.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, scalable tool for label shift correction that works with any black-box classifier, reducing the computational cost of per-model BBSE. It will advance the fields of reliable machine learning and model auditing by providing a unified estimator for deployment monitoring, particularly in high-stakes, multi-model environments like clinical AI teams.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of heterogeneous knowledge distillation with BBSE to create an architecture-agnostic shift estimator is novel, moving beyond prior work that assumes a single, known model architecture or requires expensive per-model matrix operations.

proposals.title: Architectural Asymmetry in Knowledge Distillation: A Causal Framework for Analyzing Transfer Between DenseNets and ResNets
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation is a cornerstone for model compression and transfer, but prior work (pre-2026) largely assumes architectural homogeneity or treats the teacher as a black-box label generator. This overlooks a critical, underexplored axis: how do fundamentally different architectural inductive biases (e.g., feature reuse in DenseNets vs. residual learning in ResNets) affect what knowledge is transferred and how efficiently? Understanding this is key for principled cross-architecture teaming and efficient medical model adaptation, where legacy and state-of-the-art models of different families must collaborate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the efficacy and characteristics of knowledge transfer between architecturally heterogeneous models (e.g., DenseNet teacher to ResNet student) are causally influenced by the alignment—or misalignment—of their internal feature representations and gradient flow dynamics, leading to predictable, quantifiable bottlenecks not present in homogeneous distillation.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled, causal evaluation framework. Using established pre-trained DenseNet-121 and ResNet-50 models on ImageNet, we will perform bidirectional distillation (DenseNet->ResNet, ResNet->DenseNet). We will employ a suite of metrics: standard accuracy, but also novel causal mediation analyses to trace the flow of causal influence from teacher to student layers, and representation similarity metrics (CKA, PWCCA) to quantify feature alignment. We will adapt and extend unlearning evaluation techniques from OpenUnlearning to measure what specific 'knowledge units' fail to transfer, treating failed transfer as a form of 'selective forgetting' induced by architectural mismatch.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal, mechanistic analysis of knowledge transfer across heterogeneous architectures, moving beyond performance reporting to explain *why* transfer succeeds or fails. It will deliver a novel diagnostic benchmark and metrics for cross-architecture distillation, directly informing strategies for medical model adaptation and efficient 'teaming' of diverse legacy and modern models. The insights could also refine unlearning methods by identifying architecturally-invariant knowledge components.
proposals.novelty: While distillation is well-studied, a causal, representation-level analysis focused on the *interaction* of distinct architectural families (DenseNet vs. ResNet) is novel. Applying the lens of unlearning evaluation to diagnose transfer failures reframes the problem in a new, actionable light, connecting Lipton's themes of unlearning, evaluation, and model teaming in a unique way.

proposals.title: Diagnosing Capacity Overmatch: A Taxonomy and Causal Framework for Robust Bayesian Neural Network Distillation
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation is a core technique for deploying large, calibrated models like Bayesian Neural Networks (BNNs) in resource-constrained settings, such as medical AI or on-device applications. However, the common practice of distilling into a smaller 'student' model is ill-posed when the student's capacity, in terms of parameters, architecture, or data exposure, paradoxically exceeds the teacher's in specific dimensions. Pre-2026 work lacks a formal framework to diagnose how this 'capacity overmatch' affects critical outcomes like calibration transfer and uncertainty faithfulness, leading to unreliable compressed models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that capacity overmatch is not monolithic but decomposes into distinct, measurable axes (parametric, data, architectural), each with a predictable and divergent causal impact on distillation outcomes, enabling the formulation of corrective design principles.
proposals.method: We will first establish a formal taxonomy of overmatch. Then, using controlled experiments on vision and medical text benchmarks, we will apply causal graph analysis to isolate the effect of each overmatch axis. We will distill from pre-trained BNN teachers (e.g., MC-Dropout networks) into student BNNs of varying architectures (e.g., Bayesian ResNets, Transformers) where we systematically vary one capacity dimension at a time. Outcomes will be evaluated using metrics for predictive accuracy, calibration (ECE), and uncertainty similarity (e.g., KL divergence on predictive distributions), building on evaluation frameworks from prior unlearning and audit research.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first diagnostic framework to predict and mitigate risks in BNN distillation, moving from heuristic practice to principled design. It will deliver actionable guidelines for practitioners to avoid pathological overmatch and a new benchmark suite for evaluating distillation robustness, directly contributing to safer model compression for high-stakes domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior distillation research focuses on under-matched students, we systematically study the overlooked and counter-intuitive regime of overmatch. Furthermore, we uniquely combine causal experimental design from modern ML evaluation with the rigorous uncertainty quantification of BNNs to dissect this problem.

proposals.title: Ecological Succession in Knowledge Transfer: A Causal Framework for Analyzing Overpowered Student Models in Bayesian Augmented Networks
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation and Bayesian Augmented Networks (BANs) typically assume the student is smaller than the teacher. However, real-world scenarios like continual learning or medical AI often involve adapting a model to new data or tasks, potentially using a larger, more powerful student. The dynamics and stability of this 'capacity inversion' are poorly understood, risking inefficient training or performance collapse. This gap limits the safe deployment of adaptive LLMs in critical domains like medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the interaction between a teacher and an overpowering student in a BAN follows dynamics analogous to ecological succession, where stable 'mature' states (superior student performance) are achievable under specific conditions of architectural asymmetry and data shift, which can be characterized via causal mediation analysis.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled benchmark of BANs with varying capacity ratios (e.g., BERT-base teacher to BERT-large/LLaMA student). We adapt metrics from ecological succession (e.g., species turnover, resilience) to model training, tracking the evolution of the student's posterior and its divergence from the teacher's. Using causal graphs, we will treat the teacher's output as an intervention to isolate its effect on student optimization. Experiments will systematically vary data distribution shifts (simulating medical domain adaptation) and optimization constraints to map the 'succession' landscape.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical framework for understanding capacity inversion in distillation, leading to practical guidelines for stable training of overpowered students. It will contribute a new benchmark and causal evaluation metrics for 'unlearning' or overwriting teacher biases, directly informing safer adaptation of large models in teams and clinical settings.
proposals.novelty: The ecological succession analogy provides a fundamentally new lens for BAN analysis, moving beyond static compression views. Applying causal mediation to disentangle teacher influence in this asymmetric setting is a novel methodological approach not explored in prior distillation or unlearning work.

proposals.title: Unlearning Drift: Causal Disentanglement of Model Capacity and Teacher Semantics in Bayesian Knowledge Transfer
proposals.motivation: In real-world applications like medical AI, label semantics evolve (e.g., disease definitions change), but models are often trained via knowledge distillation from a static, lower-capacity teacher. This creates a critical confounder: a high-capacity Bayesian student's predictive divergence could stem from its superior representation, the teacher's capacity limits, or the teacher's outdated concepts. Current unlearning and adaptation benchmarks lack frameworks to diagnose this temporal entanglement, risking brittle systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian neural network, given sufficient capacity and appropriate causal scaffolding, can disentangle and explicitly model the latent conceptual drift in its teacher's label semantics, enabling it to outperform the teacher not just in accuracy but in temporal robustness.
proposals.method: We will develop a temporally-aware Bayesian neural network (BNN) student trained on soft labels from a fixed-capacity teacher model. We introduce a structured latent variable within the BNN's prior to capture suspected semantic drift, using causal discovery techniques (e.g., conditional independence tests on predictive discrepancies over time) to distinguish drift from other factors. The learning algorithm will combine variational inference with a novel temporal consistency loss, regularized by metrics inspired by unlearning evaluation (e.g., comparing forget/retain performance on data slices defined by prediction confidence shifts).
proposals.contribution: 1) A new benchmarking framework for evaluating temporal robustness in teacher-student setups under latent concept drift. 2) A novel Bayesian algorithm that enables a student to identify and compensate for a teacher's outdated semantics, advancing robust knowledge transfer. 3) Insights for medical LLM adaptation, where diagnostic criteria evolve, by providing a method to audit and fix factual errors stemming from semantic drift.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond static unlearning or adaptation by framing teacher-student discrepancy as a causal temporal inference problem, explicitly modeling the evolution of label semantics as a latent variable—a perspective not addressed in existing knowledge distillation or continual learning literature.

proposals.title: When the Student Outpaces the Master: A Causal Framework for Evaluating Unlearning in High-Capacity Student Models
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation and unlearning in BANs (Born-Again Networks) are typically studied in a regime where the student is a compressed or capacity-matched version of the teacher. However, real-world scenarios like continual learning or model repair often involve updating a large, general-purpose model (the student) with a smaller, specialized, or corrected model (the teacher). The dynamics and efficacy of unlearning when the student's representational capacity exceeds the teacher's remain under-explored, posing risks for deploying unlearning methods in safety-critical domains like medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in the high-capacity student regime, standard unlearning metrics will be insufficient, as the student can 'memorize' both the teacher's correction and the original erroneous behavior, leading to superficial compliance that masks residual knowledge and creates evaluation blind spots.
proposals.method: We will construct a causal evaluation framework, adapting concepts from Lipton's prior work on GenAudit and OpenUnlearning. Using established vision and language benchmarks (e.g., Medical QA datasets), we will train large 'student' models from 'teacher' models of smaller scale or with intentionally introduced factual errors. We will then apply state-of-the-art unlearning algorithms (e.g., gradient ascent, influence unlearning) and measure performance not just on standard forget/retain metrics, but on causal mediation traces and counterfactual outputs to probe for latent knowledge retention. Experiments will systematically vary the capacity gap and task complexity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of unlearning in asymmetric BANs, yielding a more robust evaluation suite for real-world unlearning. It will produce concrete guidelines for when high-capacity student unlearning is reliable and contribute a new benchmark focused on this regime to the community.
proposals.novelty: While BANs and unlearning are established, prior work assumes symmetric or compressing architectures. Our focus on the overpowering student case, combined with a causal, probe-based evaluation strategy, addresses a critical blind spot in the trustworthiness of model editing and teaming pipelines.

proposals.title: Curriculum Distillation: A Causal Framework for Efficient and Robust Knowledge Transfer in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation is a core technique for creating efficient, deployable models, but standard approaches treat all training data equally, potentially leading to inefficient learning or fragile performance on complex reasoning tasks. This is especially critical in high-stakes domains like medicine, where models must reliably handle a spectrum of difficulty. A principled, automated curriculum for distillation could significantly improve sample efficiency and final model robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum learning strategy, which sequences distillation samples from simple to complex based on a causal model of task difficulty, will yield student models that achieve higher final performance and better generalization than those trained with a standard, non-sequenced approach, particularly on tasks requiring multi-step reasoning.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a teacher LLM (e.g., GPT-4) generates and scores a dataset of (question, answer, reasoning chain) tuples. Using causal discovery methods (e.g., PC algorithm) on features like reasoning step count and teacher confidence, we will infer a structural causal model (SCM) of difficulty. This SCM will define the curriculum. The student model (e.g., a smaller Llama variant) will then be distilled via sequential fine-tuning, starting on easy samples and gradually introducing harder ones as defined by the curriculum. We will benchmark against standard distillation and random curricula using metrics from OpenUnlearning and medical QA datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, causality-informed framework for automatic curriculum design in distillation, 2) a rigorous, multi-domain evaluation protocol for curriculum learning efficacy, and 3) empirical evidence on how difficulty progression impacts knowledge compression and reasoning fidelity in student models, with implications for safe and efficient model deployment.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning and distillation are both well-studied, their intersection is underexplored. The novelty lies in using causal inference to formally model and operationalize 'difficulty' for sequencing distillation data, moving beyond simple heuristics like sequence length or loss.

proposals.title: Adversarial Knowledge Distillation for Robust and Efficient Model Unlearning
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation is a core technique for compressing large language models (LLMs), but standard distillation objectives often fail to capture the nuanced, multi-modal distribution of the teacher's outputs, especially for safety-critical domains like medicine. Concurrently, the field of machine unlearning seeks to efficiently remove specific knowledge or behaviors from models, yet current methods lack robust evaluation and can degrade general performance. A more distribution-aware distillation paradigm could address both compression and targeted unlearning needs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing knowledge distillation as an adversarial game, where a discriminator network is trained to distinguish between teacher and student outputs, will produce a student model that more faithfully replicates the teacher's full output distribution, leading to more effective and verifiable unlearning when applied to targeted subsets of the teacher's knowledge.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) framework for sequence generation. The student LLM acts as the generator, and a separate discriminator (e.g., a transformer encoder) is trained to classify sequences as originating from the teacher or student. The student is trained with a combined loss: a standard cross-entropy loss for task performance and an adversarial loss to fool the discriminator. For unlearning, we will apply this adversarial distillation objective only on a 'retain' dataset, while using a causal inference framework to construct a counterfactual 'forget' set for evaluation, measuring the divergence in student outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel adversarial distillation algorithm for LLMs that improves distributional fidelity, 2) a new methodology for applying this to the unlearning problem, providing a stronger guarantee that forgotten information is removed, and 3) a rigorous evaluation benchmark, extending frameworks like OpenUnlearning, to assess unlearning efficacy without compromising utility on retained tasks.
proposals.novelty: While GANs have been used for distillation in computer vision, their application to sequence-to-sequence LLM distillation for the explicit purpose of enhancing unlearning is novel. This approach moves beyond matching pointwise probabilities to matching the generative *process*, which is critical for verifying the absence of specific knowledge.

proposals.title: RL-Distill: Reinforcement Learning for Policy-Guided Knowledge Distillation in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation (KD) is a core technique for creating efficient, deployable student models from large teacher LLMs. However, standard KD methods, which primarily minimize distributional divergence (e.g., KL divergence), treat all tokens equally and may fail to preserve the teacher's nuanced reasoning or factual integrity, a critical concern in domains like medicine. This limitation is highlighted by recent work on auditing and fixing factual errors in model outputs, suggesting a need for more targeted, outcome-oriented distillation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing student training as a reinforcement learning (RL) problem, where the student's policy is optimized against dense, structured reward signals from the teacher, will yield student models that better preserve the teacher's high-level reasoning and factual accuracy compared to standard supervised distillation losses.
proposals.method: We will develop an RL-based distillation framework where the student LM acts as a policy generating sequences. The reward function will be a composite of: (1) a *causal fidelity reward* based on the teacher's per-token logits or hidden states, (2) a *factual consistency reward* computed by verifying generated statements against a knowledge source or the teacher's own verifiable outputs, and (3) a *task-specific reward* (e.g., for medical QA). We will employ on-policy RL algorithms like Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to optimize the student policy, initializing from a model pre-trained with standard supervised fine-tuning. Evaluation will use established unlearning/audit benchmarks (e.g., OpenUnlearning, GenAudit) to measure factual retention and error rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled RL framework for knowledge distillation that prioritizes high-level reasoning fidelity over mere token-level mimicry. It will provide new evaluation metrics for distillation quality grounded in causal and factual accuracy, advancing the goal of creating reliable, compact models for sensitive applications like medical teaming AI. The resulting method will be a step towards 'precision distillation' for safety-critical domains.
proposals.novelty: While RL is used for aligning LLMs with human preferences (RLHF), applying it specifically to distill a teacher's *reasoning policy*—rather than human feedback—and integrating causal and factual audit signals into the reward is a novel synthesis of Lipton's themes of unlearning, evaluation, and medical adaptation.

proposals.title: Meta-Distill: Learning to Distill via Gradient-Based Meta-Learning for Efficient Model Specialization
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation is a cornerstone for deploying large language models (LLMs) in specialized domains like medicine, but current distillation losses (e.g., KL divergence) are generic and suboptimal. Manually designing or tuning task-specific distillation procedures is costly and does not generalize, hindering the efficient creation of accurate, compact models for critical applications. A method to automatically learn a distillation strategy from data would significantly accelerate and improve model adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning framework can learn a parametric distillation loss function or policy that generalizes across tasks, producing more effective student models than fixed, handcrafted losses when applied to new, unseen specialization tasks.
proposals.method: We will frame distillation as a bi-level optimization problem. An inner loop performs standard distillation for a given task using a loss function parameterized by a small meta-network. An outer loop updates these meta-parameters by evaluating the distilled student's performance on a held-out validation set across a meta-training distribution of tasks (e.g., diverse medical QA, fact-editing, or reasoning benchmarks). We will implement this using a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) variant, where the meta-objective is the student's task performance. The learned meta-loss will then be evaluated on held-out meta-test tasks for domains like medical adaptation and factual unlearning.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel, task-adaptive distillation algorithm that automates loss function design. It will provide a unified framework for efficient model specialization, directly contributing to themes of LLM adaptation and unlearning. We will release a benchmark and the meta-learned distillation strategies to facilitate research into learned optimization for model compression.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been used for few-shot learning and hyperparameter tuning, its application to directly learn a distillation loss function from task distributions is novel. This moves beyond using meta-learning only for model initialization, instead meta-optimizing the knowledge transfer process itself.

proposals.title: Born-Again Networks at Scale: A Causal Framework for Efficient Knowledge Transfer in Vision and Language
proposals.motivation: Born-Again Networks (BANs), where a student model is trained to mimic a teacher of identical architecture, have shown promise for knowledge distillation and model compression on small datasets. However, their scalability to large-scale datasets like ImageNet and modern, very deep architectures remains unexplored. This gap is critical, as the computational cost of training massive models necessitates more efficient knowledge transfer and refinement techniques, especially for resource-constrained domains like medical AI where model 'teaming' and adaptation are key.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, multi-stage BAN training regimen, informed by causal analysis of feature reuse, can achieve superior performance and more robust feature representations compared to standard training or single-stage distillation on large-scale vision and language tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the BAN framework to ResNet and Vision Transformer architectures on ImageNet, and to decoder-only language models on a large text corpus. The core method involves iterative self-distillation over multiple generations (teacher -> student -> teacher). We will integrate a causal evaluation framework to identify which learned features are truly transferred versus relearned, using metrics from pre-2026 causal mediation analysis. This will guide the design of a loss function that combines task loss, KL divergence, and a novel feature alignment penalty based on the causal analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, large-scale benchmark for BANs, establishing their efficacy beyond small-scale studies. It will deliver a practical, efficient training recipe for high-performance model families and a causal diagnostic tool for understanding knowledge transfer, contributing directly to themes of model refinement and 'unlearning' via iterative retraining.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in scaling BANs to a previously unexplored regime of dataset and model size, and in uniquely integrating causal inference techniques to analyze and improve the knowledge transfer process, moving beyond purely correlational performance metrics.

proposals.title: Dual: Bidirectional Knowledge Transfer Cycles for Model Refinement and Unlearning
proposals.motivation: Current knowledge transfer paradigms, like distillation, are largely unidirectional, flowing from a static teacher to a student. This limits the potential for iterative refinement and collaborative learning between models. In the context of LLM unlearning and factual correction—critical for safety and reliability—a bidirectional cycle could enable more robust and efficient model editing by allowing a corrected student to 'teach back' improvements to the original teacher.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that establishing a bidirectional knowledge transfer cycle between a teacher and a student model, where the student can distill corrected or specialized knowledge back into the teacher, will lead to more effective and stable model refinement, particularly for tasks like factual error correction and selective unlearning, compared to one-way distillation.
proposals.method: We will adapt a causal mediation analysis framework to identify and isolate knowledge pathways for transfer. Starting with a base LLM as the teacher, we will create a student via fine-tuning or unlearning (e.g., on a benchmark like OpenUnlearning). We then implement a 'reverse distillation' phase, using KL-divergence and contrastive losses to transfer the student's refined behavior on target concepts back to the teacher, while monitoring performance on retained knowledge. The cycle will be evaluated using causal metrics for intervention stability and standard benchmarks for general capability preservation.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a formal framework for bidirectional model co-evolution, providing new algorithms for iterative model improvement. It will yield empirical insights into whether such cycles can enhance the stability and specificity of unlearning and factual correction (extending ideas from GenAudit) without catastrophic forgetting, advancing towards more collaborative and audit-able AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge distillation is well-studied, the concept of a student model systematically teaching a teacher to improve or unlearn is largely unexplored. This inverts the standard hierarchy and creates a novel, closed-loop system for model adaptation.

proposals.title: Inverse Distillation: Diagnosing Teacher Model Weaknesses via Systematic Student Divergence Analysis
proposals.motivation: Knowledge distillation is a cornerstone for deploying large models, but it assumes the teacher is a reliable source of truth. In high-stakes domains like medicine, undiagnosed teacher errors—such as factual inaccuracies or spurious correlations—can propagate to and be amplified in the student, leading to unsafe deployments. Current evaluation paradigms (e.g., benchmarking on held-out data) often fail to isolate and characterize these latent teacher weaknesses.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic analysis of where a distilled student model diverges from its teacher, especially on data where the student is correct and the teacher is not, can serve as a high-signal diagnostic tool to uncover systematic errors, biases, and knowledge gaps in the teacher model.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal framework to analyze the teacher-student distillation pipeline. First, we will generate a diagnostic dataset by comparing teacher and student outputs on targeted probes (e.g., from medical QA benchmarks). Using methods inspired by GenAudit, we will collect evidence for correct answers. We will then apply causal mediation analysis and feature attribution techniques (e.g., TracIn) to the teacher model, using student divergence as an intervention signal to trace errors back to specific layers, attention heads, or training data artifacts in the teacher.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel evaluation framework for 'teacher auditability,' 2) a set of metrics for quantifying teacher weakness severity and propagation risk, and 3) case studies in the medical domain demonstrating how inverse distillation can find clinically relevant errors in large vision-language models before they are deployed via distillation.
proposals.novelty: While existing work focuses on improving student fidelity, we invert the paradigm, using the student as a lens to debug the teacher. This connects causal analysis of model internals with the practical problem of auditing foundation models for safe distillation, a direction largely unexplored in pre-2026 unlearning and adaptation literature.

proposals.title: Ensemble Unlearning: Parallel Distillation of Diverse Student Models for Robust Knowledge Removal
proposals.motivation: Current LLM unlearning methods, evaluated in benchmarks like OpenUnlearning, often degrade model utility or fail to fully erase target knowledge, risking privacy violations and factual errors. This is critical in sensitive domains like medicine, where models must forget incorrect or private data without compromising diagnostic reliability. A robust, generalizable unlearning solution is needed to ensure safe model deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: An ensemble of diverse student models, distilled in parallel from a single teacher model after targeted unlearning, will achieve more robust and verifiable knowledge removal while better preserving general model utility than any single unlearned model.
proposals.method: We will adapt a large teacher model (e.g., Llama-2) using a causal unlearning method (e.g., gradient ascent). From this teacher, we will distill a cohort of smaller, architecturally diverse student models (e.g., varying depths, attention mechanisms) in parallel using standard knowledge distillation. The ensemble's final output will be a weighted vote. We will rigorously evaluate using the OpenUnlearning benchmark for unlearning efficacy and causal metrics for side-effects, alongside standard language modeling and medical QA tasks to assess utility preservation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, ensemble-based framework for reliable unlearning that mitigates the brittleness of single-model approaches. It will deliver a new evaluation paradigm for unlearning robustness and offer practical insights for deploying safely adapted models in team-based clinical or research settings where error consistency is paramount.
proposals.novelty: While ensemble methods and distillation are well-studied for performance, their systematic application to the *unlearning* problem—explicitly creating diversity to enhance forgetting—is unexplored. This approach inverts the typical goal of ensembles from boosting knowledge to ensuring its absence.

proposals.title: Stochastic Weight Pruning for Efficient and Effective LLM Unlearning
proposals.motivation: Current approaches to machine unlearning for LLMs, such as gradient ascent or activation pruning, often degrade model utility or are computationally expensive. These methods focus on modifying activations or outputs, but a more fundamental and efficient approach may lie in directly modifying the model's causal structure—its weights. This is critical for domains like medicine, where models must efficiently forget outdated or incorrect information while preserving core competencies for safe human-AI teaming.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted, stochastic pruning of network weights or filters, guided by causal influence metrics, will enable more precise, efficient, and utility-preserving unlearning in LLMs compared to state-of-the-art activation-based methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal evaluation framework to identify weights most causally implicated in generating target 'forget' concepts. Using this, we will implement and benchmark stochastic weight-pruning algorithms (e.g., Lottery Ticket Hypothesis-inspired methods) against activation-editing baselines on the OpenUnlearning benchmark. We will adapt metrics from GenAudit to rigorously assess factual erasure and utility preservation, with specific testing on medical QA tasks to evaluate domain-specific robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, structurally-targeted paradigm for LLM unlearning, 2) a causal framework for identifying unlearning-relevant parameters, and 3) empirical evidence on the trade-offs between weight pruning and activation editing for forgetting, providing a new tool for efficient model adaptation and audit.
proposals.novelty: While pruning is known for model compression, its application as a primary mechanism for *causal unlearning*—systematically removing knowledge by severing specific synaptic connections rather than masking activations—is novel and challenges the prevailing focus on output-space interventions.

proposals.title: Deterministic Sparse Activation Networks: A New Paradigm for Robust and Auditable Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) rely on dense, stochastic activations, which contribute to unpredictable 'hallucinations' and make model behavior difficult to audit and correct. This is particularly problematic in high-stakes domains like medicine, where reliability and the ability to unlearn or fix specific factual errors are paramount. A deterministic, sparse activation scheme could provide a more robust and interpretable substrate for model reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training LLMs to use deterministic, inherently sparse activation patterns will yield models that are more robust to distribution shifts, less prone to hallucination, and more amenable to precise unlearning and factual correction than standard stochastic, dense models.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel training framework that replaces standard activation functions (e.g., ReLU, GELU) with a deterministic, k-sparse activation mechanism, enforced via a top-k gating function applied to intermediate transformer layer outputs. This will be integrated with a routing loss to encourage task-relevant specialization of sparse pathways. We will evaluate this architecture against standard baselines on established benchmarks for unlearning (e.g., our OpenUnlearning framework), factual error correction (extending GenAudit), and medical QA robustness, using causal mediation analysis to trace information flow through sparse pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new model architecture class with deterministic, sparse activations, 2) a rigorous evaluation of its benefits for robustness, unlearning, and factual editing compared to standard LLMs, and 3) new causal analysis tools for understanding sparse model decisions, directly advancing the themes of reliable AI and human-AI teaming in sensitive domains.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the core stochasticity and density assumptions of modern LLMs, proposing deterministic sparsity as a first-class design principle for robustness, rather than as a post-hoc compression technique. It uniquely bridges unlearning, factual correction, and medical adaptation under a single, novel architectural hypothesis.

proposals.title: Selective Activation Pruning for Robustness: A Causal Evaluation of Magnitude-Based Interventions in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit brittleness, where small input perturbations can cause significant output errors, a critical concern in high-stakes domains like medicine. Standard pruning often targets small-magnitude activations for efficiency, but this may overlook large, potentially spurious activations that drive model fragility. This project investigates whether a novel pruning paradigm, targeting large activations, can improve model robustness by mitigating the influence of noisy or overconfident feature representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that selectively pruning large-magnitude activations, rather than small ones, will causally improve an LLM's robustness to adversarial and distributional shifts by reducing its reliance on brittle, high-variance features.
proposals.method: We will adapt causal mediation analysis frameworks to identify layers and neurons whose activations are most predictive of error on robustness benchmarks (e.g., AdvGLUE, MedQA perturbations). We will then implement and compare two structured pruning strategies: (1) a novel *high-magnitude thresholding* method that zeroes out activations above a learned percentile, and (2) a baseline low-magnitude method. Evaluation will use established robustness metrics (accuracy under perturbation, consistency) and causal metrics (average treatment effect of pruning on error) across general and medical-domain LLMs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, interpretable technique for improving LLM robustness via activation-level intervention. It will provide empirical evidence on the role of activation magnitude in model fragility and deliver a unified benchmarking framework for evaluating pruning strategies on robustness, complementing existing efficiency-focused benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While activation pruning is well-studied for efficiency, applying it preferentially to *large* activations for robustness is a counter-intuitive, unexplored direction. This work uniquely combines causal analysis with magnitude-based pruning to directly target the mechanistic causes of model failure.

proposals.title: Compensatory Scaling for Pruned LLMs: A Layer-Adaptive and Input-Dependent Recovery Framework
proposals.motivation: Network pruning is a key technique for efficient LLM deployment, but post-pruning recovery often relies on a uniform scaling factor or global fine-tuning, which fails to account for the heterogeneous sensitivity of different layers and the varying impact of pruning on different input types. This one-size-fits-all approach can lead to suboptimal performance recovery, especially in safety-critical domains like medicine where model reliability is paramount. A more nuanced, adaptive recovery mechanism is needed to preserve model capabilities after compression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that learning layer-specific or input-dependent compensatory scaling factors after pruning will more effectively recover model performance than uniform scaling, by dynamically adjusting the contribution of each layer's residual signal based on its pruning-induced damage and the semantic context of the input.
proposals.method: We will develop a lightweight recovery module that attaches small, trainable scaling networks (e.g., MLPs or hypernetworks) to pruned layers. For layer-specific scaling, we will learn a static vector of scaling coefficients per layer via a short, targeted fine-tuning on a diverse calibration set. For input-dependent scaling, the scaling networks will take a compressed representation of the input (e.g., CLS token or pooled features) and generate dynamic scaling factors per layer per forward pass. We will evaluate on standard pruning benchmarks (e.g., LLM-Pruner, Wanda) and medical QA datasets, using causal mediation analysis to interpret the learned scaling patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, parameter-efficient framework for post-pruning recovery that moves beyond uniform scaling. It will yield new insights into layer-wise robustness and input-dependent computation in compressed LLMs. The resulting methods will enhance the practicality of pruning for real-world applications, including medical AI, by improving the fidelity of compressed models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work learns static pruning masks or globally fine-tunes, our approach novelly treats the scaling factor as a learnable, context-aware function to compensate for pruning damage, bridging ideas from model editing (e.g., fact correction) and efficient inference.

proposals.title: Adaptive Sparse Adversarial Pruning: Learning to Schedule Pruning Probability for Robust and Efficient LLMs
proposals.motivation: Sparse Adversarial Pruning (SAP) is a promising defense against adversarial attacks on Large Language Models (LLMs), but its fixed pruning probability schedule creates a brittle trade-off between robustness and clean-task performance. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in high-stakes, adversarial domains like medical question-answering, we need more adaptive, context-aware defenses that can optimize this trade-off dynamically.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adaptive, learned schedule for SAP's pruning probability, conditioned on model confidence and input characteristics, will yield a superior Pareto frontier in the robustness-performance trade-off compared to static or heuristic schedules.
proposals.method: We will treat the pruning probability as a policy to be learned. Using a causal framework, we will model the effect of pruning decisions on both adversarial loss and clean-task loss. We will train a lightweight, auxiliary controller network (e.g., a small MLP or LSTM) to output a per-layer or per-token pruning probability. This controller will be optimized via meta-learning or reinforcement learning, using a reward function that balances accuracy on a clean validation set (e.g., MedQA) and robustness against adversarial benchmarks (e.g., AdvGLUE).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, adaptive algorithm for adversarial pruning that improves the Pareto-optimal frontier for LLM robustness, 2) a new unified evaluation benchmark for adaptive defense methods, extending frameworks like OpenUnlearning, and 3) empirical analysis in medical and general domains, providing insights into how defense mechanisms should vary with input uncertainty and task.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on SAP uses static schedules, and unlearning research explores adaptive forgetting, this is the first to formulate the pruning defense schedule as a learned policy, directly optimizing the core trade-off via modern meta-learning techniques tailored for LLM security.

proposals.title: Architectural Agnosticism in Unlearning: A Causal Evaluation of SAP for ResNets, DenseNets, and LSTMs
proposals.motivation: The Strong Asymptotic Preservation (SAP) property provides a rigorous, causal framework for evaluating unlearning algorithms in LLMs, ensuring they remove specific data influences without degrading general performance. However, its application is largely confined to transformer-based architectures, leaving a critical gap. Extending SAP evaluation to foundational architectures like ResNets and RNNs is essential for establishing a unified theory of machine unlearning across deep learning, with direct implications for medical and multimodal models built on these backbones.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the SAP property can be meaningfully defined and measured for convolutional (ResNet, DenseNet) and recurrent (LSTM) networks, and that existing unlearning methods will exhibit architecture-dependent performance gaps when evaluated under this causal framework.
proposals.method: We will adapt the SAP evaluation protocol, which uses causal graphs to model data influence, to non-transformer architectures. First, we will define the relevant causal variables (e.g., specific training subsets, architectural components) for ResNet-50, DenseNet-121, and LSTM networks on benchmark vision (CIFAR-10/100) and text (IMDb) tasks. We will then rigorously evaluate established unlearning methods (e.g., gradient ascent, Fisher forgetting) using SAP-compliant metrics, measuring both forget quality and model utility. The analysis will isolate how architectural features like skip connections or recurrent gates affect unlearning efficacy.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first cross-architectural benchmark for causal unlearning, extending the rigorous SAP framework beyond LLMs. It will yield practical insights for deploying unlearning in heterogeneous, real-world systems—such as medical teams using a mix of vision and language models—where data compliance is critical. The findings will inform the design of more robust, architecture-aware unlearning algorithms.
proposals.novelty: While SAP is established for transformers, its application to and systematic evaluation across fundamentally different network families (CNNs, RNNs) is novel. This moves beyond benchmarking methods on a single architecture to testing the generalizability of the unlearning evaluation theory itself.

proposals.title: Adversarial Robustness of Stochastic Activation Pruning: A Causal Framework for Adaptive Attack and Defense
proposals.motivation: Stochastic Activation Pruning (SAP) is a promising defense against adversarial examples in deep learning, but its security is evaluated under naive, static attacks. In real-world safety-critical applications like medical AI, adversaries will adapt their strategies to known defenses. Pre-2026, there is a critical gap in understanding and hardening SAP against adaptive adversaries who specifically target its stochastic mechanism, which is essential for trustworthy model deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current SAP implementations are vulnerable to adaptive attacks that exploit the statistical properties of the pruning process, and that a causally-informed, adversarial training regimen using these adaptive attacks can significantly improve SAP's certified robustness without sacrificing clean accuracy.
proposals.method: We will first design adaptive attacks that treat SAP's pruning decisions as a stochastic process, using gradient estimation techniques (e.g., Expectation Over Transformation) to craft effective perturbations. We will then develop a causal graph of the attack-defense interaction to identify robust training objectives. Finally, we will integrate these adaptive attacks into a novel adversarial training loop, where the model learns to be robust to the worst-case pruning-aware perturbations, evaluated using pre-2026 robustness metrics on vision-language models in a medical domain (e.g., chest X-ray report generation).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) the first benchmark for adaptive attacks on SAP, extending unlearning evaluation frameworks to robustness; 2) a new, more robust variant of SAP hardened via causal, adaptive adversarial training; and 3) empirical evidence on the trade-offs in medical VLMs, informing safer real-world adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While SAP and adaptive attacks are known separately, their intersection is unexplored. The novelty lies in a causal, adversarial training framework specifically designed to defend against attacks that strategically exploit stochasticity, moving beyond static robustness evaluations.

proposals.title: Certified Unlearning: Provable Robustness Guarantees for Stochastic Machine Unlearning via Randomized Smoothing
proposals.motivation: Machine unlearning methods, especially for LLMs, are increasingly stochastic (e.g., via noise injection or retraining on perturbed data) to balance privacy, utility, and efficiency. However, as highlighted in recent benchmarking efforts like OpenUnlearning, there is a critical lack of formal guarantees for these stochastic defenses against adversarial attempts to recover 'forgotten' data. This gap is particularly acute in sensitive domains like medicine, where unlearning failures can have severe consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that randomized smoothing, a technique for certifying the robustness of classifiers against adversarial perturbations, can be adapted to provide the first certified robustness guarantees for stochastic unlearning algorithms, bounding the probability that an adversary can infer the membership or attributes of unlearned data points.
proposals.method: We will formalize stochastic unlearning algorithms (e.g., gradient perturbation, noisy retraining) as smoothed classifiers. By analyzing the noise distribution applied during the unlearning process, we will derive certified radii in a metric space (e.g., embedding space) within which the model's output distribution is guaranteed not to change significantly if the unlearning was successful. We will implement this framework, building on causal evaluation frameworks from prior work, to certify existing SAP-like methods on benchmarks derived from medical text and general language tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first framework for certifying the robustness of machine unlearning algorithms, moving evaluation beyond empirical metrics to provable guarantees. It will deliver concrete, auditable certificates for unlearning systems in high-stakes domains, directly addressing the verification challenge highlighted in works like GenAudit and medical adaptation studies.
proposals.novelty: While randomized smoothing is established for adversarial robustness, its application to certify the security of stochastic *unlearning* procedures is novel. This bridges the theory of certified robustness with the practical need for auditable and trustworthy data removal, a core challenge in responsible AI deployment.

proposals.title: Randomized Unlearning: Probabilistic Guarantees for Machine Unlearning via Algorithmic Stability
proposals.motivation: Machine unlearning is critical for privacy, safety, and regulatory compliance in LLMs, but current methods lack rigorous, quantifiable guarantees against adversarial reconstruction or inference attacks. Pre-2026 benchmarks like OpenUnlearning focus on empirical metrics, but a formal, probabilistic framework for bounding an adversary's success is missing. This gap limits trust in deploying unlearning for sensitive domains like medicine, where models trained on patient data must reliably forget specific information.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing unlearning algorithms as randomized procedures, we can apply tools from algorithmic stability and concentration of measure to derive formal, data-dependent upper bounds on the probability that any adversary can successfully distinguish an unlearned model from one never trained on the forgotten data.
proposals.method: We will model the unlearning process (e.g., fine-tuning on remaining data, gradient ascent) as a randomized algorithm where the randomness stems from noise injection, data subsampling, or parameter perturbation. We will adapt stability definitions (e.g., hypothesis stability, uniform stability) to the unlearning setting and use concentration inequalities (e.g., McDiarmid's, Azuma's) to bound the divergence in adversary success probability. This theoretical framework will be instantiated and empirically validated on the OpenUnlearning benchmark, measuring bound tightness against practical membership inference and reconstruction attacks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first theoretical framework for deriving certifiable, probabilistic safety guarantees in machine unlearning, moving beyond heuristic evaluation. It will yield a new evaluation metric—adversarial success probability bounds—that complements existing empirical benchmarks, offering a principled way to audit and compare unlearning methods for high-stakes applications.
proposals.novelty: While randomized algorithms are well-studied for differential privacy, their application to derive *certifiable bounds for unlearning* is novel, bridging theoretical computer science and practical LLM safety. This approach moves from measuring average-case forgetfulness to providing worst-case probabilistic guarantees against a strategic adversary.

proposals.title: Scaling Self-Attention Pathways (SAP) for Efficient Unlearning in Very Deep Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: The rise of large vision-language models (VLMs) in medical and general domains necessitates efficient mechanisms for data removal, or unlearning, to address privacy, copyright, and factual error correction. Current unlearning methods for very deep networks are computationally expensive and can degrade model performance. The Self-Attention Pathways (SAP) framework, which isolates and modifies specific attention heads for unlearning, offers a promising but untested approach for scaling to modern, deep architectures like LLaVA or ViT-L/14.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the SAP framework can be effectively scaled to very deep networks, achieving more parameter-efficient and performance-preserving unlearning compared to full-model fine-tuning or gradient ascent methods, particularly when correcting factual errors or removing sensitive concepts.
proposals.method: We will adapt the SAP framework to deep VLMs by developing a systematic method to identify critical, layer-dependent attention pathways associated with target concepts, building on causal mediation analysis techniques from pre-2026 literature. We will implement and evaluate two scaling strategies: (1) a depth-aware gating mechanism that selectively applies SAP interventions across layers, and (2) a compressed pathway representation for high-dimensional attention spaces. Evaluation will use unified benchmarks from OpenUnlearning and medical adaptation tasks, measuring unlearning efficacy, general performance retention, and computational cost.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous evaluation of pathway-based unlearning in state-of-the-art deep networks, delivering a scalable, practical tool for model editing. It will contribute new metrics for assessing the efficiency-accuracy trade-off in deep model unlearning and offer insights into the modularity of knowledge representation across network depth.
proposals.novelty: While SAP has been demonstrated in shallower models, its application and necessary adaptations for very deep networks (e.g., >50 layers) remain unexplored. The novel integration of causal pathway analysis with scalable gating mechanisms specifically for unlearning in VLMs addresses a clear gap in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Unlearning for the Edge: Applying Selective Amnesic Processing to Improve Robustness and Efficiency in Tiny Mobile Networks
proposals.motivation: The deployment of lightweight models on edge devices is critical for privacy-preserving and real-time applications, but these models are vulnerable to data poisoning, memorization of sensitive information, and distribution shifts. Pre-2026, the field of machine unlearning has focused on large models, leaving a gap in principled methods for tiny networks where computational and memory constraints are severe. This work addresses the need for efficient, verifiable model adaptation directly on resource-limited hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying Selective Amnesic Processing (SAP) to tiny mobile networks will enable efficient, verifiable unlearning of specific data points or concepts, thereby improving model robustness against adversarial examples and distribution shifts without significant degradation in general task performance.
proposals.method: We will adapt the SAP framework, which uses influence functions to identify and perturb weights critical to forgetting, for highly compressed architectures like MobileNetV3 and EfficientNet-Lite. The core technical challenge is approximating influence efficiently in low-rank parameter spaces. We will develop a causal evaluation suite, inspired by GenAudit, to measure unlearning efficacy (via membership inference and retraining gap metrics), robustness (via adversarial attack success rate), and on-device efficiency (latency, memory, energy). Experiments will use medical imaging datasets (e.g., CheXpert) to test unlearning of specific patient cohorts or spurious correlations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous benchmark for unlearning in tiny networks, establishing causal links between parameter perturbation, forgetting verification, and downstream robustness. It will deliver a practical toolkit for adapting edge models post-deployment, directly contributing to safer and more adaptable on-device AI, particularly in sensitive domains like mobile health.
proposals.novelty: While unlearning is studied in LLMs, its application to constrained, non-generative vision models for edge devices is unexplored. The novelty lies in bridging the causal, evaluation-focused methodology from LLM unlearning (e.g., OpenUnlearning) with the architectural and efficiency demands of tiny networks for tangible robustness gains.

proposals.title: Dual: Training Language Models for Robustness to SAP Inference-Time Perturbations
proposals.motivation: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes, multi-agent settings like medical teaming, where their outputs may be perturbed by other agents (e.g., via Self-Ask Prompting or SAP). Pre-2026 work on LLM robustness focuses on adversarial attacks or distribution shifts, but not on structured, inference-time perturbations from cooperative agents. This creates a critical reliability gap for LLMs acting as team members.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LLMs can be trained from scratch to be inherently robust to SAP-style perturbations, and that this robustness will generalize better than post-hoc fine-tuning methods, leading to more reliable performance in collaborative, multi-turn interactions.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel training paradigm where the model is exposed to SAP perturbations during pre-training or continued pre-training. Using a causal framework, we will synthesize plausible SAP perturbations (e.g., inserted clarifying questions, intermediate reasoning steps) and treat them as a form of structured data augmentation. We will adapt evaluation metrics from unlearning and factual correction benchmarks (e.g., GenAudit) to measure robustness as the consistency of final answers before and after perturbation, moving beyond simple accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first method for building SAP-robust LLMs from the ground up and a new benchmark for evaluating multi-agent robustness. It will contribute a principled, causal perspective on training for inference-time stability, directly impacting the reliability of LLMs in collaborative domains like healthcare.
proposals.novelty: While data augmentation and adversarial training are well-known, applying them specifically to model the structured, cooperative perturbations of SAP during foundational training is novel. This shifts the focus from defending against malicious attacks to ensuring stability within a benign but interactive teaming pipeline.

proposals.title: ChannelDrop: Stochastic Channel Pruning for Efficient and Robust LLM Unlearning
proposals.motivation: Current methods for unlearning in Large Language Models (LLMs) often rely on fine-tuning or gradient-based updates, which are computationally expensive and risk catastrophic forgetting or leaving residual traces of the 'forgotten' data. A more efficient, structured approach to model editing is needed. Pruning, a proven technique for model compression, offers a promising but underexplored avenue for targeted removal of knowledge, particularly if applied at a semantically meaningful scale.
proposals.hypothesis: Stochastically pruning entire feature map channels, rather than individual activations, during an unlearning procedure will more effectively and efficiently remove specific factual knowledge while better preserving the model's general capabilities compared to existing fine-tuning-based unlearning methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'Medical Adaptation of LLMs' evaluation framework to create a controlled unlearning benchmark, targeting specific factual associations (e.g., medical facts, biographical details). We will develop 'ChannelDrop,' a method that applies a stochastic mask over the output channels of key transformer feed-forward layers during unlearning training, guided by a causal attribution metric (inspired by GenAudit) to identify channels most implicated in generating the target knowledge. The process will be evaluated using the rigorous benchmarking principles from OpenUnlearning, measuring forget quality, model utility, and resilience to extraction attacks.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, hardware-efficient pruning paradigm for LLM unlearning, directly linking model sparsity to knowledge removal. It will provide new insights into how knowledge is structurally encoded in LLMs. The resulting benchmark and ChannelDrop method will offer the community a faster, more robust alternative to gradient-based unlearning.
proposals.novelty: While activation pruning exists for efficiency, applying stochastic channel-level pruning as a core mechanism for *unlearning* is novel. This approach moves beyond weight updates to a structural, inference-time alteration, creating a direct link between model sparsity and specific knowledge erasure not explored in prior unlearning literature.

proposals.title: Unlearning the Seed: A Causal Framework for Zero-Shot Active Learning with Large Language Models
