A bottleneck layer will be inserted after a key transformer block, trained with a Lagrangian to minimize mutual information between the input and the bottlenecked features while maximizing information relevant for a downstream diagnostic task. We will evaluate using causal mediation analysis to measure feature attribution changes and benchmark unlearning efficacy on a curated medical benchmark (inspired by OpenUnlearning) where specific patient identifiers or biased visual cues must be removed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel method for feature-level control in VLMs grounded in information theory, 2) new metrics for evaluating representation purity and unlearning completeness in medical AI, and 3) empirical evidence on the trade-offs between compression, task performance, and unlearning efficiency in complex models.
proposals.novelty: While IB has been applied to entire neural networks, its explicit application to *local* features within massive pre-trained VLMs for the dual purpose of robustness and unlearning is unexplored. This bridges Zachary Lipton's work on unlearning/auditing and medical adaptation with a foundational theory of representation learning.

proposals.title: Contrastive Feature Disentanglement for Robust and Interpretable Medical AI Teaming
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) adapted for medicine often rely on spurious correlations (e.g., scanner artifacts, hospital-specific note templates) that degrade robustness across institutions and create opaque 'shortcuts' that undermine clinician trust. This is a critical barrier to safe human-AI teaming in healthcare, where models must provide reliable, invariant reasoning. Current adaptation and unlearning methods lack principled techniques to explicitly separate these spurious features from core causal medical concepts.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying supervised and counterfactual contrastive losses during adaptation, we can disentangle latent representations of LLMs, isolating invariant clinical semantics from spurious local features, thereby improving cross-site robustness and interpretability.
proposals.method: We will adapt a clinical LLM (e.g., PMC-LLaMA, BioBERT) using a multi-task contrastive framework. A primary supervised contrastive loss will cluster representations of semantically similar medical concepts (e.g., 'pneumonia') across different hospitals. A secondary counterfactual contrastive loss, informed by causal graphs of potential spurious features (e.g., institution ID), will push representations of the same clinical label with different spurious attributes apart. We will evaluate disentanglement using metrics from the OpenUnlearning benchmark and measure robustness on held-out medical datasets from novel institutions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel adaptation technique that directly targets feature disentanglement for medical AI, moving beyond mere performance metrics. It will yield a new evaluation suite for measuring spurious correlation removal in clinical models and offer a more interpretable representation for human-AI teaming by surfacing the invariant features driving model decisions.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established, its targeted application for *disentangling* spurious vs. invariant features in medical LLM adaptation is novel. This approach uniquely bridges causal reasoning (to define spurious attributes) with representation learning to achieve unlearning-like effects without requiring explicit forgetting of training data.

proposals.title: Causal Domain Invariance for Robust Medical LLM Unlearning
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) adapted for medical applications often learn spurious correlations from training data, such as hospital-specific documentation styles or local prevalence rates, which harm robustness and fairness across diverse clinical settings. Current unlearning and adaptation methods, while effective at removing explicit factual errors, struggle to identify and remove these deeper, domain-specific feature dependencies that lead to poor generalization. This creates a critical gap in deploying reliable, equitable AI for healthcare.
proposals.hypothesis: By formalizing domain-specific spurious correlations as confounding variables within a causal inference framework, we can develop targeted unlearning algorithms that systematically remove these dependencies, leading to LLMs with improved causal robustness and out-of-domain generalization in medical tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the causal framework of Invariant Risk Minimization (IRM) and backdoor adjustment to model medical text generation. First, we will use causal discovery techniques on multi-source medical datasets (e.g., MIMIC, CheXpert) to identify potential confounders (e.g., patient demographics correlated with hospital-specific coding practices). We will then develop a novel unlearning loss function that penalizes the model's dependence on these identified confounders, integrating it with gradient-based unlearning methods. The approach will be evaluated using the OpenUnlearning benchmark framework, extended with causal robustness metrics that measure performance stability across simulated domain shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, causality-informed algorithm for robust medical LLM unlearning and a corresponding evaluation benchmark for causal robustness. It will provide empirical evidence on whether causal framing improves generalization over standard unlearning in medical NLP, directly addressing the 'progress' question in medical adaptation raised in prior work. The resulting models should demonstrate reduced bias and more reliable performance across heterogeneous clinical environments.
proposals.novelty: While causal inference has been applied to vision and tabular data for robustness, its integration into LLM unlearning—particularly for removing implicit, domain-specific textual confounders in medicine—is novel. This moves beyond auditing factual errors to structurally editing the model's reliance on spurious feature dependencies.

proposals.title: Spectral Unlearning: Frequency-Domain Regularization for Targeted Forgetting in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current LLM unlearning methods, such as gradient ascent or fine-tuning on counterexamples, often degrade general performance or fail to fully erase specific knowledge. This is a critical issue for medical and factual error correction, where models must forget incorrect or private information without harming their overall reasoning capabilities. A more targeted, mechanistic approach to unlearning is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying spectral regularization to dampen high-frequency components in the feature representations of a target concept will enable more precise and effective unlearning, minimizing interference with unrelated model knowledge compared to existing parameter-space methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt spectral regularization techniques from computer vision (e.g., Fourier feature filtering) for transformer-based LLMs. The approach involves: 1) Extracting intermediate feature activations for prompts related to the target 'forget' set. 2) Computing their frequency-domain representations via a learned transformation (e.g., DCT or learned basis). 3) Applying a regularization loss that penalizes the energy of high-frequency components in these features during unlearning fine-tuning. We will evaluate this method against baselines from OpenUnlearning on factual unlearning and medical QA benchmarks, using causal metrics to isolate the effect on forgotten vs. retained knowledge.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, theoretically grounded technique for LLM unlearning that operates directly on feature representations. It will contribute a new benchmark result for precision unlearning and offer insights into the frequency-domain structure of learned knowledge in LLMs, potentially informing more robust model editing and auditing tools like GenAudit.
proposals.novelty: While spectral regularization is known for improving generalization in CNNs, its application to the discrete, high-dimensional feature spaces of LLMs for the specific goal of targeted unlearning is unexplored. This represents a shift from parameter-space to representation-space interventions for model editing.

proposals.title: Penalized Efficiency: A Causal Framework for Robustness-Aware Pruning of Mobile Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Lightweight mobile networks, especially vision-language models (VLMs), are critical for on-device medical applications (e.g., diagnostic assistance). However, aggressive compression for efficiency often catastrophically degrades robustness to distribution shifts and adversarial inputs, creating safety risks. Current methods lack a principled way to trade off parameter efficiency against specific robustness guarantees, which is essential for reliable deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a causal, penalty-based robustness objective directly into the network pruning process will yield lightweight models that maintain significantly higher out-of-distribution (OOD) and adversarial robustness compared to standard efficiency-first pruning, with minimal parameter overhead.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend penalty-based unlearning frameworks (inspired by Lipton's work on GenAudit and OpenUnlearning) to the model compression domain. For a mobile VLM (e.g., a pruned version of BLIP or LLaVA), we will formulate a joint optimization objective that combines a standard task loss, a sparsity penalty, and a novel robustness penalty. This penalty will be defined using causal contrastive metrics—measuring the model's sensitivity to semantically invariant perturbations—and will be applied during iterative magnitude pruning or during a post-pruning recovery phase. We will benchmark against standard pruning baselines on medical VLM adaptation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, evaluation-driven framework for robustness-aware compression of mobile VLMs, 2) new causal metrics for quantifying robustness-efficiency trade-offs, and 3) empirical evidence on the feasibility of 'hardening' efficient networks for sensitive domains like healthcare, directly contributing to safer on-device AI.
proposals.novelty: While penalty methods exist for unlearning in LLMs and pruning is well-studied, applying a causal, audit-inspired penalty to explicitly preserve robustness during compression of mobile VLMs is a novel synthesis. It moves beyond treating robustness as an afterthought or requiring expensive retraining.

proposals.title: Unlearning Spurious Motion: A Local Predictive Power Penalty for Temporal Robustness in Video Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Video foundation models often rely on transient, local motion cues (e.g., camera jitter, background flicker) that correlate with but do not causally explain high-level actions or events, leading to poor generalization and spurious correlations. This is a critical issue in medical video analysis (e.g., surgical phase recognition) where models must learn invariant physiological dynamics, not incidental visual noise. Extending unlearning principles from language to the spatiotemporal domain is a necessary step toward robust, causally-aware multimodal AI.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a local predictive power penalty—originally designed for LLMs to discard superficial token correlations—to the spatiotemporal patches of a video transformer will force the model to discard transient motion features and rely on more globally consistent, semantically meaningful temporal dynamics, thereby improving out-of-distribution robustness.
proposals.method: We will adapt the local predictive power penalty framework to video by treating short sequences of spatiotemporal patches as 'local contexts.' For a video transformer (e.g., a ViViT or TimeSformer backbone), we will compute the penalty by measuring how well a lightweight probe can predict the target (e.g., action class) from a restricted set of early-layer embeddings representing local motion. During training, we will maximize the main task loss while minimizing this local predictability, using a gradient ascent/descent scheme. We will benchmark on datasets with known spurious temporal correlations (e.g., biased action recognition datasets) and medical video time-series.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal framework and benchmark for unlearning spurious temporal correlations in video models, extending the theory of unlearning beyond static data. It will deliver a practical method to improve the causal robustness of video foundation models, with validated applications in medical video analysis where reliability is paramount. The resulting metrics and evaluation suite will advance the field of multimodal model auditing.
proposals.novelty: While unlearning has been studied in LLMs, its principled application to the video domain to discard spurious *temporal* features—rather than static visual or factual associations—is novel. The technical adaptation of the local predictive power penalty to spatiotemporal patches presents a non-trivial extension of core unlearning theory.

proposals.title: Dual-Utility Adaptation: Maximizing Local Predictive Power for Domain-Sensitive Feature Unlearning in Medical LLMs
proposals.motivation: Current LLM adaptation for medicine often prioritizes general-purpose knowledge retention, which can dilute domain-specific predictive signals and embed spurious correlations from the pre-training corpus. This is problematic for clinical decision support, where local, evidence-based feature utility is paramount. The inverse problem—explicitly maximizing a model's predictive power on a target domain—remains underexplored as a principled method for creating robust, domain-sensitive representations.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating a dual-objective optimization that maximizes predictive accuracy on a target medical task while simultaneously minimizing reliance on features predictive only in the source (general) domain, we can induce a feature representation that is both highly performant and selectively 'unlearned' of broader, task-irrelevant associations.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal adaptation framework. A base clinical LLM (e.g., Med-PaLM or a tuned variant) will be adapted using a multi-term loss: a primary term for target task accuracy (e.g., diagnostic code prediction), and a counterfactual regularization term that penalizes features whose influence, estimated via causal mediation analysis or gradient-based attribution, is high on a held-out general-domain task but low on the target task. This will be benchmarked against standard fine-tuning and unlearning baselines using metrics from OpenUnlearning and evaluation suites from medical adaptation studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, evaluation-driven method for creating domain-specialized models that are not just fine-tuned but structurally aligned with local predictive utility. It will contribute a new benchmark for assessing 'domain sensitivity' in medical AI and a practical technique for teams to build more reliable, evidence-grounded clinical assistants, directly extending the GenAudit goal of factual correctness.
proposals.novelty: It reframes unlearning not as mere forgetting for privacy or correctness, but as a positive, optimization-driven process to sculpt feature spaces for local predictive power, integrating causal reasoning with adaptation in a novel dual-objective setup.

proposals.title: Multi-Task Unlearning: A Layer-Wise Auxiliary Objective Framework for Efficient and Selective Forgetting in LLMs
proposals.motivation: Current LLM unlearning methods, often applied as a single penalty at the output layer, struggle with efficiency and specificity, risking catastrophic forgetting of unrelated knowledge. This is particularly critical in sensitive domains like medicine, where models must forget specific, potentially harmful information while preserving general diagnostic reasoning. A more granular, multi-objective approach is needed to achieve precise, scalable unlearning.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying distinct auxiliary unlearning objectives (e.g., for factual errors, privacy, toxicity) at targeted intermediate layers of an LLM, rather than a single global penalty, will enable more efficient and selective forgetting with less degradation of retained model capabilities.
proposals.method: We will extend gradient-based unlearning methods (e.g., gradient ascent, KL divergence penalties) by designing a suite of layer-specific auxiliary loss functions. Inspired by causal mediation analysis, we will first identify key transformer layers most responsible for specific knowledge types (e.g., factual recall vs. reasoning). We will then apply tailored penalties—such as a contrastive loss for factual errors at middle layers and a distribution-matching loss for privacy at later layers—enabling multi-task unlearning in a single, coordinated fine-tuning run.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, scalable framework for multi-faceted unlearning, advancing beyond single-objective benchmarks. It will deliver a new evaluation suite to measure task-specific forgetting efficacy and a causal understanding of how knowledge is distributed and can be selectively removed across model layers.
proposals.novelty: While auxiliary objectives are common in multi-task learning, their strategic application for *multi-task unlearning* at diagnostically identified layers is novel. This moves beyond treating the model as a black box, using causal insights to make the unlearning process more surgical and composable.

proposals.title: Unlearning by Design: A Local Predictive Penalty for Robust and Forgettable Self-Supervised Representations
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised learning (SSL) objectives like contrastive loss optimize for invariant, general-purpose representations. However, this can inadvertently cause models to memorize spurious correlations and sensitive data, creating significant challenges for safety, privacy, and model editing (unlearning). Pre-2026, unlearning methods are largely applied post-hoc to trained models, which is inefficient and can degrade performance. A foundational approach that builds forgettability and robustness into the pre-training objective is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating a local predictive penalty—a regularization term that penalizes sensitivity to small, localized perturbations in the input space—into SSL frameworks will yield representations that are both highly informative for downstream tasks and inherently more amenable to targeted unlearning, without sacrificing overall model utility.
proposals.method: We will extend standard contrastive frameworks (e.g., SimCLR, MoCo) by augmenting the InfoNCE loss with a novel local predictive penalty. For a given anchor embedding, we will generate a perturbed variant via a localized adversarial attack or data augmentation. The penalty will minimize the predictive certainty (e.g., via entropy maximization) of the model for distinguishing the anchor from its perturbed counterpart within the projection head. We will implement this in vision (ResNet) and language (BERT) transformer architectures, benchmarking on curated datasets containing known spurious features and sensitive attributes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A novel, integrated SSL objective that proactively mitigates memorization of undesirable patterns. 2) A rigorous, causal-inspired evaluation framework to measure the 'unlearnability' and robustness of pre-trained representations. 3) Empirical evidence on whether designing for forgettability from the outset is more effective than post-hoc unlearning techniques.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial regularization exists, applying it as a *local predictive penalty* specifically to shape the SSL latent space for unlearning is novel. It moves beyond post-hoc corrections to embed the principle of 'right to be forgotten' directly into the foundational learning process.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Concept Drift Detection for Language Models via Self-Referential Divergence
proposals.motivation: In real-world deployments of LLMs, such as in medical applications or teaming environments, data distributions shift over time due to evolving knowledge, user behavior, or policy changes. Current drift detection methods require a static, clean reference dataset, which is often unavailable or expensive to maintain, creating a critical bottleneck for reliable model monitoring and triggering unlearning or adaptation procedures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intrinsic properties of a language model's internal representations and output distributions over sequential data slices can be leveraged to construct a self-referential baseline, enabling robust detection of concept drift without an external static reference set.
proposals.method: We will develop a method that treats the model's own recent historical outputs as a pseudo-reference, applying causal inference techniques to isolate drift from natural variation. We will adapt metrics from unlearning evaluation (e.g., from OpenUnlearning) to measure divergence in factual consistency (inspired by GenAudit) and feature-space statistics across time. The core algorithm will involve constructing a dynamic, rolling reference from model checkpoints or inference logs and using statistical process control (e.g., CUSUM) on a composite divergence score.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework for continuous monitoring of deployed LLMs in dynamic settings like healthcare. It will contribute a novel benchmark for unsupervised drift detection and offer a methodological bridge between the fields of unlearning evaluation and model adaptation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that assumes a fixed gold-standard dataset, our method is entirely self-referential, eliminating the need for held-out reference data. It uniquely combines causal reasoning for attribution with established unlearning metrics to distinguish harmful drift from benign variation.

proposals.title: Temporal Unlearning: Detecting Distribution Shifts in LLMs Under Non-I.I.D. Clinical Time Series
proposals.motivation: In medical AI, models are often trained and evaluated on clinical time-series data (e.g., patient vitals, notes) under unrealistic i.i.d. assumptions. Real-world deployment involves complex temporal dependencies (e.g., disease progression, policy changes) that can cause silent performance degradation. Current unlearning and auditing benchmarks, like OpenUnlearning and GenAudit, lack formal methods to detect when a model's knowledge has become misaligned due to these temporal shifts, posing a critical risk to safe clinical teaming with LLMs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the non-i.i.d. temporal structure of reference data, we can develop more sensitive and specific statistical tests to detect when an LLM's outputs on a test stream have significantly deviated from its expected behavior, even in the presence of autocorrelation and seasonality.
proposals.method: We will extend the benchmarking framework from OpenUnlearning to time-series settings. Using real-world clinical datasets (e.g., MIMIC-IV), we will simulate temporal shifts (concept drift, covariate shift). Our core technical approach adapts causal change-point detection methods (e.g., CUSUM, Bayesian online changepoint detection) to operate on sequences of LLM-generated outputs or internal representations, rather than raw data. We will develop novel metrics that account for temporal dependencies by comparing the test stream's properties against a null model fitted to the non-i.i.d. reference batch.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A novel benchmark for evaluating unlearning and model auditing methods under realistic temporal dependencies. 2) New, theoretically-grounded detection algorithms that relax the i.i.d. assumption for model monitoring. 3) Empirical validation on medical adaptation tasks, showing how failure to account for temporal structure leads to missed degradation.
proposals.novelty: While change-point detection is a classic time-series problem, its application to monitor the factual consistency and performance of large language models—by detecting shifts in their *output distributions* rather than the input data stream—is novel. This bridges causal temporal analysis with LLM evaluation and unlearning.

proposals.title: Unlearning Audit via Internal State Dynamics: Detecting Erasure in Non-Probabilistic and Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current methods for auditing machine unlearning or detecting factual errors rely heavily on model outputs, such as softmax probabilities or token likelihoods. This fails for modern vision-language models, sparse or non-autoregressive architectures, and models where output probabilities are intentionally masked for efficiency or privacy. A detection framework independent of final-layer outputs is crucial for evaluating unlearning in the diverse, real-world models used in medical and general teaming applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the causal effect of unlearning a specific concept is encoded in the dynamics of intermediate model activations and can be robustly detected using statistical and causal inference on internal representations, even when final-layer outputs are non-probabilistic or unavailable.
proposals.method: We will develop a suite of detection techniques based on Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) and Causal Mediation Analysis applied to internal model states. For a given 'forget' set, we will compare the distribution of activations in specific layers between the original and unlearned model using statistical distance metrics (e.g., Maximum Mean Discrepancy). We will adapt the TracIn (Training Influence) framework to estimate influence via gradient-based metrics computed on intermediate features rather than outputs. These methods will be validated on benchmarks from OpenUnlearning, including non-softmax models, and on medical VLMs adapted from models like Med-PaLM.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified framework for auditing unlearning and error detection that is agnostic to model output type, directly extending evaluation methodologies from LLMs to VLMs and efficient architectures. It will deliver open-source tools (extending GenAudit) for practitioners to verify unlearning claims in sensitive domains like healthcare, where model introspection is critical for trust and safety.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work focused on output distributions, our approach operationalizes unlearning as a measurable intervention in the model's internal causal pathways, enabling detection without reliance on softmax or prediction APIs. This shifts the audit paradigm from observing model behavior to analyzing its internal state changes.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Concept Shift Detection in High-Dimensional Medical Data via Causal Graph Inference and Sparse Autoencoders
proposals.motivation: Current methods for detecting dataset shift in machine learning, especially for safety-critical domains like medicine, rely on pre-trained feature extractors or low-dimensional representations, which can obscure subtle, clinically relevant concept shifts. This is a critical limitation for adapting large language and vision-language models to dynamic clinical environments, where raw data (e.g., EHR time-series, full-resolution images) contains complex, high-dimensional signals of evolving disease presentations or treatment protocols. A method that operates directly on raw data would enable more robust and timely model auditing and unlearning of outdated or harmful concepts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by learning a sparse, causal graph representation of the raw high-dimensional data manifold, we can isolate and detect concept shifts more reliably than methods using standard dimensionality reduction or pre-trained features, which may conflate nuisance variation with meaningful semantic change.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, a Sparse Causal Autoencoder (SCAE) will be trained to reconstruct raw input data (e.g., medical text tokens or image patches) while enforcing sparsity in its latent code via an L1 penalty and a learnable adjacency matrix representing causal dependencies between latent factors. Second, we will monitor for distributional shifts in these inferred sparse causal factors using non-parametric statistical tests (e.g., Maximum Mean Discrepancy) and custom metrics that quantify changes in the graph adjacency structure, directly linking shifts to interpretable data-generating mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel benchmark for evaluating shift detection methods on raw, high-dimensional medical data, extending the OpenUnlearning philosophy; 2) a new causal, sparsity-driven technique for unsupervised concept drift detection that provides interpretable indicators of *what* changed; 3) a foundation for more precise unlearning and audit interventions (à la GenAudit) in LLMs/VLMs by pinpointing corrupted data concepts at their origin.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating causal graph learning with sparse representation learning directly on raw data for shift detection, moving beyond correlation-based deep features or pre-defined ontologies. This addresses a core gap in adapting foundation models, where shifts in high-dimensional raw data streams are the rule, not the exception.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Distribution Shift Detection for Deployed Language Models via Causal Invariance and Self-Supervised Discrepancy
proposals.motivation: In real-world deployments, such as medical AI systems, data distributions shift over time, degrading model performance. Current shift detection methods require abundant labeled test data, which is costly and often unavailable in practice. This creates a critical need for robust, label-efficient detection to enable timely model adaptation or unlearning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging causal invariance in model representations and designing a self-supervised discrepancy metric, we can reliably detect distribution shifts using only unlabeled test data, even when the shift is subtle or affects only a subset of the data manifold.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will identify a stable, causally-invariant feature subspace from the model's internal representations using pre-2026 causal discovery tools (e.g., Invariant Risk Minimization principles). Second, we will design a novel self-supervised discrepancy score by training a lightweight diagnostic model to distinguish between original training data embeddings and test embeddings, using techniques like contrastive learning or Barlow Twins for representation alignment. Detection will be based on the divergence in this invariant subspace, calibrated against a null distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical toolkit for monitoring LLMs in label-scarce environments like healthcare, directly informing when unlearning or adaptation is necessary. It will contribute a new benchmark for unsupervised shift detection within the OpenUnlearning framework and advance evaluation methodologies for model reliability.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that relies on supervised probes or simple statistical tests on outputs, our method uniquely combines causal representation learning with self-supervised discrepancy estimation, targeting shifts that affect model reliability rather than just input statistics.

proposals.title: Beyond Covariate Shift: A Benchmark for Realistic and Adversarial Perturbations in Medical LLM Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Current evaluations of medical LLMs, including adaptation studies, primarily test performance on clean, curated datasets or simple covariate shifts. This fails to capture the complex, multi-faceted perturbations encountered in real-world clinical settings, such as adversarial rephrasing of queries, systematic documentation errors, or distribution shifts induced by new clinical protocols. A rigorous assessment of model robustness under these broader, realistic shifts is critical for safe deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art adapted medical LLMs will exhibit significant and previously unmeasured performance degradation under a taxonomy of realistic, structured perturbations, and that this degradation will be poorly correlated with their performance on standard benchmark covariate shifts.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel benchmark extending frameworks like OpenUnlearning and GenAudit to the medical domain. We will define a taxonomy of perturbation types: semantic (e.g., adversarial negation, jargon injection), distributional (e.g., synthetic demographic shifts, rare disease prevalence changes), and factual corruption (simulated EHR entry errors). Using causal graph frameworks to model perturbation structures, we will systematically apply these to established medical QA datasets (e.g., MedQA, PubMedQA) and evaluate a suite of adapted LLMs (e.g., Med-PaLM, BioBERT, generalist models fine-tuned on MIMIC). Metrics will go beyond accuracy to include robustness scores, consistency under perturbation, and error type analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating medical LLM robustness under realistic test-time shifts, moving beyond simple accuracy reporting. It will produce actionable failure modes and a diagnostic framework to guide future model development towards more reliable clinical AI. The benchmark and perturbation taxonomy will be released publicly to standardize robustness evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While dataset shift is a known problem, this proposal systematically operationalizes a broader, clinically-grounded taxonomy of perturbations beyond standard covariate shift, applying causal and adversarial auditing methods from general ML unlearning/auditing research specifically to the high-stakes medical LLM domain.

proposals.title: Detect and Adapt: A Closed-Loop Framework for Online Unlearning and Adaptation in Deployed LLMs
proposals.motivation: Deployed large language models (LLMs) face dynamic environments where data distributions shift, new facts emerge, or harmful outputs are discovered, necessitating continuous updates. Current approaches treat detection (e.g., of factual errors or performance drift) and model adaptation (e.g., unlearning, fine-tuning) as separate, offline processes. This decoupling creates latency, inefficiency, and vulnerability, especially in high-stakes domains like medicine where outdated or incorrect information can have serious consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating real-time shift detection with targeted, online adaptation mechanisms will create a more robust, efficient, and responsive system for maintaining LLM integrity than applying detection and adaptation sequentially or in isolation.
proposals.method: We will develop a closed-loop framework that couples a detection module, using causal metrics and statistical process control on model outputs (inspired by GenAudit and OpenUnlearning benchmarks), with an adaptation module. Upon detecting a shift (e.g., factual error clusters or performance degradation on a monitored metric), the system will trigger a targeted intervention. This intervention will employ efficient, online unlearning algorithms (e.g., gradient ascent on negative examples) or constrained continual fine-tuning, using a small, curated buffer of corrective data. The adaptation's effect will be continuously evaluated by the detection module, closing the loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a unified framework for online model maintenance, 2) new benchmarks for evaluating integrated detect-adapt systems under simulated temporal shifts, and 3) empirical evidence on the trade-offs between adaptation speed, specificity, and stability in LLMs. It directly advances the practical deployment of reliable, updatable AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While detection and adaptation are both active research areas, their tight integration into a single, automated online control system for LLMs is novel. This moves beyond benchmarking unlearning methods (OpenUnlearning) or fixing individual errors (GenAudit) to create a proactive, systemic solution for model governance.

proposals.title: Statistical Guarantees for Unlearning Detection: A Causal Framework for Model Auditing Under Distribution Shift
proposals.motivation: The rapid deployment of large language models (LLMs) in sensitive domains like medicine necessitates reliable methods to audit and enforce unlearning of harmful or private data. Current detection benchmarks, like those in OpenUnlearning, are empirical and lack formal guarantees, making it impossible to certify that a model has truly forgotten specific information, especially under real-world distribution shifts. This gap hinders trustworthy model governance and safe human-AI teaming in high-stakes settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing unlearning detection as a causal inference problem—distinguishing the causal effect of the unlearning intervention from spurious correlations—we can derive formal, non-asymptotic statistical guarantees for detection performance under defined shift conditions (e.g., covariate shift, concept drift).
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework modeling the unlearning process as an intervention on the model's training data distribution. Using tools from causal inference and statistical learning theory, we will analyze existing detection metrics (e.g., membership inference, efficacy, fidelity) and derive bounds on their Type I/II error rates. The analysis will explicitly condition on shift types, quantifying how detection power degrades with shift magnitude. We will validate our bounds empirically on medical LLM adaptation benchmarks, simulating shifts between source and deployment environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous statistical foundation for evaluating unlearning detection methods, moving beyond ad-hoc benchmarking. It will yield actionable guidelines for selecting and calibrating detection metrics under specific operational conditions, directly enhancing tools like GenAudit for reliable error correction. This advances the field towards certifiable model audits.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has empirically benchmarked unlearning, no pre-2026 research provides formal guarantees for detection under shift. The novelty lies in applying a causal lens to the unlearning audit problem to derive provable bounds, bridging statistical learning theory with practical LLM safety.

proposals.title: The Efficiency Frontier of Detection: A Computational Audit of Real-Time Methods for Unlearning and Fact-Checking in LLMs
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) in real-time, high-stakes domains like medical teaming and interactive dialogue requires robust detection methods for unlearned content and factual errors. While benchmarks like OpenUnlearning and tools like GenAudit evaluate detection accuracy, their computational costs are poorly characterized. This gap hinders practical system design, as resource-intensive methods are infeasible for latency-sensitive applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exists a significant and previously unquantified trade-off between the detection accuracy of state-of-the-art methods (e.g., for unlearning or factual error detection) and their computational efficiency, and that this trade-off is not monotonic—some high-accuracy methods may be surprisingly efficient.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic computational audit of detection methods from the unlearning (e.g., gradient-based, probability-based) and fact-checking (e.g., retrieval-augmented verification, consistency scoring) literature. Using a unified framework, we will measure per-query latency, GPU memory footprint, and FLOPs across controlled model scales (e.g., LLaMA-2 7B/13B) and dataset complexities. We will employ causal mediation analysis to isolate the contribution of specific architectural components (e.g., attention heads, MLP layers) to both detection performance and runtime cost.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive efficiency benchmark for detection tasks, providing practitioners with clear cost/accuracy Pareto frontiers to guide method selection. It will contribute a novel evaluation dimension to the OpenUnlearning and GenAudit frameworks and offer design principles for building efficient, real-time safe LLM systems, particularly for medical applications.
proposals.novelty: Prior work focuses on detection accuracy metrics; this study shifts the lens to computational efficiency as a first-class evaluation criterion, applying a rigorous causal audit methodology from Lipton's prior work to dissect the sources of inefficiency.

proposals.title: SPC-LLM: Statistical Process Control for Monitoring and Diagnosing Drift in Deployed Language Models
proposals.motivation: Deployed LLMs are subject to performance drift due to evolving data, user interactions, and unlearning interventions, yet current monitoring relies on costly, infrequent human audits. This is critical in high-stakes domains like medicine, where a model's reliability over time is paramount. There is a lack of lightweight, statistical frameworks for continuous, automated detection of distributional shifts in model behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts, adapted for high-dimensional LLM outputs, can provide a robust, interpretable, and computationally efficient method for detecting both abrupt and gradual performance drifts in real-time, enabling proactive model maintenance.
proposals.method: We will adapt univariate (e.g., Shewhart, CUSUM) and multivariate (e.g., Hotelling's T²) control charts to monitor key metrics derived from model predictions over time. For a deployed model, we will track metrics like prediction entropy, calibration error, or the output of a 'reference' model on a fixed diagnostic set. We will develop causal frameworks to distinguish between drift caused by input distribution shifts versus changes in the model's internal knowledge (e.g., post-unlearning). The method will be validated using benchmarks from OpenUnlearning and medical adaptation studies, simulating temporal drift scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled toolkit for continuous model monitoring, reducing reliance on expensive full-scale evaluations. It will contribute new metrics and causal diagnostics for distinguishing types of drift, directly informing when retraining or unlearning is necessary. The framework will be integrated into evaluation suites like OpenUnlearning to benchmark the stability of unlearning methods over time.
proposals.novelty: While SPC is mature in manufacturing, its application to monitoring the statistical properties of LLM inference streams is novel. The integration of causal reasoning to diagnose the *source* of detected drift (input vs. model) moves beyond simple detection, aligning with pre-2026 interests in causal evaluation for ML systems.

proposals.title: Anomaly Detection for Shift Exemplars: Identifying Characteristic Points for LLM Unlearning and Medical Adaptation
proposals.motivation: In critical applications like medical AI and responsible LLM deployment, models must adapt to distribution shifts, such as correcting factual errors or unlearning harmful data. Current unlearning and adaptation methods often treat all data points within a 'forget' or 'target' set uniformly, lacking a principled way to identify which instances are most representative of the shift. This inefficiency can lead to suboptimal model updates, excessive compute, and incomplete adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deep anomaly detection methods, applied in the latent space of a pre-trained model, can reliably identify a sparse subset of 'shift exemplars'—data points that most typify the distributional shift—and that targeting these exemplars will make unlearning and adaptation procedures more efficient and effective.
proposals.method: We will leverage pre-2026 deep anomaly detection methods, such as Deep Support Vector Data Description (Deep SVDD) or isolation forest variants on learned embeddings, to score instances in a target shift set (e.g., data to be unlearned). We will compute anomaly scores relative to the base training distribution's embedding manifold. The highest-scoring points will be designated as shift exemplars. We will then integrate this identification step into established unlearning pipelines (e.g., gradient ascent, model editing) and medical adaptation frameworks, comparing performance against baselines that use random or full-set sampling on benchmarks like OpenUnlearning and medical QA datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, model-intrinsic metric for shift characterization, moving beyond uniform data treatment. It will yield a practical method to accelerate and improve the precision of LLM unlearning and domain adaptation. We will release code and a new benchmark task for evaluating shift exemplar identification.
proposals.novelty: While anomaly detection is used for outlier removal, its novel application here is to positively identify characteristic *in-distribution* points for a *new* target distribution (the shift), repurposing it for selective model updating rather than data cleaning.

proposals.title: Detecting Dataset Drift in Deployed LLMs: A Causal Change Point Framework for Unlearning Triggers
proposals.motivation: Deployed large language models (LLMs) in medical and other high-stakes domains are subject to dataset shift as real-world data streams evolve, potentially degrading performance or propagating outdated or harmful information. Current unlearning methods are reactive, applied after errors are identified, rather than proactive. A method to automatically detect the onset of significant drift in model inputs or outputs is needed to trigger timely and targeted unlearning interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that statistical change point detection algorithms, applied to streaming metrics of LLM behavior, can reliably identify the onset of dataset drift, and that these detected change points can serve as effective causal triggers for initiating model unlearning procedures.
proposals.method: We will adapt offline (e.g., PELT, Binary Segmentation) and online (e.g., CUSUM, Bayesian Change Point) detection algorithms to monitor a stream of model-derived metrics, such as prediction entropy, output distribution distances (e.g., KL divergence), or feature-space shifts in embeddings. Using a causal inference framework, we will establish a causal link between detected change points and performance degradation on held-out validation tasks. The system will be evaluated on the OpenUnlearning benchmark, simulating drift scenarios, and on temporal medical QA datasets to assess clinical relevance.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, automated framework for proactive model maintenance, moving unlearning from a scheduled or reactive process to one triggered by evidence of drift. It will provide a new evaluation paradigm for unlearning methods based on timely detection and establish causal metrics for linking drift to performance loss in streaming settings.
proposals.novelty: While change point detection is classic in time-series, its application as a causal trigger for LLM unlearning is novel. This bridges the gap between statistical process control and machine learning safety, creating a new operational paradigm for continuous model adaptation.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Malignancy of Distribution Shifts in Medical AI: An Information-Theoretic Framework for Unlearning and Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Medical AI models, especially large language models (LLMs), are deployed in dynamic environments where data distributions shift due to new clinical guidelines, emerging diseases, or corrected medical knowledge. Current adaptation and unlearning methods lack rigorous, quantitative metrics to assess the 'malignancy'—the potential for harmful performance degradation—of such shifts. This gap hinders the safe and efficient updating of models in high-stakes medical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that information-theoretic measures, such as KL divergence and mutual information between model outputs on old and new data regimes, can robustly quantify the malignancy of a distribution shift, predicting downstream task performance degradation more accurately than simple accuracy-based metrics.
proposals.method: We will develop a benchmark using medical QA datasets (e.g., MedQA, PubMedQA) to simulate malignant shifts (e.g., introducing factual errors or contradictory guidelines). We will adapt and unlearn LLMs (e.g., clinical variants of LLaMA or Flan-T5) using established methods (e.g., gradient ascent, selective retraining). Our core technical contribution is a suite of malignancy metrics: 1) Output Divergence (KL divergence between pre- and post-unlearning output distributions), 2) Forgetting Specificity (mutual information loss on targeted 'toxic' facts), and 3) Retention Integrity (mutual information preserved on benign, related knowledge). We will perform causal mediation analysis to link these metrics to clinical task performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified, information-theoretic framework for evaluating distribution shift malignancy in medical AI. It will yield actionable metrics for auditing unlearning algorithms (extending GenAudit principles) and for guiding efficient model adaptation, directly addressing progress assessment challenges highlighted in medical LLM literature. The resulting benchmark will extend OpenUnlearning to the medical domain.
proposals.novelty: While divergence measures are used in general unlearning, their application to characterize the *malignancy* of a shift—specifically for medical knowledge—and the proposed integration with causal analysis to validate metric utility is novel. This moves beyond measuring if unlearning occurred to diagnosing how *dangerous* the underlying data shift is for clinical reliability.

proposals.title: Robust Distribution Shift Detection for Reliable LLM Unlearning and Medical Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Current methods for evaluating LLM unlearning and medical domain adaptation rely on average-case metrics, which are brittle to outliers and noisy data. This is critical because real-world deployment data, especially in sensitive domains like medicine, often contains corrupted or atypical samples. A failure to robustly detect when a model's behavior has shifted due to unlearning or adaptation can lead to unsafe or unreliable system outputs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that robust statistical estimators (e.g., M-estimators, trimmed means, influence function-based distances) will provide more reliable and stable detection of distribution shifts in LLM outputs compared to standard non-robust metrics, particularly in the presence of outliers common in post-unlearning audits and real-world clinical text.
proposals.method: We will adapt robust estimators to measure shifts in the probability distributions of LLM generations. For unlearning, we will integrate these into the OpenUnlearning benchmark, replacing standard probability or log-likelihood metrics with robust alternatives to assess forgetting efficacy. For medical adaptation, we will use robust two-sample tests (e.g., based on energy statistics or robust kernel MMD) to compare model outputs on curated benchmarks against noisy, real-world clinical notes. We will conduct causal analyses to isolate the effect of outliers on shift detection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, robust evaluation framework for LLM safety interventions (unlearning) and domain adaptation, increasing the reliability of audit tools like GenAudit. It will yield new, outlier-resistant metrics for benchmarking progress in medical LLMs, moving beyond averages to worst-case or high-confidence guarantees.
proposals.novelty: While robust statistics are well-established, their application to detecting distribution shifts in high-dimensional, discrete LLM output spaces is novel. This approach directly addresses a key vulnerability in contemporary LLM evaluation paradigms that has been overlooked in prior unlearning and adaptation research.

proposals.title: StreamAudit: Real-Time Detection and Correction of Factual Drift in LLM-Generated Data Streams
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in dynamic, real-time applications like medical monitoring dashboards and financial news summarization, where they process continuous data streams. Pre-2026 evaluation and unlearning benchmarks are largely static, failing to address the challenge of detecting and correcting factual errors as they emerge in high-velocity streams. This creates a critical gap for safe human-AI teaming in time-sensitive domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a lightweight, causal intervention framework can detect and correct factual inconsistencies in LLM-generated text streams with millisecond latency, significantly outperforming batch-oriented methods in accuracy-latency trade-offs.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the evidence-based auditing approach from GenAudit into a streaming architecture. The method employs a small, causal inference model trained on synthetic error streams to identify potential factual drifts in real-time. Upon detection, it triggers a targeted, retrieval-augmented correction using a sliding-window knowledge base, inspired by efficient unlearning techniques. We will evaluate on a novel benchmark built from high-frequency medical literature streams and financial news feeds, using latency-adjusted versions of metrics from OpenUnlearning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel benchmark for real-time factual consistency in data streams, 2) an open-source framework (StreamAudit) for low-latency detection and correction, and 3) empirical results quantifying the trade-offs between latency, correction quality, and computational cost for streaming LLM applications.
proposals.novelty: This moves unlearning and auditing from a static, post-hoc operation to a dynamic, online process, integrating causal streaming detection with evidence-based correction—a combination not explored in pre-2026 LLM safety literature.

proposals.title: EdgeAudit: Ultra-Lightweight Distribution Shift Detection for On-Device LLM Safety Monitoring
proposals.motivation: The deployment of LLMs on edge devices in medical and teaming applications introduces critical safety risks, as models may encounter unseen data distributions leading to unreliable or harmful outputs. Existing shift detection methods are computationally prohibitive for tiny edge hardware, creating a gap between the need for real-time safety audits and practical deployment constraints. This work addresses the core challenge of enabling continuous, local model monitoring without cloud dependency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by distilling the statistical signatures of distribution shift into a set of efficient, causal-inspired tests on model internals (e.g., attention patterns or layer activations), we can create detection algorithms that are both accurate and lightweight enough for real-time use on microcontrollers.
proposals.method: We will develop EdgeAudit, a framework that constructs a minimal, fixed-dimensional feature vector from a subset of model layer statistics computed during inference. Using pre-2026 causal discrepancy metrics (e.g., adaptations of Maximum Mean Discrepancy) and efficient statistical tests (like CUSUM on quantized features), we will train tiny detection heads (e.g., <10KB binary classifiers) to flag shifts. The approach will be evaluated on the OpenUnlearning benchmark and medical domain shifts from Med-VL benchmarks, measuring detection latency and accuracy on ARM Cortex-M class hardware.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first benchmark suite for shift detection on resource-constrained devices, establishing practical baselines for edge-based model audit. It contributes a novel methodology for 'unlearning' monitoring, allowing devices to locally identify when a model's knowledge is becoming unreliable without retraining.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work focused on server-side evaluation, we explicitly co-design the detection algorithm and the hardware constraints, moving from post-hoc auditing to proactive, embedded safety. The integration of causal discrepancy metrics into a micro-optimized feature pipeline for LLMs on edge devices is unexplored.

proposals.title: Certifiably Safe Shifts: A Causal Framework for Generating Distribution Shifts that Preserve Model Performance
proposals.motivation: Deploying machine learning models, especially in high-stakes domains like medicine, requires understanding their robustness to distribution shifts. Current research focuses on detecting or adapting to harmful shifts after they occur. A proactive, preventative approach—generating data shifts that are guaranteed *not* to degrade performance—is crucial for stress-testing models safely and for creating robust training curricula. This is the inverse of the typical robustness problem.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging a causal graph of the data-generating process, we can algorithmically generate distribution shifts that manipulate non-causal, spurious features while holding causal mechanisms invariant, thereby guaranteeing that a model's performance on the core task remains unharmed.
proposals.method: We will formalize the problem using Structural Causal Models (SCMs). Given a pre-trained model and an estimated SCM for its training domain, we will develop an algorithm that performs targeted interventions on exogenous variables associated with spurious features (e.g., image background, text style) while keeping interventions on causal features within safe bounds. This generates new synthetic or perturbed datasets. We will validate safety by deriving performance certificates based on the SCM's invariance properties, testing on vision-language and medical language models using benchmarks from the researcher's prior work (e.g., Medical Adaptation benchmarks).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel framework and algorithm for generating 'safe shifts,' 2) a diagnostic tool to identify which spurious features a model is vulnerably *reliant* on versus robustly *invariant* to, and 3) a method for creating 'safety-guaranteed' data augmentations or unlearning datasets that remove spurious correlations without harming core competency.
proposals.novelty: While prior work evaluates robustness to *given* shifts or unlearns specific features, our approach inverts the problem to *synthesize* shifts with *a priori* performance guarantees using causal reasoning, moving from post-hoc evaluation to controlled, certified stress-testing.

proposals.title: Dual-Training for Invariant Representations: Unlearning Spurious Correlations via Causal Intervention in LLMs
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often learn spurious correlations from training data, leading to brittle performance under distribution shifts and factual errors. This is especially critical in medical and high-stakes domains where models must be robust. Current unlearning and auditing methods (e.g., GenAudit) are post-hoc corrections; a training procedure that inherently builds shift-invariant representations is needed for fundamental robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: By causally decomposing model predictions into invariant (causal) and spurious (non-causal) components during training, we can develop a dual-objective procedure that actively unlearns sensitivity to distribution shifts detectable by auditing tools, leading to models with more stable and reliable outputs.
proposals.method: We will develop a training framework inspired by invariant risk minimization (IRM) and causal mediation analysis. For a given input, we will use a perturbation model (or counterfactual generator) to produce variants that alter spurious features identified by auditing metrics. The core model will have a dual-path architecture: a primary predictor and an auxiliary 'spurious feature' predictor. The training objective will combine standard loss with a penalty term that maximizes the KL divergence between the primary predictions on original and perturbed inputs, while the auxiliary predictor is trained to identify the shift. We will integrate evaluation suites from OpenUnlearning and medical adaptation benchmarks to define and measure the shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training paradigm that proactively builds shift-invariance into LLMs, moving beyond reactive unlearning. It will deliver a concrete, scalable algorithm and a new evaluation protocol linking causal representation learning to practical unlearning metrics. The resulting models should demonstrate improved robustness in medical QA and factual consistency tasks.
proposals.novelty: While IRM and causal representation learning exist, their application to LLM unlearning via a dual-path architecture explicitly penalizing sensitivity to auditor-detected shifts is novel. This bridges causal inference, unlearning benchmarks, and training-time intervention in a unified framework.

proposals.title: Federated Unlearning: Detecting and Removing Sensitive Data in Distributed Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) trained on distributed, sensitive data (e.g., across hospitals) face critical challenges in complying with data deletion requests (e.g., 'right to be forgotten'). Centralizing data for unlearning violates the privacy principles of federated learning (FL). There is a pressing need for methods that can detect and unlearn specific data points or concepts directly within the FL framework, without requiring data centralization or full model retraining.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting gradient-based influence estimation and unlearning methods to the FL setting, we can develop a federated unlearning protocol that effectively identifies and removes the influence of target data across distributed clients while preserving model utility and client privacy.
proposals.method: We will adapt influence unlearning and gradient ascent methods (e.g., Lipton's prior work on unlearning benchmarks) to operate in a federated averaging (FedAvg) framework. The core protocol involves: 1) A federated detection phase using distributed gradient similarity metrics to identify clients holding target data, 2) A federated unlearning phase where selected clients perform local gradient ascent on loss for target data, while others perform standard SGD, followed by secure aggregation. We will rigorously evaluate using the OpenUnlearning benchmark adapted to a federated, non-IID data setting, measuring both unlearning efficacy (via metrics like membership inference attack success rate reduction) and model utility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive framework and benchmark for federated unlearning in LLMs. It will deliver a practical protocol for privacy-compliant data removal in distributed settings, directly impacting sensitive domains like healthcare. The evaluation will establish causal metrics for assessing unlearning success in FL, advancing both the unlearning and federated learning fields.
proposals.novelty: While unlearning and FL are active areas, their intersection is largely unexplored, especially for LLMs. This proposal moves beyond centralized benchmarks to tackle the non-IID, privacy-constrained reality of distributed data, creating a novel federated protocol for detection and removal.

proposals.title: The Fidelity Gap: A Causal Framework for Evaluating Synthetic Proxies in LLM Unlearning and Memorization Audits
proposals.motivation: Effective unlearning and auditing for privacy in LLMs require robust evaluation, but real private data is scarce for research. Synthetic data is widely used as a proxy, yet its validity for capturing the nuances of real memorization—such as contextual embedding, frequency, and salience—remains unproven. This gap undermines confidence in unlearning benchmarks and could lead to methods that fail on genuine private data, a critical risk for medical or personal applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current synthetic data generation methods create a 'fidelity gap,' producing memorization artifacts that are causally different from those of real private data, leading to systematic over- or under-estimation of unlearning efficacy in benchmark evaluations.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal framework to model memorization, treating data attributes (e.g., format, frequency, semantic uniqueness) as treatments and memorization strength as the outcome. Using a suite of real private datasets (e.g., deduplicated medical notes) and carefully crafted synthetic analogues, we will train and then apply targeted unlearning methods (e.g., gradient ascent, model editing). We will measure the divergence in unlearning outcomes using a multi-faceted metric suite from OpenUnlearning, augmented with causal mediation analysis to isolate which data attributes drive the performance gap.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, causal analysis of the synthetic data fidelity problem in unlearning, yielding a validated framework for creating more realistic synthetic benchmarks. It will produce concrete guidelines for when synthetic data is sufficient and when it is dangerously misleading, directly improving the reliability of tools like GenAudit for real-world deployment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work benchmarks unlearning methods, none has causally deconstructed why synthetic data might fail as a proxy for real memorization. Our approach uniquely bridges causal inference with unlearning evaluation to diagnose this core validity threat.

proposals.title: Beyond Retraining Equivalence: A Causal Framework for Useful and Efficient Unlearning in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current machine unlearning research, especially for LLMs, is dominated by the goal of achieving statistical equivalence to a model retrained from scratch without the 'forgotten' data. This stringent requirement is often computationally infeasible for large models and may be unnecessary for real-world applications like compliance, privacy, or fixing factual errors. A more pragmatic approach is needed that prioritizes downstream utility and safety over perfect equivalence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by reframing unlearning as a causal intervention to remove specific harmful influences while preserving general utility, we can develop methods that are both more efficient and more useful than those seeking strict retraining equivalence.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal framework that models the influence of training data points on model outputs. Using influence functions and gradient-based metrics, we will identify and intervene on specific model parameters most causally linked to the 'forget set'. We will then design a novel unlearning objective that minimizes causal influence on the forget set while maximizing performance on a carefully constructed 'retain set' and a separate 'utility set' of general capabilities. This will be evaluated using the OpenUnlearning benchmark and specialized medical QA datasets to assess factual correctness and reasoning degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a formal causal framework for reasoning about unlearning, 2) a new class of efficient unlearning algorithms optimized for utility preservation, and 3) a set of evaluation metrics that decouple 'forgetting efficacy' from 'utility preservation', moving beyond the retraining equivalence paradigm.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the core assumption of the field by explicitly abandoning the goal of retraining equivalence in favor of a causal, utility-preserving objective, which is a non-obvious but pragmatically necessary shift for deploying unlearning in real systems like medical AI.

proposals.title: Unlearning Under Uncertainty: A Causal Framework for Approximate Forget Sets in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current LLM unlearning methods assume a precisely known 'forget set', which is unrealistic for real-world scenarios like removing copyrighted content, biased associations, or outdated medical facts where the exact data to forget is ambiguous. This gap limits the practical deployment of unlearning in sensitive domains like medicine, where models must adapt to evolving guidelines without perfect data isolation. A method to handle uncertain forget sets is crucial for responsible model editing and lifecycle management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing unlearning as a causal intervention on latent representations associated with an uncertain data distribution, we can develop a robust method that effectively removes a target concept even when the exact forget set is unknown but can be approximately characterized.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal framework where the 'forget concept' is defined by a set of proxy attributes or a small, noisy seed set. Using techniques from causal representation learning, we will identify a disentangled subspace in the model's latent representations correlated with the target concept. The unlearning procedure will then apply a gradient-based intervention, inspired by influence functions, to dampen activations in this subspace, while a contrastive loss will preserve performance on a 'retain distribution' defined as the complement. Evaluation will use the OpenUnlearning benchmark, extended with synthetic uncertainty, and medical QA tasks from MedQA where guidelines have changed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal framework and practical algorithm for LLM unlearning with approximate forget sets, moving the field beyond the idealized known-set assumption. We will contribute a new evaluation protocol for robustness to forget-set uncertainty and demonstrate its application in a medical adaptation setting, showing improved model safety and adaptability.
proposals.novelty: While existing work (e.g., gradient ascent, model editing) requires exact forget samples, our approach novelly integrates causal representation learning with unlearning to handle distributional uncertainty, a step not yet taken in pre-2026 literature. This bridges the gap between precise unlearning methods and the fuzzy realities of data management.

proposals.title: Causal Unlearning: A Framework for Provable Data Removal and Behavior Erasure in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current LLM unlearning methods, often based on gradient ascent or fine-tuning on curated data, lack formal guarantees and can degrade general performance or leave residual traces of the 'forgotten' data. This is critical in domains like medicine, where models must comply with data deletion requests (e.g., GDPR) and avoid perpetuating harmful, outdated, or private information. The field lacks a principled, causal framework to define and achieve true unlearning, as highlighted by recent benchmarking efforts like OpenUnlearning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing unlearning as a causal intervention on the model's internal representations, guided by a formal specification of 'forgotten' behavior, will yield algorithms that more effectively erase target data while preserving general utility, compared to existing gradient-based or data-centric methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal unlearning framework. First, we will define a causal graph linking training data concepts to model outputs and internal activations. Using this graph, we will specify the unlearning target as a causal intervention (e.g., do-calculus) to sever the influence of the 'forget' set. We will then implement this via a novel training objective combining: (1) a causal regularization term that minimizes mutual information between the forget set and intervened model states, and (2) a distributional alignment loss (using KL divergence) to ensure the unlearned model's output distribution matches that of a model retrained from scratch without the forget data. We will evaluate rigorously using the OpenUnlearning benchmark and causal mediation analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, causal definition of unlearning success, moving beyond heuristic metrics. It will deliver a novel algorithm with stronger theoretical guarantees for data removal. The resulting framework and evaluation suite will enable more reliable and auditable unlearning, directly addressing needs in sensitive applications like medical AI and model auditing.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses causal graphs for model explanation or error fixing (e.g., GenAudit), applying causal intervention theory directly to the unlearning optimization objective is novel. Our approach moves beyond treating unlearning as a pure optimization problem to a structured causal editing task, which is non-obvious given the prevailing fine-tuning and gradient-ascent paradigms.

proposals.title: TOFU++: Scaling and Diversifying Synthetic Profiles for Robust LLM Unlearning Evaluation
proposals.motivation: The Task of Fictitious Unlearning (TOFU) benchmark provides a crucial controlled setting for evaluating LLM unlearning methods. However, its limited scale and synthetic author homogeneity (50 profiles) may not adequately stress-test methods or reveal biases in what is forgotten or retained. As unlearning moves towards real-world applications like medical record deletion or model safety, a more robust, scalable, and diverse evaluation suite is urgently needed to prevent overfitting to narrow benchmarks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that expanding the TOFU benchmark with orders-of-magnitude more profiles and introducing systematic diversity in profile attributes (e.g., writing style, factual domains, demographic correlates) will reveal significant performance variance in current unlearning methods, exposing weaknesses not apparent on the original dataset and enabling more reliable model comparison.
proposals.method: We will develop a scalable pipeline to generate thousands of synthetic author profiles. Building on TOFU's methodology, we will use a strong base LLM (e.g., GPT-4) but introduce controlled variables: stylistic diversity via prompt engineering, domain shifts (e.g., blending medical, historical, technical facts), and parameterized 'biographical' attributes. For each profile, we will generate a corpus of 'forgotten' facts and retain a held-out set for evaluation. We will rigorously evaluate state-of-the-art unlearning methods (e.g., gradient ascent, model editing) on this expanded benchmark, analyzing performance across profile clusters using causal frameworks to isolate the effect of specific diversity dimensions on unlearning efficacy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with TOFU++, a significantly larger and more diverse open-source benchmark for unlearning, mitigating evaluation overfitting. It will yield new insights into how unlearning generalizes across data characteristics, establishing more reliable evaluation metrics. The findings will directly inform the development of more robust unlearning algorithms for sensitive domains like healthcare.
proposals.novelty: While TOFU introduced the synthetic profile paradigm, this work systematically scales and diversifies it, moving beyond a proof-of-concept to a stress-test suite. The intentional engineering of profile diversity to probe unlearning boundaries is a novel contribution over simply increasing dataset size.

proposals.title: Unlearning Audits: A Causal Framework for Holistic Evaluation of LLM Unlearning Efficacy and Equivalence
proposals.motivation: Current LLM unlearning evaluation, as highlighted in works like OpenUnlearning, relies on disparate metrics for forgetting (e.g., accuracy drop on 'forget' sets) and model utility (e.g., general performance). This fragmented approach fails to capture whether an unlearned model is truly equivalent to a model never trained on the target data, a critical requirement for safety and compliance. The lack of a unified, causal framework leaves practitioners unable to rigorously audit unlearning claims, especially in sensitive domains like medicine where factual errors, as studied in GenAudit, can have severe consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a causal inference framework, treating unlearning as an intervention, can define and measure a holistic 'equivalence gap' that more reliably captures true unlearning efficacy than current metric suites. This gap will quantify the residual statistical and causal dependence between the unlearned model and the 'forgotten' concepts.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) to model the unlearning process, defining the ideal counterfactual model (trained without the target data). Building on this, we will design three interconnected metric classes: 1) Causal Forgetting: Adapting causal mediation analysis to measure the effect of the 'forget' set on model outputs post-unlearning. 2) Distributional Equivalence: Using kernel-based tests (MMD) and classifier probes to compare the internal representations and output distributions of the unlearned model against the counterfactual baseline. 3) Strategic Auditing: Creating adversarial prompts and re-memorization tests, informed by medical QA benchmarks, to stress-test the robustness of the unlearning. We will implement and validate this framework within an extended OpenUnlearning benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal, theory-grounded framework for auditing LLM unlearning, moving beyond ad-hoc metrics. It will deliver a publicly available, holistic evaluation suite that measures not just 'forgetting' but true model equivalence, directly addressing safety and regulatory needs. The framework will offer practitioners a standardized audit tool and guide future unlearning algorithm development toward verifiable outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work catalogs metrics, our approach is novel in formally defining the unlearning objective via causal counterfactuals and deriving integrated metrics from this definition. This shifts evaluation from measuring correlated symptoms to directly assessing the causal effect of the unlearning intervention, a perspective not yet established in pre-2026 literature.
