proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A novel, pedagogically-grounded evaluation framework for LLM unlearning that measures conceptual understanding rather than mere token prediction. 2) New insights into the failure modes of existing unlearning methods by analyzing which conceptual links persist. 3) A pathway towards more robust 'team-based' unlearning where multiple model instances debate to identify and rectify retained knowledge.
proposals.novelty: While prior work benchmarks unlearning methods, none leverage established pedagogical techniques like peer instruction to probe conceptual models. Bridging causal graph analysis of knowledge with interactive, discussion-based evaluation represents a significant departure from static question-answering benchmarks.

proposals.title: The Unlearning Audit: A Computational Journalism Framework for Explaining and Diagnosing LLM Unlearning Behaviors
proposals.motivation: The rapid development of Large Language Model (LLM) unlearning methods, as benchmarked in works like OpenUnlearning, has created a critical need for interpretability. Stakeholders, from medical teams deploying adapted models to regulators, lack intuitive explanations for *how* unlearning succeeds or fails, what side-effects it induces, and whether unlearned knowledge is truly erased or merely suppressed. This opacity hinders safe deployment and trust.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying computational journalism techniques—structured data storytelling, causal narrative extraction, and explanatory visualization—to unlearning audit trails will produce more actionable, intuitive explanations of model behavioral changes than standard evaluation metrics alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the OpenUnlearning benchmark to generate rich, multi-modal audit logs for each unlearning method (e.g., gradient ascent, model editing). Using techniques from GenAudit, we will trace causal chains linking unlearning interventions to changes in model outputs on targeted and collateral tasks. We will then design a pipeline that treats these logs as a 'dataset' for computational journalism, employing narrative generation algorithms and interactive visualizations (e.g., Sankey diagrams of knowledge flow, counterfactual output comparisons) to automatically produce human-readable investigative reports on each unlearning episode.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel framework for auditing unlearning that moves beyond aggregate metrics to causal, narrative explanations; 2) a public toolkit for generating interactive audit reports, making unlearning research more accessible to interdisciplinary teams; 3) new insights into the failure modes of unlearning methods, potentially guiding more robust algorithm design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on *evaluating* unlearning with metrics, this proposal innovates by framing the audit process as an *explanatory storytelling* task, explicitly bridging model interpretability with communication design. It uniquely applies computational journalism, a field concerned with explaining complex data, to the specific problem of diagnosing knowledge removal in LLMs.

proposals.title: Unlearning by Playing: A Gamified Framework for Efficient and Engaging LLM Unlearning
proposals.motivation: Current LLM unlearning methods, while technically promising, are evaluated in sterile, automated benchmarks that ignore human factors. For real-world deployment, such as in medical teaming applications where models must rapidly adapt to new guidelines or retract harmful outputs, user engagement and sustained effort during the unlearning 'training' phase are critical. Gamification, proven to enhance motivation in education and software, remains unexplored as a lever to improve the human-in-the-loop process of model correction and knowledge updates.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating game design elements—specifically challenge-based progression, immediate feedback, and reward systems—into the interface and training loop for LLM unlearning will increase user engagement and consistency, leading to more effective and efficient unlearning outcomes compared to standard interfaces.
proposals.method: We will develop a benchmark environment extending OpenUnlearning, where human users are tasked with providing feedback (e.g., correcting factual errors as in GenAudit) to 'train' a base LLM. The control group uses a standard interface. The experimental group interacts via a gamified system presenting corrections as 'quests,' with visual progress bars, achievement badges for identifying subtle errors, and a points system tied to downstream unlearning metrics (e.g., reduction in unwanted completions). We will employ causal inference techniques to isolate the effect of engagement from interface familiarity, measuring both user interaction logs and final model performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first framework for gamified machine unlearning, a set of novel metrics for evaluating human engagement in correction tasks, and empirical evidence on how motivation impacts unlearning efficacy. It will bridge the gap between technical unlearning algorithms and human-computer interaction, offering practical design principles for real-world systems.
proposals.novelty: While gamification is known in education and LLM evaluation is mature, applying game design specifically to the *process* of machine unlearning is a novel synthesis. It moves beyond viewing unlearning as a purely algorithmic problem to one where human psychology and system design are integral to success.

proposals.title: Unlearning for Adaptation: Causal Evaluation of LLM-Generated Micro-Lessons for Mobile Medical Teaming
proposals.motivation: Mobile learning is critical for time-constrained medical teams, but existing LLM content generation is monolithic and often contains factual errors or irrelevant information, hindering efficient knowledge transfer. This problem is acute in medicine, where outdated or incorrect information can have serious consequences, and learning must be integrated into brief, on-the-go sessions. A systematic method to decompose and refine knowledge into accurate, context-aware micro-lessons is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying causal unlearning techniques to LLM-generated educational content will produce more focused, accurate, and instructionally sound micro-lessons than standard prompting or fine-tuning, as measured by domain-specific factual accuracy and learner retention metrics.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that first uses a base LLM (e.g., LLaMA-2 or Med-PaLM) to segment a medical textbook into candidate micro-lessons. We will then apply a suite of unlearning methods (e.g., gradient ascent, model editing) to explicitly remove tangential information and reinforce core causal relationships within each lesson. A causal graph of medical concepts will guide the unlearning process to preserve prerequisite knowledge. The refined lessons will be evaluated using the GenAudit framework for factual errors and a novel mobile-interface simulation to assess instructional clarity and retention.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a benchmark for evaluating the instructional quality and factual fidelity of LLM-generated micro-content, extending OpenUnlearning to the educational domain; 2) a novel application of unlearning as a positive tool for pedagogical refinement, not just privacy or safety; 3) a validated pipeline for creating mobile-optimized learning assets for medical team training.
proposals.novelty: While unlearning is typically used for privacy or removing harmful data, we repurpose it as a targeted content refinement tool for education. Furthermore, we integrate causal reasoning with unlearning to preserve essential prerequisite knowledge chains, a challenge not addressed in standard text summarization or generation.

proposals.title: Unlearning for AI Safety: A Scalable MOOC Framework for Evaluating and Teaching Machine Unlearning in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: The rapid deployment of large language models (LLMs) necessitates methods to remove harmful, private, or outdated information post-training, a process known as machine unlearning. However, as highlighted by pre-2026 benchmarks like OpenUnlearning, the field lacks standardized evaluation and accessible educational resources for practitioners. Scaling expert knowledge into a structured, open course is critical for training the next generation of AI safety researchers and engineers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Massive Open Online Course (MOOC) built around a unified benchmark for LLM unlearning will not only effectively disseminate knowledge but also, through structured assignments and automated grading, generate novel, crowdsourced insights into the comparative efficacy and failure modes of different unlearning algorithms.
proposals.method: We will develop a MOOC platform centered on the OpenUnlearning benchmark. The technical core will involve: 1) Creating video lectures and interactive notebooks covering unlearning methods (e.g., gradient ascent, model editing) and causal evaluation frameworks. 2) Implementing an automated grading system that evaluates student-submitted code on held-out benchmark tasks, measuring both efficacy (forgetting) and collateral damage (model utility). 3) Using the platform to run A/B tests on pedagogical content and collect large-scale, human-in-the-loop performance data on unlearning algorithms from course participants.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute the first major educational platform dedicated to AI safety via machine unlearning, directly scaling Zachary Lipton's team's research impact. It will also create a novel, living dataset of algorithm evaluations from a diverse global cohort, providing longitudinal data on how different methods perform across varied implementations and offering new metrics for robustness.
proposals.novelty: While MOOCs exist for ML fundamentals, none are architected as both a teaching tool and a large-scale, continuous evaluation engine for a nascent, critical subfield like LLM unlearning, turning student assignments into a distributed research audit.

proposals.title: Code2Concept: Causal Unlearning for Concept Attribution and Explanation in Student-Generated Code
proposals.motivation: Automated feedback in programming education often focuses on correctness, failing to diagnose the underlying conceptual misunderstandings that produce functional but flawed code. This gap is critical in high-stakes domains like medical training, where code may run but encode dangerous misconceptions. Pre-2026, large language models (LLMs) excel at generating code from text, but the inverse problem—inferring a student's mental model from their code—remains an open challenge for robust, interpretable AI teaching assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing code explanation as a causal unlearning problem—where we iteratively remove spurious, syntax-level features to isolate the core algorithmic concepts—we can generate more faithful and pedagogically useful explanations than standard retrieval-augmented or fine-tuned LLMs.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, a concept attribution model, built by fine-tuning a code LLM (e.g., CodeLlama) on a novel dataset of (code, concept graph) pairs, will map code snippets to a structured set of programming concepts. Second, an explanation generator, leveraging a vision-language model (e.g., LLaVA) for diagram creation, will produce multimodal explanations. The core novelty is a training regimen using counterfactual unlearning: we will synthetically corrupt code to introduce misleading features and train the model to be invariant to them, improving robustness. Evaluation will use causal mediation analysis on model outputs to measure concept attribution fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel benchmark for evaluating code-to-concept inference, extending unlearning evaluation frameworks like OpenUnlearning to educational settings; 2) a publicly available tool for generating causal, multimodal explanations from code; and 3) new insights into applying unlearning and causal methods for robust model interpretability, directly extending themes from GenAudit and medical adaptation research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs for code generation or comment synthesis, our approach uniquely treats misconception diagnosis as a causal unlearning task, actively removing reliance on superficial code patterns to isolate core conceptual understanding, a method not explored in pre-2026 educational AI or code intelligence literature.

proposals.title: CrowdAudit: A Community-Driven Framework for Scalable Dataset Creation and LLM Unlearning Evaluation
proposals.motivation: The rapid deployment of large language models (LLMs) in sensitive domains like medicine necessitates robust, scalable methods for identifying and correcting factual errors and harmful memorization. Current benchmarks for tasks like unlearning and factual auditing are static, limited in scope, and slow to adapt to new failure modes, creating a bottleneck for safety research. A community-driven approach can accelerate the creation of diverse, high-quality test cases, mirroring the success of crowdsourced data creation in other AI subfields.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, gamified crowdsourcing platform can efficiently generate a large, diverse, and high-quality benchmark for evaluating LLM unlearning and factual consistency, surpassing the scale and adaptability of expert-curated datasets. Furthermore, the resulting data will reveal novel failure patterns not captured by existing static evaluations.
proposals.method: We will design and deploy a web platform where contributors propose 'challenge problems' (e.g., factual statements to unlearn, medical QA pairs with subtle errors) and vet others' submissions via a peer-review and voting system, inspired by platforms like Dynabench. Quality will be ensured through a reputation system and cross-verification by a smaller panel of domain experts. The framework will integrate causal metrics from unlearning research (e.g., efficacy, specificity) and auditing methods (like those in GenAudit) to automatically score LLM responses to crowd-sourced challenges, creating a unified, living benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield 1) an open-source platform and protocol for continuous, community-driven dataset creation, 2) a large, dynamic benchmark (CrowdAuditBench) for evaluating unlearning and factual error correction in LLMs, and 3) new insights into model failure modes across domains, particularly in medical adaptation. It directly extends the evaluation-centric and unlearning themes of the researcher's prior work into a scalable, participatory paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While crowdsourcing is common for data labeling, applying it to create *evaluation benchmarks* for advanced LLM safety tasks like unlearning is novel. The key innovation is the closed-loop system where the community both identifies new model vulnerabilities and creates the test cases to measure progress in addressing them, moving beyond static benchmarks.

proposals.title: Faithful Unlearning: Causal Alignment of Post-hoc Explanations in Edited Language Models
proposals.motivation: Post-hoc explanation methods (e.g., SHAP, LIME) are widely used to interpret model predictions but are often criticized for being unfaithful, generating plausible but misleading rationales. This problem is acute after model editing or unlearning, where the model's internal reasoning changes, but explanations may not reflect this, eroding trust in high-stakes domains like medicine. Ensuring explanation faithfulness is a critical, unsolved challenge for deploying reliable and auditable AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by causally modeling the effect of unlearning interventions on a model's internal representations, we can develop post-hoc explanation methods whose feature attributions are provably aligned with the edited model's true reasoning pathways.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal framework treating unlearning as an intervention on the base model. First, we will adapt and extend the OpenUnlearning benchmark to include explanation faithfulness metrics. We will then train causal mediation models to map edits in parameter space to changes in attention and activation patterns for specific factual tuples. Our core technical contribution will be a novel explanation method, Causal-Aligned Attribution (CAA), which uses these mediation estimates to constrain post-hoc attribution algorithms (e.g., integrated gradients) to only highlight features causally implicated in the edited model's output.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel, causality-grounded method for generating faithful post-hoc explanations in edited LLMs; 2) an extension of the OpenUnlearning benchmark with rigorous metrics for evaluating explanation faithfulness; and 3) empirical validation in medical QA tasks, demonstrating improved auditability after model updates to fix errors (extending GenAudit's goals).
proposals.novelty: While prior work evaluates unlearning efficacy or generates explanations separately, our approach is novel in its direct, causal alignment of post-hoc explanations with the mechanistic impact of unlearning, moving beyond correlation to guarantee faithfulness.

proposals.title: Stakeholder-Adaptive Explanations: A Causal Framework for Multi-Audience LLM Unlearning Verification
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of LLM unlearning methods relies on monolithic benchmarks (e.g., OpenUnlearning) that produce a single 'explanation' of efficacy via aggregate metrics. This fails to address the distinct needs of regulators (requiring causal proof of harm removal), developers (needing debugging insights), and users (seeking transparent, understandable assurances). This gap is critical in high-stakes domains like medicine, where unlearning sensitive data or incorrect facts (as in GenAudit) demands trust from all parties.
proposals.hypothesis: The sufficiency of a single explanation for an unlearning operation varies systematically across stakeholder groups; a multi-faceted, stakeholder-adaptive explanation framework will improve perceived trust and enable more precise, actionable evaluation of unlearning methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a causal inference framework to generate distinct explanation 'views' from a single unlearning intervention. For regulators, we adapt causal mediation analysis on model internals to attribute output changes to the unlearning procedure. For developers, we design metrics based on gradient-based feature attribution (e.g., Integrated Gradients) to localize 'what the model forgot' in parameter space. For users, we generate contrastive, natural language explanations derived from evidence passages (inspired by GenAudit) comparing pre- and post-unlearning outputs. We will validate the framework by applying it to medical LLM adaptation benchmarks, measuring stakeholder-specific satisfaction and task performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a formal, multi-stakeholder evaluation framework for LLM unlearning, moving beyond monolithic metrics; 2) novel, causal and feature-attribution methods tailored for explainable AI auditing; 3) empirical evidence on explanation efficacy across groups, informing future standards for responsible AI deployment in sensitive domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work benchmarks unlearning methods (OpenUnlearning) or fixes errors (GenAudit), none systematically deconstructs the explanation of *why* unlearning succeeded/failed for different human audiences. Our integration of causal analysis for regulatory proof with developer-oriented debugging and user-facing contrastive explanations is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: ExTax: A Causal Framework for Taxonomizing Explanation Types and Goals in Human-LLM Teaming
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains like medicine, the demand for explanations is critical for trust and safety. However, the field lacks a systematic taxonomy linking the technical mechanisms of explanation methods (e.g., feature attribution, counterfactuals) to the specific human-centric goals they serve (e.g., debugging model logic, verifying factual correctness). This gap leads to mismatched evaluations and hinders progress in creating effective human-AI teams.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, causal taxonomy of explanation types, mapped to distinct human cognitive goals and downstream task outcomes, will enable more precise method selection and rigorous, goal-aligned evaluation of explainability in human-LLM teaming scenarios.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a systematic literature review (pre-2026) to extract technical dimensions (e.g., scope, faithfulness, format) and stated human goals. Using causal modeling, we will formalize the relationships between explanation properties, user inferences, and decision outcomes. We will then instantiate and validate the taxonomy through controlled user studies in a medical QA domain, measuring how different explanation types (e.g., retrieved evidence vs. attention visualization) impact goal-specific metrics like error detection (factual correction) and trust calibration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a formal, actionable taxonomy for explanation research, 2) a causal framework linking explanation properties to human goals, and 3) a new benchmark dataset for evaluating explanations in goal-oriented human-LLM teaming, directly extending the evaluation rigor of OpenUnlearning and GenAudit into the explanation space.
proposals.novelty: While prior work catalogs explanation techniques, our approach uniquely integrates a causal perspective to rigorously connect technical properties to human goals, and grounds validation in the concrete, high-stakes context of human-LLM medical teaming, moving beyond abstract faithfulness metrics.

proposals.title: Causal Unification: A Philosophy-of-Science Framework for Evaluating Explanations in Medical Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of ML explanations (e.g., saliency maps, feature attributions) relies on ad-hoc, often correlative metrics, lacking a principled foundation for what constitutes a 'good' explanation. This is critical in high-stakes domains like medicine, where flawed explanations can undermine trust and lead to harmful decisions. Grounding explanation quality in established philosophical theories of scientific explanation provides a rigorous, domain-aware standard for assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the 'Causal Unification' model from philosophy of science—which posits that explanations are good if they integrate a phenomenon into a unified causal structure—provides a normative framework that, when operationalized, yields explanation metrics that better correlate with human expert utility and robustness under distribution shift than current correlative or plausibility-based metrics.
proposals.method: We will first formalize key philosophical models (Causal-Mechanical, Unificationist) into testable criteria (e.g., causal invariance, parsimony, coherence). We will then develop novel metrics, building on pre-2026 causal inference and robustness techniques (e.g., counterfactual simulation, interventional Shapley values), to score post-hoc explanations from vision-language and LLMs on medical benchmarks. These metrics will be validated via large-scale human studies with clinicians, assessing correlation with perceived explanation utility for tasks like diagnosis from the MIMIC dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a philosophically grounded, empirically validated benchmark for explanation quality, moving beyond surface plausibility. It will yield new, causal-robustness-focused explanation methods and metrics, directly contributing to safer and more interpretable medical AI. The resulting framework, 'ExplainAudit,' will extend the team's prior work on GenAudit and medical adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work evaluates explanations via faithfulness or plausibility, this proposal is novel in directly deriving evaluation criteria from normative philosophical theories of scientific explanation and operationalizing them for modern ML. It uniquely bridges causal inference, philosophy of science, and medical AI evaluation.

proposals.title: Cognitive Audits of Unlearning: Measuring Mental Model Shifts in Human-AI Teaming
proposals.motivation: Current LLM unlearning and factual correction methods (e.g., GenAudit) are evaluated with automated metrics, ignoring their impact on human users. In high-stakes domains like medicine, where AI is used for teaming and decision support, it is critical that explanations post-correction foster accurate human mental models. Without this, users may retain trust in erroneous AI outputs or misunderstand the model's updated capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that standard unlearning methods, while effective at removing surface-level text, fail to induce appropriate mental model updates in human users, and that structured explanations grounded in causal attribution will significantly improve human understanding of the model's corrected knowledge.
proposals.method: We will adapt a medical QA benchmark where an LLM initially provides incorrect answers. We will apply state-of-the-art unlearning/correction methods (e.g., gradient ascent, selective fine-tuning). For each corrected output, we will generate multiple explanation types (e.g., counterfactual, feature-importance). We will then design a cognitive psychology experiment where clinicians perform prediction tasks about the AI's future behavior, measuring their inferred decision boundaries and confidence. We will use causal mediation analysis to isolate the effect of explanation type on mental model accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel evaluation framework for AI unlearning that centers human understanding, 2) empirical evidence on which explanation formats best align human mental models with corrected AI systems, and 3) actionable guidelines for designing 'human-centered unlearning' pipelines, particularly for medical AI teaming applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work benchmarks unlearning algorithms (OpenUnlearning) or medical adaptation progress, no research integrates cognitive psychology experiments to audit whether knowledge removal is effective from a human-in-the-loop perspective. This shifts the evaluation paradigm from model-centric to user-centric.

proposals.title: Forensic Accountability in LLMs: A Causal Framework for Evidentiary Reasoning and Legal-Standard Compliance
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes domains like medicine and law, their factual errors and inability to cite verifiable evidence create significant liability risks. Current evaluation metrics fail to assess whether a model's reasoning process meets legal standards for accountability, such as the ability to produce a defensible audit trail. This gap hinders the safe integration of AI into regulated environments where decisions must be explainable and contestable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formalizing legal standards for evidence (e.g., Daubert standard, best evidence rule) as causal constraints on model reasoning, we can develop LLMs whose outputs are forensically accountable—meaning their fact claims can be systematically traced to source evidence and their reasoning withstands structured challenge.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel benchmark, LEGAL-AUDIT, extending unlearning evaluation frameworks (e.g., OpenUnlearning) to test a model's adherence to legal-evidentiary rules. We will then design an intervention architecture that integrates a causal graph, built from retrieved evidence, with the LLM's generation process. This architecture, inspired by causal mediation analysis, will enforce that model outputs are *necessitated* by the evidence subgraph, enabling the generation of a forensic audit log that justifies each claim and identifies potential evidentiary gaps or conflicts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A rigorous, legally-grounded benchmark for evaluating forensic accountability in LLMs, 2) A novel model architecture that enforces evidentiary reasoning, producing actionable audit trails, and 3) New metrics that move beyond fact-checking to assess the *soundness* of the reasoning chain, directly addressing accountability needs in medical and legal teaming applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., GenAudit) corrects errors, it does not enforce a legal standard of proof during reasoning. Our approach is novel in translating abstract legal principles into causal computational constraints, creating a formal bridge between jurisprudence and machine learning for proactive accountability, not just post-hoc correction.

proposals.title: VisAudit: A Visualization-Driven Framework for Auditing and Correcting Factual Errors in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes domains like medicine, their propensity for factual errors poses significant risks. While recent work like GenAudit introduces evidence-based correction, and OpenUnlearning benchmarks unlearning methods, the explanation interfaces for these processes remain rudimentary. Effective human-AI teaming requires that clinicians and auditors can rapidly understand model errors and the evidence for corrections, a challenge where current text-only or simple saliency map explanations fall short.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying established principles from information visualization—such as data-ink ratio, visual encoding effectiveness, and interactive querying—to the design of explanation interfaces will significantly improve the speed and accuracy with which human auditors can identify, understand, and correct factual errors in LLM outputs.
proposals.method: We will develop VisAudit, a framework integrating visualization science with LLM auditing. First, we will extend the GenAudit pipeline to generate structured diagnostic traces (error type, conflicting evidence spans, confidence scores). Second, we will design and implement multiple interactive visual encodings for these traces, grounded in pre-2026 visualization theory (e.g., parallel coordinates for error provenance, juxtaposed text highlighting with evidence provenance). Third, we will conduct a controlled user study with medical professionals, using metrics from OpenUnlearning and causal inference to evaluate the causal effect of interface design on correction accuracy and time, compared to a text-based baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, open-source visualization framework for model auditing, 2) empirical evidence linking visualization design to human performance in the LLM correction loop, and 3) a set of validated design guidelines for explanation interfaces in high-stakes domains, advancing the field of human-LLM teaming.
proposals.novelty: While visualization is common in model interpretability (e.g., for attention), its principled application to the specific workflow of *factual error correction*—bridging benchmarks (OpenUnlearning), correction methods (GenAudit), and domain adaptation (Medical LLMs)—is novel and non-obvious.

proposals.title: Ensemble Auditing: Causal Attribution and Unlearning for Interpretability in Massive Model Stacks
proposals.motivation: The deployment of massive ensembles and model stacks, particularly in high-stakes domains like medicine, creates a critical interpretability crisis. While these systems can outperform individual models, their predictions become opaque 'black boxes within black boxes,' undermining trust and hindering debugging, especially for factual errors or safety concerns. Existing post-hoc explainability methods (e.g., SHAP, LIME) struggle with the computational and conceptual complexity of these composite systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a causal framework, treating ensemble components as interventions, can scalably attribute predictions to specific sub-models or training data segments, and that this attribution can directly enable targeted 'unlearning' of undesirable behaviors without full model retraining.
proposals.method: We will develop 'Ensemble Auditing,' building on causal mediation analysis and influence function approximations to trace prediction pathways through stacked LLMs/VLMs. The method will adapt and extend concepts from GenAudit (for error tracing) and OpenUnlearning benchmarks. We will treat each ensemble member or layer stack as a node in a causal graph, estimating its contribution to the final output. This attribution map will then guide a novel unlearning procedure that selectively adjusts parameters in identified critical nodes, evaluated rigorously using the standardized metrics from the OpenUnlearning framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a scalable, causal interpretability tool for massive ensembles, 2) a new paradigm where explanation and model editing (unlearning) are directly linked, and 3) a rigorous evaluation of these methods on biomedical and general-domain benchmarks, extending progress metrics from Medical Adaptation studies. It directly addresses the 'teaming' challenge of understanding composite AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While unlearning and interpretability are active fields, combining them through a causal lens specifically for model stacks is novel. This moves beyond explaining single models or unlearning from monolithic datasets, instead enabling surgical audit and correction in complex, team-based AI architectures.

proposals.title: Unlearning to Interpret: Causal Attribution and Factual Erasure for Edge-Deployed Tiny Language Models
proposals.motivation: The deployment of ultra-small language models (e.g., <10M parameters) on edge devices for medical triage, teaming, or personal assistants is accelerating, but their interpretability remains an open challenge. Pre-2026 interpretability tools (e.g., SHAP, attention visualization) are computationally prohibitive for these constrained environments and fail to provide actionable, causal insights into model decisions, which is critical for safety-critical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing interpretability through the lens of causal unlearning—specifically, measuring a model's sensitivity to the erasure of specific factual concepts—we can develop efficient, post-hoc attribution methods that are viable for tiny models and correlate with real-world error modes.
proposals.method: We will adapt and scale causal unlearning techniques from LLMs to tiny models. First, we will create a benchmark of edge-relevant tasks (e.g., medical Q&A, instruction following) and define a set of 'concept neurons' or embedding directions using linear probes. We will then develop a lightweight, gradient-based attribution metric that estimates the causal effect of ablating these concepts on model outputs, inspired by influence function approximations but designed for sub-100M parameter architectures. This method will be evaluated against established metrics on our new 'TinyAudit' benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) A novel, computationally efficient framework for interpreting tiny models via causal unlearning, 2) The 'TinyAudit' benchmark for evaluating interpretability methods in edge scenarios, and 3) Empirical evidence linking unlearning-based attributions to factual error correction in domains like medicine, advancing the goal of auditable, trustworthy edge AI.
proposals.novelty: While unlearning is studied for LLM safety and model editing, its application as a core, efficient interpretability primitive for resource-starved edge models is novel. This bridges Lipton's work on unlearning/auditing with the practical constraints of embedded deployment, a largely unexplored intersection.

proposals.title: Selective Obfuscation: Designing Language Models with Controllable Interpretability for Privacy-Preserving Teaming
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in sensitive domains like medicine, protecting proprietary model logic and private training data from adversarial reverse engineering is critical. Current interpretability methods aim for universal transparency, which can inadvertently expose models to extraction attacks, data reconstruction, and membership inference. There is a need for a principled approach to make models selectively interpretable—clear to authorized team members (e.g., clinicians) but opaque to unauthorized analysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating causal intervention techniques with unlearning objectives, we can train LLMs where specific reasoning pathways are interpretable only under a 'key' (e.g., a privileged prompt or context), while remaining provably obfuscated to standard interpretability probes, thereby decoupling authorized understanding from model privacy.
proposals.method: We will develop a training framework using a multi-task objective. First, we adapt causal mediation analysis to identify and isolate model components (e.g., attention heads) responsible for specific factual or logical inferences. Second, we apply targeted unlearning (inspired by gradient ascent or influence function methods) on these components to degrade the fidelity of standard interpretability tools (e.g., saliency maps, probing classifiers) unless a 'key' context is provided. Third, we will evaluate obfuscation using a unified benchmark extending OpenUnlearning, measuring the gap in explanation quality between authorized and unauthorized users.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for 'interpretability access control' in LLMs, 2) a benchmark for evaluating privacy against model extraction and inversion attacks, and 3) empirical results on medical QA tasks demonstrating the trade-off between teaming utility and privacy. It directly advances the goal of trustworthy human-AI teaming in high-stakes settings.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on model privacy (e.g., differential privacy) or interpretability, which are often at odds, this proposal uniquely treats selective obfuscation as an inverse problem to unlearning, using causal interventions to create conditionally interpretable models—a non-obvious synthesis of Lipton's core themes.

proposals.title: Dataset-Level Explanations for LLMs: A Causal Unlearning Framework for Global Model Audits
proposals.motivation: Current explainable AI (XAI) methods, like feature attribution, provide local explanations for individual predictions, which is insufficient for auditing model behavior at scale. In high-stakes domains like medicine, practitioners need to understand systematic biases, spurious correlations, and factual error patterns across entire datasets to ensure model safety and reliability. Scaling explanation from single predictions to dataset-level trends remains a critical, unsolved challenge for deploying trustworthy LLMs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically applying and measuring causal unlearning interventions across a dataset, we can generate global explanations that identify the core concepts, artifacts, and data subsets that most significantly drive a model's aggregate performance and failure modes.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend unlearning methods (e.g., gradient ascent, model editing) from a causal inference perspective. First, we will define a suite of dataset-level explanation targets (e.g., performance on a demographic slice, reliance on a spurious feature). For each target, we will formulate a causal intervention: unlearning a specific concept (the 'treatment') and measure its average treatment effect on model behavior across the dataset using robust metrics. The method will iteratively apply these interventions, building a global 'explanation graph' that quantifies the contribution of different data concepts to overall model behavior, validated through controlled synthetic datasets and real-world medical QA benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework and open-source toolkit for generating global, causal explanations of LLM behavior, moving beyond local saliency maps. It will provide practitioners with actionable insights for dataset curation, model debugging, and compliance auditing. The project will also advance unlearning research by framing it as a tool for model explanation, not just privacy or copyright.
proposals.novelty: While unlearning is typically studied for removing private data or copyrighted content, we repurpose it as a positive tool for large-scale model explanation. Our approach is novel in its causal, dataset-wide perspective, contrasting with prior work focused on local explanations or unlearning for single facts.

ideas.title: Leverage adversarial training for unlearning
ideas.description: Use adversarial objectives to penalize model for generating forget set content.
ideas.sourceTitle: TOFU: A Task of Fictitious Unlearning for LLMs

ideas.title: Combine with adversarial training techniques

ideas.title: Challenge separability of practical datasets
ideas.description: Re-examine the assumption that many practical datasets are separable for deep networks.
ideas.sourceTitle: What is the Effect of Importance Weighting in Deep Learning?

ideas.title: Scale down for few-shot NER learning
ideas.description: Adapt the framework for extreme low-data regimes, like few-shot or one-shot learning for new entity types.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deep Active Learning for Named Entity Recognition

ideas.title: Combine with semi-supervised or transfer learning
ideas.description: Integrate the active learning approach with semi-supervised or transfer learning methods for NER.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deep Active Learning for Named Entity Recognition

ideas.title: Scale BANs to large-scale datasets
ideas.description: Apply Born-Again training to ImageNet-scale datasets and very deep architectures.
ideas.sourceTitle: Born Again Neural Networks

ideas.title: Active learning without a seed set
ideas.description: Challenge the need for an initial labeled seed set to start the deep active learning process.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deep Active Learning for Named Entity Recognition

ideas.title: Use computer vision adversarial training for text
ideas.description: Adapt adversarial training methods from computer vision, like PGD, to the discrete text domain for misspellings.
ideas.sourceTitle: Combating Adversarial Misspellings with Robust Word Recognition

ideas.title: Invert the attack: use misspellings for data augmentation
ideas.description: Use adversarial misspelling generators not for attack but as a primary source for robust data augmentation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Combating Adversarial Misspellings with Robust Word Recognition

ideas.title: Distillation under Dual Unknowns: Weak Teacher Guidance for Test-Time Label Shift Adaptation
ideas.description: This research investigates knowledge distillation in a challenging scenario where both the source (training) and target (test) label distributions are unknown or corrupted. The core experiment inverts the traditional setup by using a weak model, trained on data with an uncertain source p(y), as the teacher for a strong student model. The objective is to determine if the distillation loss and the student's learning dynamics, when the teacher is weak and potentially mis-specified, can provide a reliable signal to jointly estimate the true source label noise and the test-time label shift. This explores a novel synergy where the 'inverted' distillation process becomes a mechanism for unsupervised domain adaptation under dual uncertainty about label distributions.

ideas.title: Scale explanation to entire datasets, not predictions
ideas.description: Develop methods to generate global explanations for a model's behavior across an entire dataset.
ideas.sourceTitle: The mythos of model interpretability

ideas.title: Apply ecological survey re-weighting for label density estimation
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from ecology, where scientists must estimate true wildlife population densities from surveys that are inherently biased due to varying detection probabilities across different habitats (the covariate shift). The analogous solution involves modeling detection functions and re-weighting observed counts to correct for this bias, a process formalized in methods like distance sampling. Adapted to the machine learning context for robust label density estimation, this involves treating the existing labeled dataset as a 'survey' of a larger, unlabeled target population. The core method is to estimate, for any point in the feature space, the probability that a sample would be included in the labeled set (its 'detectability'). Importance weights for the labeled data are then calculated as the inverse of this probability, calibrated for the target feature distribution. Applying these weights during density estimation corrects for the covariate shift between the labeled source and the target domain, yielding an unbiased estimate of the true label density, which is crucial for downstream tasks like prior shift correction or learning with noisy labels.

ideas.title: Minimalist BBSE with simple base classifiers: An analogical approach from structural engineering
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from structural engineering, where the load capacity of complex structures is predicted using aggregated simple component models. The analogous solution involves using many low-fidelity models combined with a calibration meta-model. Adapted to the ML context, we propose exploring BBSE's performance not with a single weak predictor like a random forest, but with a deliberately constructed, large ensemble of extremely simple and diverse base classifiers (e.g., decision stumps, shallow trees). The core research question is whether aggregating their outputs before applying BBSE—akin to using engineering safety factors—can yield more robust and accurate label shift estimation than using a single, slightly more complex but still weak, base model. This tests the limits of BBSE's 'scaling' dimension by pushing base classifier simplicity to an extreme while leveraging ensemble principles for stability.

ideas.title: Jointly Robust BBSE via Iteratively Reweighted Confusion Matrix and Moment Estimation
ideas.description: This method adversarially hardens BBSE by making both of its core components—the confusion matrix and the test prediction moment—robust to outliers and distribution shifts. It integrates an iterative reweighting procedure that assigns lower influence to source data points that are atypical for their predicted class (stabilizing C) and to test predictions that deviate severely from the central tendency of the model's output distribution (stabilizing mu_hat). This joint optimization converges to a prevalence estimate that is consistent under milder conditions than standard BBSE and far more resistant to corruptions in either the source or target data. The approach provides a unified framework for robustness, moving beyond simple post-hoc trimming of test predictions.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Generation BBSE via Regularized Confusion Propagation
ideas.description: This research investigates the application of Black-Box Shift Estimation (BBSE) within a sequence of iteratively trained models (e.g., a Born-Again Network sequence). The core challenge is that the confusion matrix, inverted in BBSE to estimate label shift, becomes increasingly ill-conditioned as models converge and their predictions co-vary across generations, leading to unstable estimates. The novel synergy is to use a tailored regularization scheme for matrix inversion at each generational step, informed by the spectral properties of the preceding generation's confusion matrix. This stabilizes the shift estimation, enabling the reliable tracking of how the label distribution evolves through the sequence, which in turn provides a new diagnostic for understanding and improving multi-generational self-training.

ideas.title: Calibrating to a Semantic Baseline: Addressing Label Concept Drift via Isotopic Tracers
ideas.description: This idea tackles concept drift within label shift by drawing an analogy from geochemistry and paleoclimatology. In those fields, proxy data for variables like temperature are calibrated against a stable isotopic standard to separate true climatic signals from changes in the proxy system itself. Adapted to machine learning, the solution is to maintain a small, immutable 'semantic baseline' dataset where labels are defined with high precision and consistency. The learning system would then model predictions on operational data as a calibrated deviation from this baseline. By explicitly learning this calibration function, the model can dynamically adjust for subtle, time-varying changes in what the label `y` semantically represents, isolating and correcting for pure label concept drift separate from feature-based or prior probability shifts.

ideas.title: Self-Consistent Knowledge Distillation via Bootstrapping
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that true labels or a standard cross-entropy loss with them are necessary for knowledge distillation. Drawing an analogy from ecological mark-recapture methods, which estimate population size using only internal, self-consistent sampling data, we propose a bootstrapping distillation framework. The teacher model 'marks' data with its soft predictions. The student is trained on one marked subset and then evaluated on a second, overlapping subset, with a loss function that maximizes the consistency between the student's predictions and the teacher's marks on that second set. This creates a closed-loop, self-supervised distillation process that relies solely on the teacher's outputs, adapting a solution from population dynamics to machine learning.

ideas.title: Assume adversary knows the defense model
ideas.description: Study robustness under a stronger threat model where the attacker has full knowledge of the word recognition defense.
ideas.sourceTitle: Combating Adversarial Misspellings with Robust Word Recognition

ideas.title: Auxiliary classifier is the best penalty proxy
ideas.description: Challenge using a linear classifier on local features as the sole measure of predictive power.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning Robust Global Representations by Penalizing Local Predictive Power

ideas.title: Balanced combined dataset is always optimal
ideas.description: Challenge that equal mixing of original and counterfactual data is the best training strategy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning the Difference that Makes a Difference with Counterfactually-Augmented Data

ideas.title: Dual: Use model to pre-label for humans
ideas.description: Invert the loop: use the model to suggest annotations, reducing human effort to verification and correction.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deep Active Learning for Named Entity Recognition

ideas.title: Investigate unlearning's impact on general capabilities
ideas.description: Study how unlearning forget data affects model performance on other tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: TOFU: A Task of Fictitious Unlearning for LLMs

ideas.title: Temporal Robustness in Overpowered Bayesian Neural Networks under Evolving Label Semantics
ideas.description: This research investigates Bayesian Neural Networks (BANs) in a novel, temporally dynamic setting where a high-capacity student network is trained by a lower-capacity teacher model, and the very definition of the output labels undergoes subtle, unannounced concept drift over time. The core problem is to disentangle whether predictive discrepancies arise from the student's superior representational power, the teacher's inherent limitations, or the teacher's increasingly outdated label semantics. The synergy enables the development of new metrics and learning algorithms that allow overpowered students to identify, model, and compensate for the teacher's latent conceptual drift, leading to more robust and temporally adaptive Bayesian learning systems.

ideas.title: Detection without model predictions or softmax
ideas.description: Create detection techniques that work for models without probabilistic outputs or softmax layers.
ideas.sourceTitle: Failing Loudly: An Empirical Study of Methods for Detecting Dataset Shift

ideas.title: Scale to self-supervised pre-training
ideas.description: Incorporate the local predictive penalty into self-supervised learning frameworks like contrastive pre-training.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning Robust Global Representations by Penalizing Local Predictive Power

ideas.title: Adversarially Robust Ensemble Distillation with a Devil's Advocate
ideas.description: This approach trains a student model using knowledge distillation from a teacher ensemble, but crucially incorporates an adversarial 'Devil's Advocate' network. The Advocate's role is to analyze the ensemble's predictions and generate targeted challenges, such as hard counterexamples or alternative label distributions, that expose potential groupthink or blind spots. The student is optimized with a dual objective: to match the ensemble's predictions on standard data while simultaneously resisting being fooled by the Advocate's critiques, thereby learning a more calibrated and robust representation. This results in a student model that benefits from ensemble wisdom without inheriting its collective biases or overconfidence.

ideas.title: Dual problem of weight-agnostic learning
ideas.description: Formally characterize the class of problems where deep nets are provably weight-agnostic.
ideas.sourceTitle: What is the Effect of Importance Weighting in Deep Learning?

ideas.title: Unlearning for smaller, specialized models
ideas.description: Apply unlearning techniques to compact models with limited capacity.
ideas.sourceTitle: TOFU: A Task of Fictitious Unlearning for LLMs

ideas.title: Apply BANs to broader range of tasks
ideas.description: Extend Born-Again Network experiments to more complex vision, NLP, and other domain tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Born Again Neural Networks

ideas.title: Use adversarial debiasing techniques from fairness
ideas.description: Incorporate adversarial loss functions to penalize reliance on spurious features during training.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning the Difference that Makes a Difference with Counterfactually-Augmented Data

ideas.title: Develop more effective unlearning algorithms
ideas.description: Create new methods that achieve true model behavior as if forget data was never learned.
ideas.sourceTitle: TOFU: A Task of Fictitious Unlearning for LLMs

ideas.title: Transfer domain adaptation theory tools
ideas.description: Apply theoretical tools from domain adaptation to analyze weighting in deep nets.
ideas.sourceTitle: What is the Effect of Importance Weighting in Deep Learning?

ideas.title: Adaptive Joint Shift Modeling with Causal-Aware Feedback
ideas.description: This research direction develops a unified framework for learning under realistic joint distribution shifts, where p(y) and p(x|y) both change. It integrates a method to detect and characterize the p(x|y) shift using label information as a diagnostic tool. The synergy emerges by using this diagnostic signal to inform and guide the adaptation process, creating a feedback loop. This allows the model to not just be robust to the joint shift, but to understand its causal structure—distinguishing between, for example, a shift in feature quality within a class versus a change in the causal mechanisms linking y to x—and adapt its strategy accordingly.

ideas.title: EM-Guided Multi-Teacher Knowledge Distillation Under Latent Domain Shift
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a framework where a student model is trained to distill knowledge from an ensemble of teachers, each potentially specialized to a different data domain or task. Crucially, when presented with unlabeled target data suffering from an unknown latent domain shift, an Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm is employed. The E-step estimates the latent domain assignment or shift parameters for the target data, while the M-step uses these estimates to adaptively weight the contributions from each teacher in the ensemble for distilling knowledge to the student. This creates a synergistic, iterative loop where the student is refined for the shifted domain while simultaneously clarifying the nature of the shift itself, a capability neither multi-teacher distillation nor standard EM for shift estimation achieves alone.

ideas.title: Scale SAP to very deep networks
ideas.description: Investigate the efficiency and effectiveness of SAP when applied to modern, very deep neural networks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Stochastic Activation Pruning for Robust Adversarial Defense

ideas.title: Architecture-Agnostic Black-Box Shift Estimation via Heterogeneous Knowledge Distillation
ideas.description: This research fuses knowledge distillation between heterogeneous architectures (like DenseNets and ResNets) with scalable Black-Box Shift Estimation (BBSE) to develop a novel, architecture-agnostic framework for label shift correction in extreme multi-class settings. The core idea is to use a lightweight, distilled 'surrogate' model, trained on the outputs of diverse, pre-trained source architectures, to generate the consistent confusion matrix needed for BBSE's inversion step. This bypasses the computational challenges of inverting large matrices for thousands of classes per individual model and decouples the shift estimator from any specific source model family, enabling a single estimator to work across multiple black-box deployments.

ideas.title: Generational Self-Adaptation to Evolving Label Concepts
ideas.description: This research proposes a multi-generation Bootstrapped Adaptive Network (BAN) sequence specifically designed to address environments with concept drift within label shift. Each successive student model is trained not only on the pseudo-labels from its immediate teacher but also on a distilled representation of the evolving label semantics observed across previous generations. The sequence maintains a dynamic, explicit model of the label concept trajectory, allowing the system to adapt to new definitions while preserving the ability to interpret or recover older labeling conventions. This enables robust, long-term learning in non-stationary domains where the very meaning of a class changes subtly over time.

ideas.title: Cross-Architectural Knowledge Distillation via a Universal Intermediate Representation
ideas.description: This idea expands the study of distillation between different model families (like DenseNets and ResNets) by drawing an analogy from comparative linguistics. In that field, translating between unrelated languages often employs a pivot language or an interlingua—an abstract intermediate representation that captures pure meaning. Adapting this solution, the research would investigate methods for cross-architectural knowledge distillation via a universal intermediate representation. Instead of direct transfer, knowledge from a source model (e.g., a DenseNet) would be projected into an architecture-agnostic 'interlingua,' such as semantic feature graphs or a small neutral proxy network. This intermediate representation would then be used to guide the training of the heterogeneous target model (e.g., a ResNet), potentially creating a more robust and generalizable framework for knowledge transfer between disparate neural network families.

ideas.title: Analyze model sensitivity to specific spurious features
ideas.description: Quantify how training on combined data reduces reliance on identified spurious features like genre.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning the Difference that Makes a Difference with Counterfactually-Augmented Data

ideas.title: Investigate sensitivity-robustness relationship further
ideas.description: Further explore the relationship between word recognition sensitivity and downstream classifier robustness.
ideas.sourceTitle: Combating Adversarial Misspellings with Robust Word Recognition

ideas.title: Assume streaming or non-stationary data
ideas.description: Challenge the static pool-based assumption and adapt active learning for a continuous data stream.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deep Active Learning for Named Entity Recognition

ideas.title: Integrate data programming with weak supervision
ideas.description: Use weak supervision to label potential counterfactuals at scale before human review.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning the Difference that Makes a Difference with Counterfactually-Augmented Data

ideas.title: Study the inverse weighting problem
ideas.description: Given a trained model, infer the implicit importance weights it learned.
ideas.sourceTitle: What is the Effect of Importance Weighting in Deep Learning?

ideas.title: Expand TOFU benchmark with more profiles
ideas.description: Increase dataset size and diversity of synthetic author profiles for robust evaluation.
ideas.sourceTitle: TOFU: A Task of Fictitious Unlearning for LLMs

ideas.title: Disentangling Concept and Label Shift via Invariant Anchor Concepts
ideas.description: This research addresses the unidentifiability of concurrent concept and label shift by positing the existence of stable 'anchor concepts'—data subgroups with an invariant relationship between features and labels. The method algorithmically identifies these anchors using weak supervision or metadata, then employs them to factor observed drift into a label prior shift component and a bounded concept drift component. This enables robust model adaptation and provides interpretable diagnostics about what aspects of the labeling semantics have actually changed over time.

ideas.title: Extend defense to multi-character adversarial attacks
ideas.description: Adapt the word recognition defense against attacks with multiple adversarial character changes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Combating Adversarial Misspellings with Robust Word Recognition

ideas.title: Human revisions may introduce new biases
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that human revisions perfectly isolate causal features without bias.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning the Difference that Makes a Difference with Counterfactually-Augmented Data

ideas.title: Analyze learned representations for interpretability
ideas.description: Perform detailed visualization and analysis of features discarded and retained by the penalty.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning Robust Global Representations by Penalizing Local Predictive Power

ideas.title: Transfer from causal inference for robustness
ideas.description: Incorporate causal inference frameworks to model and remove domain-specific local feature dependencies.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning Robust Global Representations by Penalizing Local Predictive Power

ideas.title: Develop more efficient RNN training algorithms
ideas.description: Create optimization techniques to reduce the computational cost and difficulty of training recurrent neural networks.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Critical Review of Recurrent Neural Networks for Sequence Learning

ideas.title: Distill without using the true labels
ideas.description: Rely solely on teacher outputs for training, removing the standard cross-entropy loss.
ideas.sourceTitle: Born Again Neural Networks

ideas.title: Minimalist BBSE with Overpowered Student Classifiers
ideas.description: This research investigates the intersection of Black-Box Shift Estimation (BBSE) and the Bounded-Agreement Noise (BAN) model under a deliberately asymmetric setup. It explores whether the distribution shift correction provided by minimalist BBSE, using simple random forest base classifiers, can be effectively leveraged by a subsequent student model whose capacity far exceeds that of the teacher (or base predictor). The core question is whether BBSE's primary value is in providing a robust, simplified correction signal that powerful students can exploit for superior generalization, or if the inherent limitations of the weak base predictors create an insurmountable bottleneck. This systematically tests the resilience and utility of BBSE in an under-explored regime where correction and learning capacities are radically decoupled.

ideas.title: Committee-Based Knowledge Distillation with Dynamic Weighting
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that knowledge distillation must rely on a single teacher or a uniformly averaged ensemble. Drawing an analogy from political science, where committees use weighted voting to form collective decisions, we propose a 'committee-based' distillation framework. Here, an ensemble of teachers acts as a committee. A meta-learner or gating network dynamically assigns a weight to each teacher's output for every training sample, based on the teacher's historical performance on similar data or inherent model confidence. The student model is then trained to mimic this dynamically weighted consensus, rather than a single teacher's output or a simple average. This adapts the democratic solution of representative weighting to the machine learning context, aiming for more robust and specialized knowledge transfer by prioritizing the most relevant teachers for each input.

ideas.title: Dual: Train networks to be SAP-robust
ideas.description: Train networks from scratch to perform well specifically under the SAP inference-time perturbation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Stochastic Activation Pruning for Robust Adversarial Defense

ideas.title: Adapting Concentration-of-Measure Techniques from Statistical Physics for Finite-Sample BBSE Bounds
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to provide tighter finite-sample error bounds for Black-Box Shift Estimation (BBSE) in high-dimensional feature spaces by drawing an analogy to techniques from statistical physics and high-dimensional probability. In those fields, analyzing systems with many random variables (like spin glasses) requires non-asymptotic concentration bounds that explicitly account for dimension and sample size, using tools like the entropy method and logarithmic Sobolev inequalities. The adapted idea is to apply this 'concentration-of-measure' framework to the BBSE estimator. Specifically, we would model the high-dimensional feature representations as structured random vectors, derive problem-specific concentration inequalities for the empirical confusion matrix, and consequently bound the error in the estimated label shift correction. This approach aims to move beyond generic PAC or asymptotic bounds by incorporating the geometry of the feature space and the properties of the black-box model, yielding tighter, more informative guarantees for practical high-dimensional, finite-sample scenarios.

ideas.title: Invert the causality in sequence processing
ideas.description: Create models where future outputs directly and primarily influence past or present states.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Critical Review of Recurrent Neural Networks for Sequence Learning

ideas.title: Anchored Multi-Generation Learning with Divergence-Aware Feedback
ideas.description: This system trains a sequence of models where each student becomes the next teacher, but crucially incorporates a parallel, static training arm using original ground-truth data to anchor each generation. A monitoring module continuously measures distributional divergence (e.g., using FID, feature statistics, or performance on a held-out validation set) between the model's synthetic outputs and the anchor data. Based on this signal, a feedback controller adjusts the training curriculum for the next student, dynamically rebalancing the mixture of synthetic teacher data and ground-truth data to correct for drift. This creates a robust, self-correcting sequence that mitigates collapse, preserves core competencies, and can potentially yield controlled, positive evolution.

ideas.title: Transfer HMM training algorithms to RNNs
ideas.description: Adapt training methods from Hidden Markov Models, like Baum-Welch, for recurrent neural network learning.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Critical Review of Recurrent Neural Networks for Sequence Learning

ideas.title: Apply invariant risk minimization (IRM) framework
ideas.description: Use IRM to learn predictors invariant across original and counterfactual data environments.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning the Difference that Makes a Difference with Counterfactually-Augmented Data

ideas.title: Black-Box Shift Estimation via Distillation-Aware Teacher Output Regularization
ideas.description: This research fuses the generalization of Black-Box Shift Estimation (BBSE) to multi-class covariate shift with the exploration of new distillation objectives. The core idea is to design distillation loss functions that do not merely transfer knowledge but actively diagnose the stability and transferability of the teacher's p(y|x) under shifting p(x). These novel losses, such as those measuring output consistency under simulated shifts or sensitivity to covariate perturbations, then inform a regularization term within the BBSE optimization. This results in a more principled and robust estimator that can down-weight or adapt teacher outputs which are likely to be unreliable under the target distribution, ultimately improving label shift correction where traditional BBSE may fail due to violations of its strict p(y|x) stability assumption.

ideas.title: Crowdsource exercise and dataset creation
ideas.description: Scale content creation by enabling the community to propose and vet new practice problems and datasets.
ideas.sourceTitle: Dive into Deep Learning

ideas.title: Robust, Semi-Parametric EM for Latent Shift with Validation and Bootstrapping
ideas.description: This approach strengthens the original EM-for-shift idea by directly attacking its sensitivity to initialization and model misspecification. It bootstraps the EM algorithm with a robust, data-driven initialization derived from contrastive or self-supervised learning on the unlabeled data to approximate the latent structure. During the iterative EM process, a stability validation loop monitors convergence using criteria like prediction consistency on a small trusted subset or cluster stability metrics, allowing for dynamic model adjustment. This results in a more reliable and robust estimator for latent variable shift that is less likely to fail due to poor starting points or incorrect parametric assumptions.

ideas.title: Consistency-Regularized Label-Free Knowledge Distillation
ideas.description: This method performs knowledge distillation using only teacher outputs, but critically augments the standard distillation loss with a strong consistency regularization objective. The student network is trained to produce similar output distributions for multiple augmented views of the same input, enforcing robustness and smoothing its decision boundaries. This self-supervised signal acts as a regularizer, preventing the student from overfitting to noise or systematic errors in the teacher's predictions. The combined objective enables stable, high-quality distillation even when teacher confidence is miscalibrated or when certain classes are poorly represented in its outputs.

ideas.title: Address concept drift within label shift
ideas.description: Tackle scenarios where the definition of labels y changes subtly over time.
ideas.sourceTitle: Detecting and Correcting for Label Shift with Black Box Predictors

ideas.title: Inverse Ensemble Diagnosis: Inferring Teacher Biases from Student Shift Under Multi-Teacher Distillation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes training a student model via distillation from an ensemble of multiple teachers with potentially diverse architectures or training data. The core innovation is to then treat the observed label shift between the student's predictions and a target dataset as an inverse problem. By analyzing how this shift relates to the contributions of different teachers, the method aims to infer the specific biases, blind spots, or miscalibrations inherent to each black-box teacher model. This enables a detailed audit of the teacher ensemble, revealing which models contribute to specific types of errors in the student, rather than just diagnosing a monolithic predictor.

ideas.title: Massively scale human revisions via crowdsourcing
ideas.description: Create very large counterfactual datasets via distributed crowdsourcing platforms.
ideas.sourceTitle: Learning the Difference that Makes a Difference with Counterfactually-Augmented Data

ideas.title: Post-hoc explanations can be faithful
ideas.description: Develop methods to ensure post-hoc explanations reliably reflect the original model's reasoning.
ideas.sourceTitle: The mythos of model interpretability

ideas.title: Study minimal data needed for effective unlearning
ideas.description: Find the smallest perturbation required to forget, preserving other knowledge.
ideas.sourceTitle: TOFU: A Task of Fictitious Unlearning for LLMs

ideas.title: Dual: shift-invariant model training

ideas.title: Invariance-Based Label Shift Detection via Multi-Environment Stability
ideas.description: This method detects label shift by leveraging the principle of invariance across multiple source environments. It first identifies a subset of features whose distribution, given the label, remains stable across all available source domains. The core detection mechanism then tests whether this conditional invariance holds on new target data; a statistically significant violation indicates a shift in the label distribution. This approach provides a robust and testable alternative to instrumental variable methods, as it relies on observable stability in historical data rather than untestable causal assumptions.

ideas.title: Regularized Confusion Matrix Estimation via Tikhonov Regularization
ideas.description: This idea addresses the core challenge of estimating true class prevalences from a black-box predictor's outputs when its confusion matrix is non-invertible—a common scenario with imbalanced classes, redundant predictions, or perfect predictor correlations. Drawing an analogy from geophysics, where ill-posed inverse problems are solved via Tikhonov regularization, we propose to adapt this stabilization technique. The solution involves adding a regularization term (like a scaled identity matrix) to the empirical confusion matrix before inversion, transforming the estimation into a regularized least-squares problem. This yields stable, approximate estimates of the true class distribution, even when the standard matrix inversion fails, providing a principled bias-variance trade-off for practical deployment with unreliable black-box predictors.

ideas.title: Label shift with unknown source distribution p(y)
ideas.description: Estimate test p(y) when the source label distribution is also unknown or noisy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Detecting and Correcting for Label Shift with Black Box Predictors

ideas.title: Use knowledge distillation for selective forgetting

ideas.title: Distributed Robust BBSE with Adaptive Regularization for Petabyte-Scale Data
ideas.description: This research develops a distributed, fault-tolerant system for Black-Box Shift Estimation (BBSE) designed to operate on petabyte-scale test datasets partitioned across thousands of nodes. The core innovation is the integration of a robust, regularized confusion matrix inversion strategy—such as Tikhonov regularization or truncated SVD—directly into the distributed computation and aggregation protocol. This allows the system to automatically handle partitions of data where the local confusion matrix is near-singular due to class imbalance or missing predictions, preventing numerical instability from cascading through the aggregation. The result is a scalable and statistically robust framework for estimating label distribution shifts in massive, real-world data deployments where data heterogeneity is the norm.

ideas.title: Online Black-Box Shift Estimation with a Dynamic Teacher for Streaming Adaptation
ideas.description: This research develops an online algorithm that fuses Black-Box Shift Estimation (BBSE) with a dynamically updated teacher model to monitor and adapt to label shift in a continuous data stream. Instead of using a fixed pretrained teacher, the teacher model is continuously refined or replaced using the streaming data, ensuring its predictions remain a relevant and well-calibrated source for the BBSE confusion matrix estimates. This closed-loop system allows the shift estimates to remain accurate even as the underlying data distribution evolves significantly over time, preventing degradation from teacher obsolescence. The result is a robust framework for real-time model monitoring and maintenance in dynamic environments like social media trends or adaptive sensor networks.

ideas.title: Apply error-correcting code techniques to spelling

ideas.title: Adapt Geophysical Signal Processing for Robust BBSE
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from geophysical signal processing. In seismology, robust statistical methods (like M-estimators with Huber norms) are essential for separating true seismic signals from strong, impulsive cultural noise. Analogously, in Black-Box Shift Estimation (BBSE), the stream of classifier predictions on the test set can be seen as a signal contaminated by 'outlier noise' from model miscalibration or adversarial examples on specific instances. The adapted solution is to integrate a robust estimation framework directly into the BBSE optimization. Instead of standard moment matching, we solve for the shift parameters using a robust loss function (e.g., Huber, Tukey's biweight) that reduces the contribution of test predictions with extreme, unreliable softmax outputs. This creates an outlier-resistant BBSE method that maintains accuracy in the presence of pathological test samples, much like geophysical methods maintain fidelity in noisy environments.

ideas.title: Apply SAP to other network architectures

ideas.title: Use adversarial training for distillation

ideas.title: Study minimal character edits for defense failure
